sections.subtopic: IUCN World Conservation Congress and Red List
sections.title: IUCN World Conservation Congress and Organizational Overview
sections.focus: Outcomes of the 2025 Congress in Abu Dhabi and the foundational details of the IUCN.
sections.rationale: The Congress resolutions and IUCN's governance structure are distinct, highly testable static and current affairs topics for UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The IUCN World Conservation Congress 2025 took place in Abu Dhabi, adopting a 20-year Strategic Vision 'Unite for Nature on the Path to 2045'. Key resolutions included a just phase-out of fossil fuels, recognizing wild animals as climate allies, and the first World Summit of Indigenous Peoples and Nature. IUCN, founded in 1948 and headquartered in Gland, Switzerland, is governed by the Members' Assembly and IUCN Council.
evidenceBullets: Congress 2025 held in Abu Dhabi, UAE.
Adopted 'Unite for Nature on the Path to 2045' 20-year Strategic Vision.
First-ever motion advocating for a just phase-out of fossil fuels adopted.
IUCN founded in 1948, located in Gland, Switzerland.
Members' Assembly is the highest governing body, held every 4 years.
Publishes IUCN Report on Protected Areas and IUCN World Heritage Outlook.

sections.subtopic: IUCN World Conservation Congress and Red List
sections.title: India's National Red List Roadmap and Vision 2025-2030
sections.focus: India's 5-year initiative to assess species extinction risks and publish National Red Data Books.
sections.rationale: A specific national initiative with clear targets, nodal agencies, and international alignments, making it a prime candidate for scheme/initiative-based questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: India unveiled its National Red List Roadmap and Vision 2025-2030 to establish a nationally coordinated red listing system. It is a 5-year initiative aiming to assess the extinction risk of approximately 11,000 species and publish National Red Data Books for flora and fauna by 2030. It is prepared by ZSI and BSI in collaboration with IUCN India and aligns with the CBD and KMGBF.
evidenceBullets: Aims to assess extinction risk of ~11,000 species over 5 years.
Goal to publish National Red Data Books for flora and fauna by 2030.
Nodal Agency: Union Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change.
Prepared by ZSI and BSI in collaboration with IUCN India and Centre for Species Survival.
Internationally aligned with CBD and Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF).

sections.subtopic: IUCN World Conservation Congress and Red List
sections.title: IUCN Key Instruments: Red List and Green Status of Species
sections.focus: Details of the IUCN Red List categories, recent species status updates, and the IUCN Green Status framework.
sections.rationale: Species status updates and the distinction between Red List (extinction risk) and Green Status (conservation success) are high-yield areas for UPSC Prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species, established in 1964, divides species into 9 categories from Not Evaluated to Extinct. Recent updates uplisted Indian birds like the Indian Courser and Indian Roller to Near Threatened, and the Long-billed Grasshopper-warbler to Endangered. The IUCN Green Status of Species, launched in 2021, complements the Red List by measuring conservation success and recovery across 8 categories, using a Green Score from 0 to 100%.
evidenceBullets: IUCN Red List divides species into 9 categories (NE, DD, LC, NT, VU, EN, CR, EW, EX).
Indian Courser, Indian Roller, and Rufous-tailed Lark uplisted to Near Threatened.
Long-billed Grasshopper-warbler uplisted to Endangered.
Green Sea Turtle improved from Endangered to Least Concern.
IUCN Green Status of Species launched in 2021 to measure conservation success, complementing the Red List.
Green Status includes 8 categories and a Green Score (0-100%) indicating proximity to full recovery.

sections.title: Kenton R. Miller Award
sections.focus: Details of the Kenton R. Miller Award and the IUCN World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA).
sections.rationale: This section covers a specific international award, its constituting body (WCPA), and a recent Indian recipient, making it a prime candidate for objective prelims questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Kaziranga Director becomes first Indian to receive IUCN’s Kenton R. Miller Award for innovation in national parks. Constituted by: World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA), one of six technical commissions of IUCN. Established in 2006 for Innovation in National Parks and Protected Area Sustainability. Honors individuals who show innovation and leadership in conserving national parks and protected areas. Presented every two years by the IUCN-WCPA.
evidenceBullets: Kaziranga Director is the first Indian to receive the Kenton R. Miller Award.
The award is constituted by the World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA), one of IUCN's six technical commissions.
Established in 2006, it honors innovation and leadership in conserving national parks and protected areas.
The award is presented every two years by the IUCN-WCPA.

sections.title: IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC)
sections.focus: Establishment, role, and recent Indian leadership appointment to the IUCN SSC.
sections.rationale: The SSC is a major IUCN body, and the election of an Indian conservationist as its Chair is highly relevant for UPSC current affairs.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Established in 1949, it is a science-based network of more than 10,500 volunteer experts. India’s renowned wildlife conservationist, Vivek Menon, has been elected as the new Chair of the IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC) for the 2025-2029. Role: To provide information to IUCN on biodiversity conservation, the inherent value of species, their role in ecosystem health and functioning, etc.
evidenceBullets: The IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC) was established in 1949 as a science-based network of volunteer experts.
Indian wildlife conservationist Vivek Menon was elected as the new Chair of the IUCN SSC for 2025-2029.
The SSC provides information to IUCN on biodiversity conservation, species value, and ecosystem health.

sections.title: IUCN World Heritage Outlook 4 Report
sections.focus: Findings of the IUCN World Heritage Outlook 4 report specifically regarding Indian natural heritage sites.
sections.rationale: Reports and indices are high-yield for UPSC. The specific categorization of Indian National Parks and the Western Ghats provides excellent factual anchors for statement-based questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: It categorises Western Ghats and 2 national parks (NP) in India – Assam’s Manas NP and West Bengal’s Sundarbans NP as being of “significant concern”. Other findings: Only Khangchendzonga National Park remained ‘Good’ in Conservation Outlook Rating. Sundarbans National Park: Witnessed Deterioration in its conservation outlook status from ‘Good with some Concerns’ to ‘Significant Concerns’ from 2020-2025.
evidenceBullets: The report categorizes the Western Ghats, Manas NP (Assam), and Sundarbans NP (West Bengal) as being of 'significant concern'.
Khangchendzonga National Park is the only Indian site that remained 'Good' in the Conservation Outlook Rating.
Sundarbans National Park's conservation outlook deteriorated from 'Good with some Concerns' to 'Significant Concerns' between 2020 and 2025.

sections.title: Green Status Assessment for the Lion
sections.focus: IUCN Green Status for the Lion, Asiatic Lion conservation profile, and morphological differences between Asiatic and African Lions.
sections.rationale: Species profiles, especially for flagship species like the Asiatic Lion, and comparative biology (Asiatic vs. African) are classic UPSC question formats.
sections.sectionExcerpt: IUCN releases First Green Status Assessment for the Lion (Panthera leo). Lion’s Green Status: ‘Largely depleted’. Extinct Regions: North Africa and Southwest Asia. Asiatic Lion Profile: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix I; IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Difference between Asiatic and African Lions: Asiatic Lion has sparser and shorter mane (ears visible), a distinctive longitudinal skin fold along the belly, and is slightly smaller than African lions.
evidenceBullets: The First Green Status Assessment for the Lion categorizes its status as 'Largely depleted'.
The Asiatic Lion is listed as Vulnerable (VU) on the IUCN Red List, Schedule I of WPA 1972, and Appendix I of CITES.
Asiatic lions have a distinctive longitudinal skin fold along the belly, which is usually absent in African lions.
Male Asiatic lions have sparser and shorter manes compared to African lions, making their ears almost always visible.
Asiatic lions are generally smaller in body size than African lions.

sections.title: Asiatic Lions Conservation and Barda Wildlife Sanctuary
sections.focus: Conservation initiatives for Asiatic Lions, specifically Project Lion and the emergence of Barda Wildlife Sanctuary as a secondary habitat.
sections.rationale: Covers Project Lion and the geographical and ecological details of Barda WLS, which are highly testable in UPSC prelims for mapping and species conservation.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Project Lion was announced in 2020 to expand lion habitats beyond Gir to areas like Girnar, Pania, Mitiyala, and Barda Wildlife Sanctuary (Gujarat). Under the WPA 2022 amendment, CITES-listed species are now under Schedule IV. Barda's key flora includes Gorad, Babul, and Bamboo, and fauna includes Sambar, chital, and chinkara.
evidenceBullets: Project Lion (2020) aims to expand habitats beyond Gir to Girnar, Pania, Mitiyala, and Barda.
Barda Wildlife Sanctuary (Porbandar and Devbhumi Dwarka, Gujarat) is emerging as a second home for Asiatic Lions.
WPA 2022 amendment places CITES-listed species under Schedule IV of the Act.

sections.title: UNESCO's World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) & Cold Desert BR
sections.focus: The MAB programme, WNBR designation criteria, India's Biosphere Reserves, and specific ecological and cultural details of the Cold Desert BR.
sections.rationale: Biosphere reserves and UNESCO MAB are recurring UPSC themes. The specific inclusion of Cold Desert BR and its associated protected areas, biome, and culture makes it a high-yield area.
sections.sectionExcerpt: UNESCO designated India's Cold Desert Biosphere Reserve to the WNBR. WNBR is managed by the MAB Programme (1971). Designation is by the UNESCO Director-General via the MAB ICC using the Seville Strategy. India has 13 BRs in the WNBR. The Cold Desert BR in Himachal Pradesh covers Pin Valley NP, Chandratal, and Sarchu & Kibber WLS. It features a cold desert biome (rain-shadow zone), distinct Buddhist culture (Gompas, Chorten, Mani Walls), and fauna like the Snow Leopard and Himalayan Ibex.
evidenceBullets: WNBR (launched 1976) is managed by UNESCO's Man and the Biosphere (MAB) Programme (launched 1971).
Designations are made by the Director-General of UNESCO following MAB ICC decisions, using the Seville Strategy.
India has 13 Biosphere Reserves recognized under WNBR.
Cold Desert BR (Himachal Pradesh) covers Pin Valley NP, Chandratal, and Sarchu & Kibber WLS.
Cold Desert BR lies in a rain-shadow zone with distinct Buddhist culture (Gompas, Chorten) and fauna like Snow Leopard and Himalayan Ibex.

sections.title: Wildlife Protection Act (WPA), 1972 and 2022 Amendments
sections.focus: The structural changes to WPA schedules, institutional bodies, and rules regarding hunting exceptions and translocation.
sections.rationale: The WPA is a foundational environmental law for UPSC. The 2022 amendments reducing schedules to 4 and specific rules on hunting exceptions (Sections 11/12) are critical for prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Kerala introduced amendments to ease procedures for killing wild animals threatening humans. The WPA 1972 (2022 Amendment) features 4 Schedules: Schedule I (highest protection), II (lesser protection), III (protected plants), and IV (CITES species). It establishes bodies like the National Board for Wildlife (chaired by PM) and WCCB. Sections 11 and 12 allow the Chief Wild Life Warden to permit hunting of Schedule I and II animals in specific cases (e.g., threat to human life, disease). Translocation requires Central Government permission for Schedule I animals.
evidenceBullets: WPA 2022 Amendment reduced schedules to 4: Sch I (highest protection), Sch II (lesser protection), Sch III (plants), Sch IV (CITES species).
Establishes the National Board for Wildlife, chaired by the Prime Minister.
Chief Wild Life Warden can permit hunting of Schedule I and II animals under Sections 11 and 12 if they are dangerous to human life or diseased beyond recovery.
Translocation of Schedule I animals requires prior permission from the Central Government.

sections.title: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
sections.focus: Issuance of new ABS regulations by the National Biodiversity Authority.
sections.rationale: Although brief, it introduces a newly updated regulatory framework by a statutory body (NBA) which is relevant for environmental governance tracking.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) has issued the new Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025.
evidenceBullets: The National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) issued the Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025.

sections.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
sections.title: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
sections.focus: Key highlights of the 2025 ABS regulations, including Digital Sequence Information (DSI), Prior Informed Consent (PIC), and benefit-sharing slabs.
sections.rationale: The 2025 regulation introduces new concepts like DSI and specific financial slabs for benefit sharing, which are highly testable in UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Notified by the National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) to replace 2014 Guidelines. Includes Digital Sequence Information (DSI) as a mode of benefit sharing. Requires Prior Informed Consent (PIC) for accessing biological resources. Slabs based on annual turnover range from nil (up to 5 Crore) to 0.6% (above 250 Crore). High-value resources require at least 5% of auction/sale proceeds.
evidenceBullets: Notified by NBA to replace 2014 Guidelines under BDA 2002.
Includes Digital Sequence Information (DSI) - digital representations of genetic resources like DNA/RNA.
Exempts access to cultivated medicinal plants notified under BDA 2002.
Benefit sharing slabs range from nil (up to 5 Cr turnover) to 0.6% (above 250 Cr turnover).
High-Value Biological Resources (e.g., red sanders) require minimum 5% of auction/sale proceeds.

sections.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
sections.title: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
sections.focus: The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and its associated protocols and guidelines governing Access and Benefit Sharing globally.
sections.rationale: International environmental conventions and their specific protocols (Nagoya, Cartagena) are staple UPSC questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) is a 1992 Rio Earth Summit treaty with 196 members, including India. Its goals are conservation, sustainable use, and fair benefit-sharing. Major protocols include Cartagena (Biosafety) and Nagoya (ABS). The Bonn Guidelines (2002) are voluntary for ABS, while the Nagoya Protocol (2010) is legally binding.
evidenceBullets: CBD adopted at 1992 Rio Earth Summit; India is a member.
Three main goals of CBD: Conservation, Sustainable use, Fair and Equitable Benefit-Sharing.
Cartagena Protocol (2003) deals with Biosafety.
Nagoya Protocol (2010) is a legally-binding agreement for fair and equitable sharing of benefits from genetic resources.
Bonn Guidelines (2002) are voluntary guidelines for ABS.
Multilateral Mechanism for DSI use adopted at CBD COP16 (2024).

sections.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
sections.title: India's Legal Framework on ABS (Biological Diversity Act, 2002)
sections.focus: Statutory framework under the BD Act 2002, its three-tier implementation system, and notable case studies.
sections.rationale: The three-tier institutional structure (NBA, SBB, BMC) and the scope of the BD Act are critical for polity and environment governance questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 covers all biological resources within India, broader than the Nagoya Protocol's scope. It features a three-tier statutory system: National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) with civil court powers, State Biodiversity Boards (SBBs), and Biodiversity Management Committees (BMCs) at the local level. A key case study is the Kani tribal community's benefit sharing for the Jeevani drug from the Arogyapacha plant.
evidenceBullets: BD Act 2002 covers all biological resources in India, which is broader than the Nagoya Protocol.
Implemented via a three-tier system: NBA (Central), SBBs (State), and BMCs (Local).
National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) possesses the powers of a civil court.
Kani tribal community of Kerala shares benefits from the Jeevani drug developed from the Arogyapacha plant.

sections.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
sections.title: CSS-Integrated Development of Wildlife Habitats Scheme (CSS-IDWH)
sections.focus: Scheme details, nodal ministry, funding structure, and the Species Recovery Programme.
sections.rationale: Centrally Sponsored Schemes related to environment, especially those funding protected areas and species recovery, are frequently tested.
sections.sectionExcerpt: CSS-IDWH is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme under MoEFCC for wildlife protection. It provides financial assistance to State/UTs for Protected Areas, wildlife outside protected areas, and recovery programmes for critically endangered species. 22 species are included, with Gharial and Sloth Bear recently recommended. Key components include Development of Wildlife Habitats, Project Tiger, and Project Elephant.
evidenceBullets: Centrally Sponsored Scheme under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC).
Funds Protected Areas, wildlife outside PAs, and critically endangered species recovery programmes.
22 species currently included, such as Snow Leopard, Asiatic Lion, and Gangetic River Dolphin.
Gharial and Sloth Bear recommended for inclusion in the Species Recovery Programme.
Key components include Project Tiger and Project Elephant.

sections.subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
sections.title: Species Profile: Sloth Bear
sections.focus: Characteristics, habitat, threats, and conservation status of the Sloth Bear.
sections.rationale: Species profiles with specific IUCN/WPA statuses and unique behavioral traits (e.g., non-hibernating, myrmecophagous) are standard UPSC prelims material.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Sloth Bear specializes in eating termites and ants and does not hibernate. It is solitary, nocturnal, and native to India, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. Its conservation status is Schedule I under WPA 1972, Appendix I under CITES, and Vulnerable (VU) under IUCN. Daroji Sloth Bear Sanctuary in Karnataka is Asia's first dedicated sloth bear sanctuary.
evidenceBullets: Specializes in eating termites and ants and does not hibernate unlike other bears.
Native to forests and grasslands of India, Nepal, and Sri Lanka.
Conservation Status: WPA 1972 Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, IUCN Vulnerable (VU).
Daroji Sloth Bear Sanctuary in Karnataka is the first dedicated sloth bear sanctuary in Asia.
Project Sloth Bear initiated in 2025.

sections.title: Project Cheetah and Associated Habitats
sections.focus: Cheetah reintroduction details, comparison of Asiatic and African cheetahs, and Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary profile.
sections.rationale: Covers a major flagship species reintroduction program, critical IUCN/CITES statuses of two cheetah subspecies, and geographical/ecological details of Gandhi Sagar WLS.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Project Cheetah, launched in 2022 under the umbrella of Project Tiger and Elephant, aims to reintroduce cheetahs to India. Implemented by NTCA with WII's technical assistance, it currently supports cheetahs in Kuno National Park. The Asiatic Cheetah is Critically Endangered (IUCN) and found only in Iran, while the African Cheetah is Vulnerable. Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary in Madhya Pradesh (Khathiar-Gir dry deciduous forest, River Chambal) is also highlighted, featuring flora like Salai and fauna like Dholes and Mugger crocodiles, alongside Chaturbhuj Nala rock shelters.
evidenceBullets: Project Cheetah operates under Project Tiger and Elephant; implemented by NTCA with WII technical assistance.
Asiatic Cheetah: IUCN Critically Endangered, CITES Appendix I, WPA Schedule I; found only in Iran; extinct in India since 1952.
African Cheetah: IUCN Vulnerable, CITES Appendix I; Namibia holds the largest population.
Cheetahs are the world's fastest mammals and do not roar unlike other big cats.
Gandhi Sagar WLS: Located in MP (Nimar region), features Khathiar-Gir dry deciduous forest, River Chambal, and Chaturbhuj Nala rock shelters.

sections.title: Tiger Reserves and Supreme Court Directives
sections.focus: Supreme Court guidelines on tiger reserve management, core vs. buffer zone regulations, and statutory provisions for Tiger Reserves under WPA 1972.
sections.rationale: Highly examinable polity and environment intersection detailing SC bans on core area safaris, NTCA's statutory role, and Human-Wildlife Conflict (HWC) compensation rules.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Supreme Court issued directions to protect tiger reserves, banning tiger safaris and night tourism in core/critical habitats. Safaris are permitted only on non-forest or degraded forest land in buffer zones. Commercial mining and major hydro projects are prohibited in buffer areas. Human-Wildlife Conflicts (HWCs) are to be treated as Natural Disasters with a uniform 10 lakh ex-gratia for human deaths. Tiger Reserves are declared under WPA 1972 by State Governments on NTCA's advice, and boundary alterations require NTCA recommendation and National Board for Wild Life (NBWL) approval.
evidenceBullets: SC banned tiger safaris and night tourism in core areas/designated tiger corridors.
Safaris are permitted only on non-forest or degraded forest land in buffer zones.
Human-Wildlife Conflicts (HWCs) to be granted Natural Disaster status with 10 lakh ex-gratia for human deaths.
Tiger Reserves consist of inviolable Core Areas and regulated Buffer Areas (sustainable use zones).
Reserves are notified by State Governments under WPA 1972 on the advice of NTCA.
Boundary alterations of Tiger Reserves require NTCA recommendation and NBWL approval.

sections.title: International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA)
sections.focus: India's role as the host for the IBCA Headquarters.
sections.rationale: A brief but significant international environmental initiative hosted by India.
sections.sectionExcerpt: India and the International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA) signed a Headquarters Agreement to host the IBCA Headquarters and Secretariat in India, facilitating the efficient discharge of its official functions.
evidenceBullets: India signed an agreement to host the IBCA Headquarters and Secretariat.

sections.subtopic: International Big Cat Alliance and Small Cats
sections.title: International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA)
sections.focus: Genesis, structure, membership, and conservation targets of the IBCA.
sections.rationale: IBCA is a major recent international initiative led by India, highly relevant for UPSC Prelims regarding its target species, membership, and structure.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Launched by India in 2023 to commemorate 50 years of Project Tiger, the IBCA is a treaty-based inter-governmental organization. It aims to conserve 7 big cats: Tiger, Lion, Leopard, Snow Leopard, Cheetah, Jaguar, and Puma. All except Puma and Jaguar are found in India and protected under Schedule I of WPA, 1972. It has 95 member countries, with 16 founding members, and its apex decision-making body is the IBCA Assembly, which meets every 2 years.
evidenceBullets: Launched by India in 2023 to mark 50 years of Project Tiger.
Aims to conserve 7 big cats: Tiger, Lion, Leopard, Snow Leopard, Cheetah, Jaguar, and Puma.
Puma and Jaguar are not found in India; the rest are protected under WPA 1972 Schedule I.
Treaty-based inter-governmental organization with 95 member countries and 16 founding members.
First IBCA assembly hosted in New Delhi in 2025.

sections.subtopic: International Big Cat Alliance and Small Cats
sections.title: Status of Small Cats in India
sections.focus: Findings of the WII-NTCA report, characteristics of small cats, and specific profiles of major small cats in India.
sections.rationale: Species-specific questions (IUCN status, habitat, characteristics) are a staple in UPSC Prelims. The distinction between big and small cats is also a prime conceptual question.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A report by WII and NTCA assessed small cats in India. India has 10 small cat species, with the Jungle Cat being the most widespread and the Rusty-Spotted Cat being the world's smallest. Unlike big cats that have a flexible hyoid bone to roar, small cats have a rigid hyoid bone allowing them to purr. Key species include the Asiatic golden cat (NT, endemic to Indo-Malayan eco-realm), Caracal (LC, arid regions of RJ, GJ, MP), Fishing cat (VU, wetlands), and Eurasian lynx (LC, Ladakh). All are WPA Schedule I.
evidenceBullets: Report released by Wildlife Institute of India (WII) and National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA).
India hosts 10 small cat species; Jungle Cat is most widespread, Rusty-Spotted Cat is the world's smallest.
Small cats have a rigid hyoid bone (purr continuously), whereas big cats have a flexible hyoid bone (roar).
Caracal (Siya Gosh) is found only in Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Madhya Pradesh (IUCN: Least Concern).
Fishing cat (IUCN: Vulnerable) has webbed hind feet and is found in wetland environments like Sundarbans.

sections.subtopic: International Big Cat Alliance and Small Cats
sections.title: Status of Elephants in India
sections.focus: Findings of the SAIEE 2021-25 DNA-based elephant count and population distribution.
sections.rationale: Elephant population statistics, census methods, and regional distribution are highly testable facts for environment and ecology.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Synchronous All India Population Estimation of Elephants (SAIEE) 2021-25 is India's first DNA-based count conducted by WII under Project Elephant. India harbors about 60% of the global Asian Elephant wild population (>22,000). The Western Ghats hosts the largest population, followed by North Eastern Hills. State-wise, Karnataka supports the highest population, followed by Assam, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala.
evidenceBullets: SAIEE 2021-25 is India's first DNA-based elephant count, conducted by WII.
India holds approximately 60% of the global wild Asian Elephant population.
Western Ghats region hosts the largest population of wild elephants in India.
Karnataka has the highest state-wise elephant population, followed by Assam, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala.
A small feral population of elephants exists in the Andaman Islands.

sections.title: Asian Elephants and Comparison with African Elephants
sections.focus: Characteristics, habitat, conservation status of Asian Elephants, and morphological differences from African Elephants.
sections.rationale: This section covers all the provided information on elephants, forming a distinct biological and conservation topic separate from crocodiles.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Asian Elephants (Elephas maximus) are the largest land mammals in Asia, featuring a matriarchal social structure and a 22-month gestation period. They are classified as Endangered by IUCN, Schedule I under WPA, and Appendix I under CITES. Project Elephant was launched in 1992. Key differences from African elephants include smaller size, twin-domed head, smaller rounded ears, humped back, and tusks typically only visible in some males.
evidenceBullets: Asian Elephants have a matriarchal social structure and the longest gestation period in mammals at 22 months.
Conservation Status of Asian Elephants: IUCN Endangered, WPA 1972 Schedule I, and CITES Appendix I.
Project Elephant was launched in 1992, and there are currently 33 Elephant Reserves across 14 major states.
Asian elephants have a twin-domed head with a central indent and a humped/convex back, whereas African elephants have a rounded/flat single dome and a concave/dipped back.
Only some male Asian elephants have visible tusks, while both male and female African elephants have larger tusks.

sections.title: Crocodile Conservation Project and Native Species of India
sections.focus: 50 years of the Crocodile Conservation Project, recovery statistics, and the characteristics, habitats, and conservation statuses of Saltwater, Mugger, and Gharial crocodiles.
sections.rationale: Groups the 50th anniversary of the conservation project with the detailed breakdown of the three native crocodilian species, as their habitats, statuses, and recovery efforts are deeply interconnected.
sections.sectionExcerpt: India celebrated 50 years of its Crocodile Conservation Project, launched in 1975. Odisha is the only state with conservation centres for all three native species: Saltwater (Dangamal), Mugger (Ramatirtha), and Gharial (Tikarpada). India holds 80% of the global wild gharial population. The three native species are the Saltwater Crocodile (Least Concern), Mugger (Vulnerable), and Gharial (Critically Endangered). Gharials are expert fish catchers with a unique 'Ghara' on males' snouts, while Muggers use tools and dig burrows.
evidenceBullets: The Crocodile Conservation Project was launched in April 1975 based on H.R. Bustard's recommendations.
Odisha is the only Indian state with conservation centres for all 3 native species: Tikarpada for gharials, Dangamal for saltwater crocodiles, and Ramatirtha for muggers.
Saltwater Crocodile (Estuarine): IUCN Least Concern; found in brackish water and mangroves like Bhitarkanika, Andaman & Nicobar, and Sundarbans.
Mugger (Marsh Crocodile): IUCN Vulnerable; known to use tools like balance sticks to lure birds and is the only Indian species that digs burrows on land.
Gharial: IUCN Critically Endangered; adult males have a bulb-like 'Ghara' on their snout; found in deep, fast-flowing rivers like Chambal, Girwa, and Son.
India holds 80% of the global wild gharial population, supported by sanctuaries like the National Chambal Sanctuary.

sections.title: River Dolphins in India
sections.focus: Population status, species differentiation (Gangetic vs. Indus), biological characteristics, and conservation initiatives.
sections.rationale: This section provides a comprehensive overview of India's river dolphins, making it a distinct and highly examinable topic for UPSC, especially regarding species comparison, IUCN status, and specific conservation zones.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change and Wildlife Institute of India (WII) released 'Population Status of River Dolphins in India 2024'... India’s River dolphin population: 6327- Gangetic River dolphins (6324), Indus River Dolphins (only 3)... Ganges and Indus River Dolphins are two distinct species based on genetic and morphological data. Functionally blind, and rely on echolocation to navigate and hunt... Gangetic Dolphin Specifics: National Aquatic Animal of India... Indus River Dolphin Specifics: Punjab’s State Aquatic Animal... Conservation Measures: Project Dolphin (2020), Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Wildlife Sanctuary, Bihar, India’s first National Dolphin Research Centre (NDRC) at Patna.
evidenceBullets: India's river dolphin population is 6327, comprising 6324 Gangetic and only 3 Indus River Dolphins.
Both Gangetic and Indus River Dolphins are listed as Endangered (EN) on the IUCN Red List and Schedule I of WPA, 1972.
River dolphins are functionally blind and rely on echolocation to navigate and hunt.
The Gangetic Dolphin is the National Aquatic Animal of India, while the Indus River Dolphin is Punjab's State Aquatic Animal.
Key conservation initiatives include Project Dolphin, Vikramshila Gangetic Dolphin Wildlife Sanctuary in Bihar, and India's first National Dolphin Research Centre (NDRC) at Patna.

sections.title: Vultures in India and Conservation Efforts
sections.focus: WII assessment findings, IUCN statuses of the 9 vulture species, threats from NSAIDs, and conservation initiatives.
sections.rationale: Vultures are a classic UPSC environment topic. The detailed breakdown of 9 species and their IUCN statuses, along with specific conservation sites and threats, forms a distinct and critical study block.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Wildlife Institute of India (WII) releases its 1st Pan-India Assessment and Monitoring of Endangered Species (Vultures)... Focus: Four Critically Endangered species i.e., the White-rumped, Indian, Slender-billed, and Red-headed vultures at 216 sites across 17 states... Threats: Ingestion of toxic non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like diclofenac (used to treat livestock)... Conservation Initiatives: Vulture Conservation Breeding Centre (VCBC)- at Rani (Assam), Pinjore (Haryana)... White-rumped Vulture (Gyps bengalensis): Critically Endangered (CR)... Egyptian Vulture (Neophron percnopterus): Endangered (EN).
evidenceBullets: Four vulture species in India are Critically Endangered (CR): White-rumped, Indian, Slender-billed, and Red-headed vultures.
The Egyptian Vulture is Endangered (EN), while Bearded, Cinereous, and Himalayan Griffon are Near Threatened (NT).
A major threat to vultures is the ingestion of toxic non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like diclofenac used to treat livestock.
Vulture Conservation Breeding Centres (VCBC) are located at Rani (Assam) and Pinjore (Haryana).
Protected Areas hold 54% of all documented vulture nests according to the WII assessment.

sections.title: Draft Policy Paper on Camels and Camel Conservation
sections.focus: Population decline drivers, habitat, pastoralist communities, and biological adaptations of camels.
sections.rationale: The draft policy paper introduces a new focus on camel conservation, highlighting specific pastoralist communities and unique biological traits that are highly relevant for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Draft policy paper on Camels, prepared by Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry and Dairying and Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has proposed National Camel Sustainability Initiative (NCSI)... India’s camel population declined by more than 75% since the 1970s... Habitat: Dryland ecosystems (referred as “Ship of the Desert”) and are primarily reared (90%) in Rajasthan and Gujarat. Camel-rearing Pastoralist communities: Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar... Camels' humps store fats providing them energy when food is scarce and they store water in their blood cells, not their humps.
evidenceBullets: India's camel population has declined by over 75% since the 1970s.
90% of camels in India are reared in the dryland ecosystems of Rajasthan and Gujarat.
Key camel-rearing pastoralist communities include the Raika, Rabari, Fakirani Jat, and Manganiyar.
Camels store fat in their humps for energy, while water is stored in their blood cells, not their humps.
The National Camel Sustainability Initiative (NCSI) was proposed by the Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry and Dairying and the FAO.

sections.subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
sections.title: Key Camel Breeds in India
sections.focus: Characteristics, habitats, and conservation status of single-humped and double-humped camels in India.
sections.rationale: Distinct topic covering various regional camel breeds, especially the unique Kharai and Bactrian camels, which are highly testable for UPSC environment and geography sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Details single-humped dromedary camels like Bikaneri, Jaisalmeri, Mewari, Kachchhi, and the swimming Kharai camel of Gujarat (preserved by Rabari and Fakirani Jat tribes). Also covers the double-humped Bactrian camel found in Ladakh, known for its cold desert adaptations, and notes that the wild Bactrian camel is Critically Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Kharai camels can swim up to 3 km in seawater, survive by grazing on mangroves, and are preserved by Rabari and Fakirani Jat tribes.
Domestic Bactrian camels are found exclusively in the high-altitude cold desert of Ladakh in India.
Bactrian camels have two humps acting as energy (fat) reservoirs, not water.
Wild Bactrian Camel (Camelus ferus) is a distinct species listed as Critically Endangered by IUCN.

sections.subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
sections.title: Wild and Domestic Yak
sections.focus: Habitat, characteristics, and conservation status of Wild Yak (Bos mutus) and Domestic Yak (Bos grunniens).
sections.rationale: Clear species profile with specific IUCN/WPA statuses and geographical distribution, making it a standard environment question candidate.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Discusses the first-ever chromosome-level genome assembly of the Indian/Domestic Yak. Details the Wild Yak (Bos mutus), native to the Tibetan plateau, China, and India (regionally extinct in Bhutan), listed as Vulnerable (IUCN) and Schedule I (WPA). Contrasts with the Domestic Yak (Bos grunniens), known as the 'ship of the Himalayas'.
evidenceBullets: Wild Yak (Bos mutus) is native to China and India but is regionally extinct from Bhutan.
Wild Yak conservation status: IUCN Vulnerable (VU), WPA Schedule I, CMS Appendix I.
Domestic Yak (Bos grunniens) has larger lungs and heart than cattle to adapt to high altitudes.

sections.subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
sections.title: Tamil Nadu's Endangered Species Conservation
sections.focus: Conservation status and key traits of four specific species targeted by Tamil Nadu's conservation fund.
sections.rationale: Highly testable cluster of four distinct species with varying IUCN statuses, endemicity, and unique characteristics (e.g., 'Tiger of Kaveri').
sections.sectionExcerpt: Tamil Nadu sanctioned Rs 1 crore for conserving four species: Lion-tailed macaque (Endangered, endemic to Western Ghats), Madras Hedgehog (Least Concern, endemic to India, known as 'thorny rat'), Striped Hyaena (Near Threatened, scavenger declining in Mudumalai), and Hump-headed mahseer fish (Critically Endangered, endemic to India, called 'Tiger of Kaveri').
evidenceBullets: Lion-tailed macaque is Endangered (IUCN), Schedule I (WPA), and endemic to the Western Ghats.
Madras Hedgehog is Least Concern (IUCN), endemic to India, and is a nocturnal species known locally as the 'thorny rat'.
Striped Hyaena is Near Threatened (IUCN) and acts as a natural scavenger regulating diseases.
Hump-headed mahseer fish is Critically Endangered (IUCN) and is known as the 'Tiger of Kaveri'.

sections.subtopic: Camel Breeds and Endangered Species of Tamil Nadu
sections.title: Van (Sanrakshan Evam Samvardhan) Amendment Rules, 2025
sections.focus: Key provisions and changes introduced in the 2025 amendment to the forest conservation rules.
sections.rationale: Important legislative/regulatory update regarding Compensatory Afforestation and mining in forest areas, a frequent theme in UPSC polity and environment sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Highlights the MoEFCC's Van (Sanrakshan Evam Samvardhan) Amendment Rules, 2025. Key changes include allowing Compensatory Afforestation (CA) land to be transferred to the Forest Department or notified as a protected area. It mandates double the extent of CA on degraded forest land for mining critical/strategic minerals and allows state governments to grant initial 'working permission' for linear projects.
evidenceBullets: Compensatory Afforestation (CA) land can now be transferred to the Forest Department or notified as a protected area under the Indian Forest Act, 1927.
Mining of critical/strategic minerals requires CA on degraded forest land at a minimum of double the extent.
State governments are allowed to grant initial 'working permission' for linear projects like roadworks, railway tracks, and transmission lines.

sections.subtopic: Forest Conservation Act and Green India Mission
sections.title: Forest Clearance and Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act, 2023
sections.focus: Statutory procedures for forest clearance, redefined scope of 'forest', and exemptions under the 2023 Amendment Act, including the Godavarman judgment context.
sections.rationale: The 2023 Amendment is highly testable for UPSC Prelims due to controversies over the definition of forests and strategic exemptions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Procedure for Forest Clearance involves Stage I (In-Principle) and Stage II (Final). Approval Authority varies: up to 5 ha by Chief Conservator, above 5 ha or mining by MoEFCC. The Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act, 2023 defines forest as land notified under laws like Indian Forest Act 1927 or recorded in govt records on/after Oct 25, 1980. It exempts land changed to non-forest use before 1996. Supreme Court directed following the 1996 Godavarman dictionary meaning until identification is complete. Exemptions include land within 100 km of borders for national security, up to 10 ha for security infrastructure, and up to 5 ha in LWE areas.
evidenceBullets: Stage I clearance requires conditions like funding compensatory afforestation; Stage II is final clearance.
MoEFCC grants clearance for projects above 5 hectares or any mining proposals.
2023 Act exempts forest land within 100 kms of international borders or LAC for national security projects.
Exempts up to 5 hectares for defence or public utility projects in Left-Wing Extremism-affected areas.
Godavarman case (1996) expanded forest definition to include dictionary meaning and all government records irrespective of ownership.

sections.subtopic: Forest Conservation Act and Green India Mission
sections.title: National Mission for a Green India (GIM)
sections.focus: Objectives, funding, implementation mechanisms, and key strategies of the Green India Mission under NAPCC.
sections.rationale: Core climate change initiative linked to India's NDCs, carbon sink targets, and CAMPA funding, making it a standard environment topic.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Launched in 2014 under NAPCC. Objectives include increasing forest/tree cover over 24 million hectares and creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3.0 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent by 2030. Funding comes from Mission allocation and National CAMPA Fund. Implemented via a bottom-up model with Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMCs). Strategies include Micro-Ecosystem Approach for vulnerable zones, leveraging CSR, selling carbon credits via voluntary markets, and deploying a Green India Force of trained youth.
evidenceBullets: GIM is one of the 8 missions under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC).
Aims to create an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3.0 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent by 2030.
Funding is partially sourced from the National CAMPA Fund.
Implementation relies on a bottom-up model with Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMCs).
Strategies include selling carbon credits from forestry and agroforestry via voluntary markets.

sections.subtopic: Forest Conservation Act and Green India Mission
sections.title: Environmental Accounting on Forest 2025 and ISFR 2023
sections.focus: MoSPI's forest accounting report based on the UN SEEA framework and the latest forest cover statistics from ISFR 2023.
sections.rationale: ISFR data is a staple for UPSC Prelims geography and environment questions, and the new SEEA-based accounting report introduces a novel economic-environmental metric.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Environmental Accounting on Forest 2025 Report released by MoSPI is the first dedicated publication on forest accounting based on the UN System of Environmental Economic Accounts (SEEA) Framework. SEEA integrates economic and environmental data. ISFR 2023 shows total forest and tree cover increased to 25.17 percent of geographical area (21.76 percent Forest cover, 3.41 percent Tree cover). Maximum increase seen in Chhattisgarh, UP, Odisha, and Rajasthan. 8 states/UTs have forest cover above 75 percent, including Mizoram, Lakshadweep, A&N Islands, and Arunachal Pradesh.
evidenceBullets: Environmental Accounting on Forest 2025 Report was released by the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI).
The report is based on the UN System of Environmental Economic Accounts (SEEA) Framework developed in 2012.
ISFR 2023 reports total forest and tree cover at 25.17 percent of India's geographical area.
Maximum increase in forest cover was recorded in Chhattisgarh, Uttar Pradesh, Odisha, and Rajasthan.
8 states/UTs, including Mizoram and Lakshadweep, have forest cover above 75 percent of their area.

sections.subtopic: ISFR 2023 and UN High Seas Treaty
sections.title: ISFR Data and Forest Key Terms
sections.focus: State-wise forest cover rankings, mangrove cover changes, NDC carbon stock targets, and definitions of key forest-related terms.
sections.rationale: This section contains highly testable factual data regarding India's forest cover rankings and precise definitions of forest area versus forest cover, which are classic UPSC prelims traps.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Details the top states in forest cover by area (Madhya Pradesh, Arunachal Pradesh, Chhattisgarh) and percentage (Lakshadweep, Mizoram, A&N Islands). Notes a decrease in total mangrove cover to 4,992 sq km. Highlights India's NDC target progress in carbon stock. Defines key terms including Forest Area, Forest Cover (>= 1 hectare, >= 10% canopy), Tree Cover, Carbon Stock, and Protected Forests.
evidenceBullets: Top 3 states by forest area: Madhya Pradesh, Arunachal Pradesh, Chhattisgarh.
Top 3 states by forest percentage: Lakshadweep (91.33%), Mizoram, Andaman & Nicobar Islands.
Total Mangrove cover decreased by 7.43 sq km to 4,992 sq km (Gujarat decreased; AP and Maharashtra increased).
Forest Cover requires >= 1 hectare area and >= 10% tree canopy, irrespective of ownership.
Tree cover comprises patches < 1 hectare outside recorded forest areas.

sections.subtopic: ISFR 2023 and UN High Seas Treaty
sections.title: UN High Seas Treaty (BBNJ) and UNCLOS
sections.focus: Provisions of the BBNJ Agreement, its relationship with UNCLOS, and the legal boundaries of maritime zones.
sections.rationale: International treaties and maritime boundaries are high-yield for UPSC. The BBNJ agreement is a major recent development, and UNCLOS maritime zones (EEZ, Territorial Sea) are fundamental geography and IR concepts.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Covers the BBNJ Agreement (High Seas Treaty), a legally binding treaty under UNCLOS aimed at conserving marine biodiversity beyond national jurisdiction. It outlines key pillars like Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), Marine Genetic Resources (MGRs), and Environmental Impact Assessments. Also details UNCLOS maritime zones: Territorial Sea (12 nm), Contiguous Zone (24 nm), and EEZ (200 nm), along with dispute settlement mechanisms like ITLOS.
evidenceBullets: BBNJ is the third implementation agreement under UNCLOS (others: 1994 Part XI and 1995 Fish Stocks).
BBNJ requires 60 ratifications to enter into force; India has signed but not ratified.
Non-UNCLOS members can also join the BBNJ agreement.
UNCLOS Maritime Zones: Territorial Sea (up to 12 nm, full sovereignty), Contiguous Zone (up to 24 nm), EEZ (up to 200 nm).
Deep Seabed beyond national jurisdiction is the 'common heritage of mankind', managed by the International Seabed Authority (ISA).

sections.subtopic: ISFR 2023 and UN High Seas Treaty
sections.title: Ramsar Convention on Wetlands
sections.focus: Overview of the Ramsar Convention, its mandate, and recent CoP15 details.
sections.rationale: Ramsar is a staple environmental topic for UPSC. The recent CoP15 and the definition of 'wise use' are key examinable points.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Summarizes the Ramsar Convention on Wetlands following its 15th Conference of Parties (CoP15) in Zimbabwe. It defines the 'wise use' of wetlands, notes its genesis in 1971 (entered into force in 1975), and highlights its 172 member countries (India joined in 1982) with a secretariat in Gland, Switzerland.
evidenceBullets: Ramsar CoP15 was held in Victoria Falls, Zimbabwe with the theme 'Protecting Wetlands for Our Common Future'.
'Wise use' means maintaining ecological character using ecosystem-based approaches within sustainable development.
Adopted in 1971 in Ramsar, Iran; entered into force in 1975; India joined in 1982.
Secretariat is located in Gland, Switzerland.

sections.subtopic: Ramsar Convention and New Sites in India
sections.title: Mechanisms under Ramsar Convention
sections.focus: Ramsar List criteria, Montreux Record status in India, and Wetland City Accreditation details.
sections.rationale: This section forms a distinct conceptual block detailing the structural mechanisms of the Ramsar Convention, including the Montreux Record and WCA, which are highly testable static-current linkages.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Details the Ramsar List, noting the UK has the most sites, Brazil's Rio Negro is the largest, and India has 96 (highest in Asia). The Montreux Record lists wetlands facing ecological change due to human interference; in India, it includes Keoladeo National Park and Loktak Lake, while Chilika Lake was removed. Wetland City Accreditation (WCA) is a voluntary system approved at COP12 (2015) that is valid for 6 years.
evidenceBullets: India has 96 Ramsar sites, the highest number in Asia.
The Montreux Record currently includes India's Keoladeo National Park and Loktak Lake; Chilika Lake was removed after successful conservation.
Wetland City Accreditation (WCA) was approved at COP12 in 2015 and remains valid for 6 years before requiring renewal.

sections.subtopic: Ramsar Convention and New Sites in India
sections.title: Outcomes of Ramsar COP15 and India's Contributions
sections.focus: Key declarations, strategic plans, and India's specific resolutions and achievements at Ramsar COP15.
sections.rationale: Groups the international outcomes of COP15 with India's specific participation, resolutions, and achievements (like WCA for Indore and Udaipur), forming a coherent international relations and environment block.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Highlights COP15 outcomes including the Victoria Falls Declaration, 5th Strategic Plan 2025-2030, and the Indo-Burma Ramsar Regional Initiative (IBRRI) supported by IUCN's BRIDGE project. India introduced a resolution on sustainable lifestyles aligned with Mission LiFE, was elected to the Standing Committee (2025-2028), and saw Indore (Sirpur Lake) and Udaipur awarded Wetland City Accreditation.
evidenceBullets: The Victoria Falls Declaration acknowledges the ecological, social, and economic roles of wetlands.
IBRRI Member States include Cambodia, Lao PDR, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam, with the IUCN serving as the Secretariat.
India successfully introduced a resolution promoting sustainable lifestyles for the wise use of wetlands, in line with Mission LiFE.
Indore and Udaipur were awarded Wetland City Accreditation; Udaipur is surrounded by five major wetlands including Pichola and Fateh Sagar.

sections.subtopic: Ramsar Convention and New Sites in India
sections.title: New Ramsar Sites in India
sections.focus: Newly designated Ramsar sites in India, their geographical locations, and specific ecological or historical details.
sections.rationale: A purely geographical and ecological block focusing on the newly added Ramsar sites. The specific flora, fauna, and geographical markers make this highly testable for match-the-following questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Lists new Ramsar sites including Khichan, Siliserh, and Menar in Rajasthan, along with sites in Bihar and Chhattisgarh. Khichan Wetland hosts wintering demoiselle cranes. Menar Wetland Complex is a freshwater monsoon wetland hosting critically endangered vultures. Siliserh Lake is a human-made wetland in the Sariska tiger reserve buffer zone, created in 1845 on a tributary of River Ruparel.
evidenceBullets: Khichan Wetland in Rajasthan's Thar Desert hosts wintering flocks of migratory demoiselle cranes.
Menar Wetland Complex in Udaipur, Rajasthan, hosts critically endangered white-rumped and long-billed vultures.
Siliserh Lake in Rajasthan is a human-made wetland located in the buffer region of the Sariska tiger reserve.
Other newly listed sites include Udaipur Lake, Gogabeel Lake, and Gokul Reservoir in Bihar, and Kopra Jalashay in Chhattisgarh.

sections.subtopic: Indian Wetlands and Coral Reef Conservation
sections.title: Wetlands in News
sections.focus: Key facts, geographical locations, and ecological significance of recently highlighted wetlands in India.
sections.rationale: Groups all specific wetland locations, their geographical features (oxbow lakes, reservoirs), and associated biodiversity into a single testable block.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Covers Gokul Reservoir, Udaipur Lake, and Gogabeel Lake in Bihar (oxbow lakes, habitats for blackbuck and migratory birds, community reserves); Kopra Jalashay in Chhattisgarh; Vembanad lake in Kerala (largest brackish wetland, shrinking surface area); and Dal Lake in J&K (hosted Khelo India water games, fed by Arrah river).
evidenceBullets: Gokul Reservoir (Buxar, Bihar) is an oxbow lake and an important habitat for Blackbuck.
Gogabeel Lake (Katihar, Bihar) is the 1st community reserve of Bihar, formed from the flow of Mahananda, Kankhar, and Ganga rivers.
Vembanad Lake (Kerala) is the largest brackish, humid tropical wetland ecosystem in Kerala, and its surface area shrank by 27% between 1917 and 1990.
Dal Lake (J&K) hosted the first Khelo India water games, is fed by the Arrah river, and is divided into Gagribal, Lokut Dal, Bod Dal, and Nagin basins.

sections.subtopic: Indian Wetlands and Coral Reef Conservation
sections.title: Coral Reefs and Coral Bleaching
sections.focus: Coral biology, optimal growth conditions, bleaching mechanisms, conservation technologies, and global/Indian distribution.
sections.rationale: A comprehensive ecological and geographical overview of coral reefs, making it a high-yield topic for UPSC environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Details the symbiotic relationship between polyps and zooxanthellae, types of reefs (fringing, barrier, atoll), and optimal conditions (23-29°C, shallow clear water). Explains coral bleaching due to stress and highlights conservation technologies like Biorock and 3D-printed reefs. Maps distribution globally (Coral Triangle) and in India (Andaman & Nicobar, Lakshadweep, Gulf of Kachchh/Mannar).
evidenceBullets: Corals have a symbiotic relationship with zooxanthellae algae; unlike hard corals, soft corals lack calcium carbonate skeletons.
Optimal coral conditions include 23-29°C temperatures, clear shallow waters less than 50 meters deep, and stable salinity.
Biorock Technology uses electrical currents to stimulate coral formation via the deposition of calcium carbonate.
India's largest coral reef system is located in the Andaman and Nicobar Group of Islands.
The Coral Triangle includes the Philippines, Brunei, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Timor-Leste, Solomon Islands, and Australia.

sections.subtopic: Indian Wetlands and Coral Reef Conservation
sections.title: Mangrove Ecosystems and SAIME Initiative
sections.focus: The SAIME model for sustainable aquaculture, mangrove ecological characteristics, and mangrove distribution in India.
sections.rationale: Combines a specific conservation initiative (SAIME) with the foundational ecology and current status of mangroves in India, forming a complete study unit.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Discusses the Sustainable Aquaculture in Mangrove Ecosystems (SAIME) model recognized by FAO, aimed at protecting mangroves in South Asia (Sunderbans) through low-density shrimp farming. Details mangrove characteristics (pneumatophores, vivipary, carbon storage) and notes India's mangrove cover (4,991.68 sq. km) with West Bengal holding the largest share.
evidenceBullets: SAIME is a multi-stakeholder partnership promoting sustainable shrimp trade to protect mangrove ecosystems in South Asia, specifically operationalizing Integrated Mangrove Aquaculture (IMA) in the Sunderbans.
Mangroves feature unique root systems like pneumatophores and stilt roots, and exhibit vivipary where seeds germinate on maternal plants.
Mangroves act as Nature's Carbon Vault, storing 7.5 to 10 times more carbon per acre than tropical forests.
India recorded an 11.4% net increase in mangrove cover between 2001 and 2023.
West Bengal holds the largest share of India's mangroves (42.45%), followed by Gujarat (23.32%) and the Andaman & Nicobar Islands (12.39%).

sections.subtopic: Island Protection Zone and Blue Flag Beaches
sections.title: Island Protection Zone (IPZ) and Coastal Initiatives
sections.focus: Regulatory frameworks for island protection and regional coastal conservation efforts.
sections.rationale: IPZ is a crucial regulatory topic under the Environment Protection Act, highly relevant for UPSC prelims regarding coastal and island governance.
sections.sectionExcerpt: MoEFCC extended the validity of infrastructure projects approved under the 2011 Islands Protection Zone (IPZ) notification. IPZ is a regulatory framework to safeguard the ecological integrity of Andaman & Nicobar and Lakshadweep groups of islands. It is similar to the Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ)... Notified in 2011 under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. Regulation includes Island Coastal Regulation Zone (ICRZ) and Integrated Islands Management Plans (IIMPs). Regional initiatives for conservation: TN-SHORE, largely funded by the World Bank.
evidenceBullets: IPZ was notified in 2011 under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 to safeguard the ecological integrity of Andaman & Nicobar and Lakshadweep.
The IPZ regulation includes the Island Coastal Regulation Zone (ICRZ) and Integrated Islands Management Plans (IIMPs).
TN-SHORE is a regional initiative largely funded by the World Bank to strengthen Tamil Nadu's coastal economy and resilience.

sections.subtopic: Island Protection Zone and Blue Flag Beaches
sections.title: UNFF and UNOC3 Conferences
sections.focus: Key outcomes and objectives of the United Nations Forum on Forests and the Third UN Ocean Conference.
sections.rationale: International environmental conferences and their associated action plans (like the Nice Ocean Action Plan) are frequent targets for UPSC questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: India participated in the 20th session of the United Nations Forum on Forests (UNFF) held in New York... Established by UN Economic and Social Council of the United Nations (ECOSOC) Resolution 2000/35. The third United Nations Ocean Conference (UNOC3) held in Nice, France and co-hosted by France and Costa Rica concluded with the adoption of the Nice Ocean Action Plan. Priorities include implementing the High Seas Treaty (BBNJ Agreement), scaling up funding for SDG 14, and supporting the creation of the International Panel for Ocean Sustainability (IPOS).
evidenceBullets: UNFF was established by the UN Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC) in October 2000 to promote sustainable forest management.
UNOC3 was held in Nice, France, co-hosted by France and Costa Rica, and concluded with the adoption of the Nice Ocean Action Plan.
UNOC3 priorities include implementing the High Seas Treaty (BBNJ Agreement) and scaling funding for SDG 14 (Life below water).
The Nice Ocean Action Plan supports the creation of the International Panel for Ocean Sustainability (IPOS).

sections.subtopic: Island Protection Zone and Blue Flag Beaches
sections.title: International Blue Flag Certification
sections.focus: Criteria, awarding body, and the list of Blue Flag certified beaches in India.
sections.rationale: Blue Flag beaches are a recurring theme in UPSC exams; knowing the awarding body, criteria, and specific Indian locations is essential.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Five Maharashtra Beaches received the prestigious International Blue Flag Certification. A globally recognized eco-label awarded to beaches, marinas, and tourism boats. Total beaches in India: 18, highest in Maharashtra. Awarded by: Foundation for Environmental Education (FEE) of Denmark since 1985. Criteria: Awarded to beaches that meet 33 criteria related to cleanliness, beauty, and environmental sustainability. List includes Shivrajpur Beach (Gujarat), Radhanagar Beach (Andaman & Nicobar), Eden Beach (Puducherry), and Rushikonda Beach (Andhra Pradesh).
evidenceBullets: Blue Flag Certification is awarded by the Foundation for Environmental Education (FEE) of Denmark since 1985.
Certification requires meeting 33 rigorous criteria covering accessibility, education, environmental performance, and water quality.
India currently has 18 Blue Flag beaches, with the highest number located in Maharashtra.
Key Indian Blue Flag beaches include Shivrajpur (Gujarat), Radhanagar (A&N Islands), Eden (Puducherry), and Rushikonda (Andhra Pradesh).

sections.subtopic: Island Protection Zone and Blue Flag Beaches
sections.title: AIM4NatuRe Initiative
sections.focus: FAO's initiative for monitoring global ecosystem restoration efforts.
sections.rationale: New international environmental initiatives, especially those linked to the Global Biodiversity Framework, are highly testable.
sections.sectionExcerpt: AIM4NatuRe (Accelerating Innovative Monitoring for Nature Restoration) Launched by: The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) on Earth Day. Aim: To improve monitoring and reporting of global ecosystem restoration efforts. Initiative leverages cutting-edge technology... to restore at least 30% of degraded ecosystems by 2030, as outlined in Target 2 of the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF). Part of FAO's AIM4Forests Programme.
evidenceBullets: AIM4NatuRe was launched by the FAO to improve monitoring and reporting of global ecosystem restoration efforts.
It aligns with Target 2 of the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) to restore at least 30% of degraded ecosystems by 2030.
It is an expansion of FAO's AIM4Forests Programme to provide a holistic approach to nature restoration monitoring.

sections.subtopic: Island Protection Zone and Blue Flag Beaches
sections.title: Central Asian Mammals Initiative (CAMI)
sections.focus: CMS framework for protecting migratory mammals in Central Asia.
sections.rationale: Species conservation initiatives under CMS are standard UPSC environment topics.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Central Asian Mammals Initiative (CAMI) Central Asian countries have come together under CAMI to protect 17 shared species like Saiga, Bukhara Deer etc. CAMI was launched in 2014 at the 11th Meeting of the Conference of the Parties (COP11) to the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals (CMS).
evidenceBullets: CAMI aims to coordinate conservation efforts for 17 key migratory mammal species, including the Saiga and Bukhara Deer.
It was launched in 2014 under the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals (CMS) at COP11.

sections.subtopic: Island Protection Zone and Blue Flag Beaches
sections.title: Rare Mushroom Sightings in Telangana
sections.focus: Discovery and characteristics of Blue Pinkgill and Shuttlecock mushrooms in India.
sections.rationale: Recent biodiversity discoveries and species characteristics (like azulene pigment) are common in Prelims science and environment sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Rare Blue Pinkgill and Shuttlecock Mushrooms were recently spotted in Telangana forests. Blue Pinkgill Mushroom: Native to New Zealand. Derives color from azulene, pigment rare in fungi but common in some flowers and plant. Spotted in Kadamba Reserve Forest. Shuttlecock Mushroom: Recorded in Kawal Tiger Reserve. Marks the first confirmed sighting in Eastern Ghats, extending its known range beyond Western Ghats and western India.
evidenceBullets: The Blue Pinkgill Mushroom is native to New Zealand and derives its color from the rare fungal pigment azulene.
The Shuttlecock Mushroom was recorded in Kawal Tiger Reserve, marking its first confirmed sighting in the Eastern Ghats.

sections.title: AviList
sections.focus: Unified global checklist of bird species for conservation.
sections.rationale: A standalone initiative for bird conservation replacing older international lists.
sections.sectionExcerpt: AviList: The first-ever unified global checklist of bird species to aid effective bird conservation. Collaborative global effort that included representatives from BirdLife International, the Cornell Lab of Ornithology... It will replace the International Ornithological Committee (IOC) and Clements lists and will be updated annually.
evidenceBullets: AviList is the first-ever unified global checklist of bird species.
It replaces the International Ornithological Committee (IOC) and Clements lists.
It is a collaborative effort involving BirdLife International and the Cornell Lab of Ornithology.

sections.title: Conservation Reserves and Hesaraghatta Grassland
sections.focus: Legal status of Conservation Reserves under WPA 1972 and details of Hesaraghatta Grassland.
sections.rationale: Combines the statutory framework of Conservation Reserves with the specific geographical and ecological details of Hesaraghatta Grassland.
sections.sectionExcerpt: State Government has notified Hesaraghatta Grassland Conservation Reserve under Section 36A of the Wildlife Protection Act (WPA) 1972. Considered a protected area under WPA 1972 and can be declared by State Governments and Central Government... Typically act as buffer zones to or connectors and migration corridors... Hesaraghatta Grassland Location: Around Hesaraghatta Lake near Bengaluru in Karnataka and is the last remaining grassland habitat in Bengaluru region. Large catchment of the Arkavathy River.
evidenceBullets: Conservation Reserves are declared under Section 36A of the Wildlife Protection Act (WPA) 1972.
They act as buffer zones or migration corridors between established national parks, sanctuaries, and forests.
A Conservation Reserve Management Committee advises the Chief Wild Life Warden.
Hesaraghatta Grassland is the last remaining grassland habitat in the Bengaluru region.
It forms a large catchment for the Arkavathy River and Thippagondanahalli reservoir.

sections.title: Global Ocean Darkening
sections.focus: Causes, mechanisms, and impacts of reduced light penetration in global oceans.
sections.rationale: A specific environmental phenomenon with distinct causes (coastal vs open ocean) and ecological impacts.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A study found that 21% of the global ocean had become darker between 2003 and 2022, especially in the Arctic, Antarctic, and Gulf Stream region. It refers to a reduction in light penetration into the global oceans, shrinking the photic zone. Reasons: In Coastal Oceans: Combination of nutrient, organic material and sediment loading... In Open Oceans: warming of the surface oceans (leading to algal bloom)... Impact: Limits Ocean productivity.
evidenceBullets: 21% of the global ocean became darker between 2003 and 2022, notably in the Arctic, Antarctic, and Gulf Stream.
Ocean darkening shrinks the photic zone, which is the sunlit layer up to 200 meters deep where 90% of marine life thrives.
Coastal darkening is caused by nutrient, organic material, and sediment loading from agricultural runoff.
Open ocean darkening is driven by surface warming leading to algal blooms and climate-driven circulation changes.
Impacts include limited ocean productivity, declining fish stocks, and affected climate regulation.

sections.title: Environmental DNA (eDNA)
sections.focus: Definition, applications, and significance of eDNA in biodiversity conservation.
sections.rationale: A modern biomonitoring tool with specific applications and advantages over traditional methods.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Scientists are now focusing on modern tools like environmental DNA (eDNA) analysis for biodiversity conservation. It is the genetic material, like DNA from cells, tissues, fluids, and excrement, left by organisms in the environment (e.g., water, soil, Air, etc.). Application: Mapping species presence and distribution, tracking Ecosystems, etc. Significance: Non-invasive, highly sensitive, rapid and cost-effective.
evidenceBullets: eDNA is genetic material left by organisms in environments like water, soil, or air.
It is used for mapping species presence, distribution, and tracking ecosystems.
It is a non-invasive, highly sensitive, rapid, and cost-effective biomonitoring method compared to traditional methods.

sections.title: Blue Washing and Related Terms
sections.focus: Deceptive marketing tactics including blue washing, greenwashing, and pinkwashing.
sections.rationale: Groups related corporate deceptive marketing terminologies relevant to environmental and social ethics.
sections.sectionExcerpt: To counter 'greenwashing' allegations against it, Waste to energy (WTE) industry seems to have adapted 'blue washing'. About Blue Washing: It is a deceptive marketing tactic that overstates a company's commitment to responsible and sustainable social and ethical business practices. Greenwashing: Making false or misleading claims about a company's environmental efforts... Pinkwashing: Using LGBTQ+ rights as a marketing tool.
evidenceBullets: Blue washing overstates a company's commitment to responsible, sustainable, social, and ethical business practices.
Greenwashing involves making false or misleading claims about environmental efforts to appear eco-friendly.
Pinkwashing uses LGBTQ+ rights as a marketing tool while failing to improve actual conditions for LGBTQ+ employees.

sections.title: Functional De-extinction
sections.focus: Concept, techniques, and examples of reviving ecological roles of extinct species.
sections.rationale: A distinct biotechnology and conservation concept involving genetic engineering to mimic extinct species.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A US biotech company, Colossal Biosciences, claims to have genetically engineered three grey wolf pups to carry traits of the extinct dire wolf, calling it world's first successful functional de-extinction. It focuses on reviving ecological roles and traits of extinct species. Revived organisms are not exact replicas but genetically engineered to closely resemble extinct species... Techniques used: CRISPR gene editing, cloning, and genome mapping.
evidenceBullets: Functional de-extinction focuses on reviving the ecological roles and traits of extinct species rather than creating exact replicas.
Techniques include CRISPR gene editing, cloning, and genome mapping.
Colossal Biosciences engineered grey wolf pups to carry traits of the extinct dire wolf.
Other planned candidates for functional de-extinction include the wooly mammoth, moa, thylacine, and dodo.

sections.title: Ungulates
sections.focus: Characteristics, habitat, and ecological significance of hoofed mammals in India.
sections.rationale: Covers a specific animal group surveyed by WII and NTCA, detailing their ecological role.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Wildlife Institute of India and National Tiger Conservation Authority did India's first survey of ungulates (hoofed animals), warning of their decline in Odisha, Jharkhand, and Chhattisgarh. Characteristics: Primarily herbivores mammals including chital, antelopes, sambar, nilgai, etc.; Prey species for tigers. Significance: Aid forest/grassland regeneration through seed dispersal; nutrient cycling; soil health.
evidenceBullets: Ungulates are hoofed mammals, primarily herbivores, including chital, antelopes, sambar, and nilgai.
WII and NTCA conducted India's first survey of ungulates, noting declines in Odisha, Jharkhand, and Chhattisgarh.
They serve as key prey species for tigers.
They aid in forest and grassland regeneration through seed dispersal, nutrient cycling, and maintaining soil health.

sections.title: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES)
sections.focus: International agreement regulating wildlife trade, its appendices, and key initiatives.
sections.rationale: CITES is a highly examinable topic for UPSC, especially its legally binding nature, the distinction between its three appendices, and associated initiatives like MIKE and ICCWC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Voluntary international agreement between governments ensuring international trade in wild animals and plants does not threaten their survival. Legally binding on Parties, but does not take the place of national laws. 185 parties (including India - ratified in 1976). CITES Three Appendices: Based on degree of protection needed. Key Initiatives of CITES: MIKE Programme to monitor illegal killing of elephants; CITES Tree Species Programme (CTSP); International Consortium on Combating Wildlife Crime (ICCWC), 2010.
evidenceBullets: CITES is legally binding on parties but does not replace national laws.
India ratified CITES in 1976.
Appendix I covers species threatened with extinction; Appendix II covers species needing trade control; Appendix III covers species protected in at least one country asking for assistance.
Key initiatives include the MIKE Programme, CITES Tree Species Programme, and ICCWC.
CITES publishes the World Wildlife Trade Report.

sections.title: International Union for the Protection of New Varieties of Plants (UPOV)
sections.focus: Plant Variety Protection, Breeder's Rights, and India's non-member status.
sections.rationale: UPOV is crucial for agriculture and IP rights. India's unique stance of not joining UPOV and instead using the PPV&FR Act makes it a prime target for comparative questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: An intergovernmental organization to provide and promote an effective system of Plant Variety Protection (PVP). Grants Breeder's Rights (an IP right) to incentivize the development of new, improved plant varieties. UPOV Convention 1991: It strengthens breeders' rights and makes the farmer's privilege (to save seeds) optional for member countries. India's Status: Not a member and uses its own sui generis law, the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Authority Act, 2001.
evidenceBullets: Established in 1961 to promote Plant Variety Protection (PVP) and grant Breeder's Rights.
UPOV Convention 1991 strengthens breeders' rights and makes farmer's privilege to save seeds optional.
India is not a member of UPOV.
India uses the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Authority Act, 2001, uniquely protecting both breeder and farmer's rights.

sections.title: Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) Alliance
sections.focus: Coalition of organizations providing biodiversity data and tools.
sections.rationale: A smaller topic, but the specific coalition members (Birdlife, CI, IUCN, UNEP-WCMC) are factual anchors suitable for a standalone objective question.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A coalition of four organizations: Birdlife International, Conservation International, The International Union of Conservation for Nature (IUCN), and The United Nations Environment Programme - World Conservation Monitoring Centre (UNEP-WCMC). Founded in 2008. Mission: To provide data, tools and guidance that help organisations act on biodiversity-related risks.
evidenceBullets: IBAT is a coalition of Birdlife International, Conservation International, IUCN, and UNEP-WCMC.
Founded in 2008 to provide data and tools for biodiversity-related risks.

sections.title: National Green Tribunal (NGT)
sections.focus: Statutory framework, jurisdiction, powers, and governance of the NGT.
sections.rationale: NGT is a core environmental governance topic. Its legislative framework (the 7 Acts), powers (Civil Court, Natural Justice), and appeal process are frequent UPSC targets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A Statutory and specialized body aimed at effective and expeditious disposal of cases relating to environmental protection. Established in 2010 under the NGT Act. Principal Place of Sitting: New Delhi. Legislative Framework: Water Act, Forest Act, Air Act, Environment (Protection) Act, Public Liability Insurance Act, Biological Diversity Act. Powers: Has power of a Civil Court... Guided by Principles of Natural Justice. Members: Hold office for 5 years, but shall not be eligible for re-appointment. Appeal: Orders are binding but not final (appeal could be made in SC within ninety days).
evidenceBullets: Established in 2010 under the NGT Act as a statutory body for environmental cases.
Principal sitting place is New Delhi, with four others: Bhopal, Pune, Kolkata, Chennai.
Jurisdiction covers 7 Acts including Water Act, Forest Act, Air Act, EPA 1986, Public Liability Insurance Act, and Biological Diversity Act.
Has powers of a Civil Court but is guided by Principles of Natural Justice and not bound by the Code of Civil Procedure or Indian Evidence Act.
Members hold office for 5 years and are not eligible for re-appointment.
Appeals against NGT orders can be made to the Supreme Court within 90 days.

sections.subtopic: Global Forest Assessment and UNFCCC COP30
sections.title: Global Forest Resources Assessment (GFRA) 2025
sections.focus: Key findings of the GFRA 2025, FAO's role, and India's global rankings in forest cover and rubber plantations.
sections.rationale: This section covers a major standalone report release with specific factual data (FAO, 5-year frequency, India's 9th rank) that is highly relevant for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Global Forest Resources Assessment (GFRA) 2025 Released by: FAO, every 5 years. This year's edition published during Global Forest Observations Initiative (GFOI) Plenary in Bali, Indonesia. GFOI is a flagship programme of the Group on Earth Observations (GEO) (India is a member). Key highlights: Forests cover 32% of global land area. Nearly half of the world's forests are located in tropics... Europe has the largest forest area, accounting for 25% of world's total. India's Forest Extent: Moved up one rank to 9th position in terms of total forest area globally, accounting for 2% of global forest area; Ranks 5th in terms of rubber plantation.
evidenceBullets: The Global Forest Resources Assessment (GFRA) is released by the FAO every 5 years.
The Global Forest Observations Initiative (GFOI) is a flagship programme of the Group on Earth Observations (GEO), of which India is a member.
Forests cover 32% of the global land area, with nearly half located in the tropics.
Europe has the largest forest area, accounting for 25% of the world's total.
India ranks 9th globally in total forest area (accounting for 2% of global forest area) and 5th in rubber plantations.

sections.subtopic: Global Forest Assessment and UNFCCC COP30
sections.title: UNFCCC COP30 Overview and Background
sections.focus: Details of the COP30 summit in Belem, the Belem Package, and foundational facts about the UNFCCC.
sections.rationale: Covers the basic facts of the recent COP30 and the static background of the UNFCCC, which are frequent targets for UPSC environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The 30th Conference of Parties (COP30) to United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) was held in Belem, Brazil which coincided with the 10th anniversary of the Paris Agreement.
