Hosted in the Amazon region, the summit was framed as the 'COP of Implementation', 'COP of Truth', and the 'Forest COP', rooted in the 'spirit of Mutirao' (inclusion and balance). It concluded with adoption of Belem Package, by 195 Parties... United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) Genesis: It emerged from 1992 Earth Summit at Rio de Janeiro, adopted in 1994. It is one of the three sister conventions (Rio Conventions)... Signatories: 198 (India is a member). Report: NDC Synthesis Report. Headquarters: Bonn, Germany.
evidenceBullets: COP30 was held in Belem, Brazil, coinciding with the 10th anniversary of the Paris Agreement.
The summit concluded with the adoption of the Belem Package by 195 Parties.
UNFCCC emerged from the 1992 Earth Summit at Rio de Janeiro and is one of the three Rio Conventions (along with UNCBD and UNCCD).
UNFCCC has 198 signatories (including India) and is headquartered in Bonn, Germany.
The UNFCCC releases the NDC Synthesis Report.

sections.subtopic: Global Forest Assessment and UNFCCC COP30
sections.title: COP30 Outcomes and Initiatives
sections.focus: Specific initiatives launched at COP30, including the Belem Mission, Global Implementation Accelerator, NAPs Alliance, and TFFF.
sections.rationale: The various funds, alliances, and platforms launched at COPs are critical for UPSC prelims, requiring distinct focus from the general COP overview.
sections.sectionExcerpt: COP30 Outcomes and Initiatives. Implementation Work Programme (Mutirao Decision). Belem Mission to 1.5 C Launched By/Under: COP29-COP31 Troika (UAE, Azerbaijan, Brazil)... Global Implementation Accelerator Launched By/Under: COP 30 & 31 Presidencies... National Adaptation Plans (NAPs) Implementation Alliance Launched By/Under: UNDP, Italy, Germany, NAP Global Network, NDC Partnership... Forests & Nature. Tropical Forest Forever Facility (TFFF) Launched By/Under: Endorsed by Brazil, Indonesia, DRC, China. Primary Purpose: Establish a $125B fund Tropical Forest Investment Fund (TFIF) that pays tropical forest countries for maintaining and expanding forest cover.
evidenceBullets: The Belem Mission to 1.5 C was launched by the COP29-COP31 Troika (UAE, Azerbaijan, Brazil) as an action-oriented platform.
The Global Implementation Accelerator is a voluntary initiative by COP 30 & 31 Presidencies to support NDC and NAP implementation.
The NAPs Implementation Alliance was launched by UNDP, Italy, Germany, NAP Global Network, and NDC Partnership to mobilize investment for national adaptation priorities.
The Tropical Forest Forever Facility (TFFF) was endorsed by Brazil, Indonesia, DRC, and China to establish a $125B Tropical Forest Investment Fund (TFIF).

sections.title: Forest and Agriculture Finance Initiatives
sections.focus: Tropical Forest Forever Facility (TFFF), RAIZ Accelerator, and Scaling J-REDD+ Coalition
sections.rationale: These three initiatives form a distinct cluster focused on mobilizing finance for tropical forests, restoring degraded farmland, and reducing emissions from deforestation.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Tropical Forest Forever Facility (TFFF): India joined as an Observer. Trustee and Interim host: World Bank. Aims to mobilise $25 billion from member countries and attract up to $100 billion from private investors. Mandates at least 20% of all payments flow directly to Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities. Resilient Agriculture Investment for net-Zero land degradation (RAIZ) Accelerator: Brazil & 10 Partner Nations. Hosted by Brazil under the FAO FAST Partnership. To restore 3 million hectares of degraded farmland. Scaling J-REDD+ Coalition: Offers a credible, mature pathway to mobilize $3-6 billion per year by 2030 to halt and reverse tropical deforestation.
evidenceBullets: TFFF aims to mobilize $25 billion with the World Bank as trustee and India participating as an observer.
TFFF mandates that at least 20% of all payments flow directly to Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities.
RAIZ Accelerator aims to restore 3 million hectares of degraded farmland and is hosted by Brazil under the FAO FAST Partnership.
Scaling J-REDD+ Coalition aims to mobilize $3-6 billion per year by 2030 to halt and reverse tropical deforestation.

sections.title: Climate Finance and Adaptation Frameworks
sections.focus: Baku to Belém Roadmap, NCQG, and Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA) frameworks
sections.rationale: This section groups the core COP finance targets (NCQG) and adaptation tracking mechanisms (GGA, Baku Adaptation Roadmap) into a single examinable block.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Baku to Belém Roadmap to 1.3T: The official pathway to mobilize the New Collective Quantified Goal (NCQG) of $1.3 trillion/year by 2035. Tripling of adaptation finance: Increase adaptation finance to $120 billion per year. Measurement framework for tracking adaptation progress under the Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA): Indicators adopted across 7 thematic sectors. Formalizes a four-stage cycle. Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA) was established under Article 7.1 of the Paris Agreement (2015). Baku Adaptation Roadmap: Approves and establishes the work for 2026-2028, until the next Global Stocktake of Paris Agreement.
evidenceBullets: Baku to Belém Roadmap to 1.3T is the official pathway to mobilize the NCQG of $1.3 trillion/year by 2035.
The framework targets tripling adaptation finance to $120 billion per year.
The GGA measurement framework adopted indicators across 7 thematic sectors: water, food, health, ecosystems, infrastructure, poverty, and culture.
The Global Goal on Adaptation (GGA) was established under Article 7.1 of the Paris Agreement (2015).
Baku Adaptation Roadmap establishes adaptation work for 2026-2028 until the next Global Stocktake.

sections.title: General International Climate Initiatives
sections.focus: FINI, Open Coalition on Compliance Carbon Markets, and Belem Declaration for Green Industrialization
sections.rationale: Covers broad international initiatives focused on adaptation investment pipelines, carbon markets, and green industrialization.
sections.sectionExcerpt: FINI (Fostering Investible National Implementation): Supported by IDB and GCF to make NAPs investible and develop USD 1 trillion in adaptation investment pipelines by 2028. Open Coalition on Compliance Carbon Markets: Initiative to harmonize carbon pricing and integrity standards across national borders and collaborate in defining best practices for MRV. Belem Declaration for Green Industrialization: Accelerating decarbonization of heavy emitting industries and promoting green industrialization.
evidenceBullets: FINI is supported by IDB and GCF to develop USD 1 trillion in adaptation investment pipelines by 2028.
Open Coalition on Compliance Carbon Markets aims to harmonize carbon pricing and integrity standards across national borders.
Belem Declaration for Green Industrialization focuses on decarbonizing heavy emitting industries and promoting green industrialization.

sections.title: Energy and Infrastructure Transitions
sections.focus: Power Grids expansion, PACT, and Belém 4x Pledge on Sustainable Fuels
sections.rationale: Groups initiatives specifically targeting energy transition, grid resilience, and sustainable fuels.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Plan to Accelerate the Expansion and Resilience of Power Grids: Supported by Green Grids Initiative (GGI). Plan to Accelerate Coal Transitions (PACT): Supported by Powering Past Coal Alliance (PPCA) to fast-track the transition from coal to clean energy. Belém 4x Pledge on Sustainable Fuels: Participants include Italy, Japan, India and Brazil. Objective is to quadruple the use of sustainable fuels (hydrogen, biofuels, e-fuels) by 2035 compared to 2024 levels, with progress tracked by the IEA.
evidenceBullets: Plan to Accelerate the Expansion and Resilience of Power Grids is supported by the Green Grids Initiative (GGI).
Plan to Accelerate Coal Transitions (PACT) is supported by the Powering Past Coal Alliance (PPCA).
Belém 4x Pledge on Sustainable Fuels aims to quadruple the use of sustainable fuels by 2035 compared to 2024 levels.
Progress of the Belém 4x Pledge will be tracked by the International Energy Agency (IEA).

sections.title: Social, Health, and Information Integrity Declarations
sections.focus: Belém Health Action Plan, Gender Action Plan, Information Integrity, and Hunger/Poverty Declarations
sections.rationale: Clusters the social, health, gender, and information-focused declarations adopted at Belém/COP.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Belém Health Action Plan: World's first international climate adaptation plan dedicated to health, with $300 million initial funding to build climate-resilient health sector. Belém Gender Action Plan (GAP): Adopted by Parties at COP30 for 2026-2034 to implement Lima Work Programme on gender. Declaration on Information Integrity on Climate Change: Establishes shared international commitments to address climate disinformation. First time the topic of information integrity has been included in the COP Action Agenda. Belém Declaration on Hunger, Poverty, and People-Centered Climate Action: Signed by 43 countries and the EU to promote actions addressing unequal climate impacts.
evidenceBullets: Belém Health Action Plan is the world's first international climate adaptation plan dedicated to health, backed by $300 million initial funding.
Belém Gender Action Plan (GAP) was adopted for 2026-2034 to implement the Lima Work Programme on gender.
Declaration on Information Integrity on Climate Change marks the first time information integrity is included in the COP Action Agenda.
Belém Declaration on Hunger, Poverty, and People-Centered Climate Action was signed by 43 countries and the EU to address unequal climate impacts on vulnerable populations.

sections.subtopic: COP30 Health, Oceans, and Methane Initiatives
sections.title: COP30 Emergency Actions and Key Outcomes
sections.focus: Early warning systems, climate-health financing, heat resilience, and food waste reduction initiatives launched at COP30.
sections.rationale: This section covers distinct funds and coalitions (SOFF, CHFC, Cool Coalition) that are highly testable for UPSC prelims regarding their parent bodies and primary targets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Emergency Action includes the 2030 Strategy for Climate Risk and Early Warning Systems (CREWS) and Brazil's Call to Action on Integrated Fire Management. Other outcomes include the Systematic Observation Impact Bond by SOFF (UNEP, WMO, UNDP) to help LDCs and SIDS meet GBON standards. The Climate and Health Funders Coalition committed $300 million. The Global Mutirao Against Extreme Heat, led by the Cool Coalition, aims to reduce heat risks for 3 billion people, alongside the Global Cooling Pledge. The Food Waste Breakthrough, led by UNEP, targets halving global food waste and reducing methane emissions by 7% by 2030.
evidenceBullets: Systematic Observation Impact Bond is the world's first impact bond on weather and climate data, created by SOFF (UNEP, WMO, UNDP).
Climate and Health Funders Coalition (CHFC) committed an initial $300 million focusing on extreme heat, air pollution, and infectious diseases.
Global Mutirao Against Extreme Heat is led by the COP30 Presidency and UNEP-led Cool Coalition, joined by 185 cities.
Food Waste Breakthrough is led by UNEP and targets halving global food waste and a 7% reduction in methane emissions by 2030.

sections.subtopic: COP30 Health, Oceans, and Methane Initiatives
sections.title: COP30 Sectoral Initiatives: Oceans, Methane, and Energy
sections.focus: Various global coalitions and toolkits launched at COP30 targeting ocean conservation, methane reduction, agriculture, and clean energy.
sections.rationale: Contains multiple specific initiatives (Oceans Breakthroughs, NOW, Global Carbon Harvest Coalition, UNEZA, Super Pollutant Accelerator) with clear targets and partner organizations, ideal for match-the-following questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Key initiatives at COP30 include the Oceans Breakthroughs Dashboard to track progress in 5 ocean sectors and the Global network of regenerative seascapes aiming to mobilize USD 20 billion. The No Organic Waste (NOW) initiative, supported by the Global Methane Hub, commits to cutting 30% of methane from organic waste by 2030. The Global Carbon Harvest Coalition involves Brazil, India, and Kenya focusing on soil organic carbon and biochar. The Utilities for Net Zero Alliance (UNEZA) aims to invest USD 1 trillion by 2030. The Super Pollutant Country Action Accelerator by CCAC targets reductions in methane, black carbon, HFCs, and tropospheric ozone.
evidenceBullets: Oceans Breakthroughs Dashboard tracks 5 ocean sectors to help cut emissions by up to 35% by 2050.
No Organic Waste (NOW) initiative is supported by the Global Methane Hub and targets a 30% cut in methane from organic waste by 2030.
Global Carbon Harvest Coalition is a joint effort by Brazil, India, and Kenya focusing on soil organic carbon, biochar, and enhanced rock weathering.
Utilities for Net Zero Alliance (UNEZA) aims to invest approximately USD 1 trillion by 2030 to expand clean energy.
Super Pollutant Country Action Accelerator is a CCAC programme targeting methane, black carbon, HFCs, and tropospheric ozone.

sections.subtopic: COP30 Health, Oceans, and Methane Initiatives
sections.title: Country or Regional Platforms for Climate and Nature Finance
sections.focus: The announcement by India and other developing nations to create climate finance platforms supported by the Green Climate Fund.
sections.rationale: A specific, India-involved climate finance mechanism supported by a major international fund (GCF), making it highly relevant for UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: At COP30, India, along with 12 other developing countries and the African Islands States Climate Commission (AISCC), announced plans to develop 'Country or Regional Platforms for Climate and Nature Finance'. These are strategic, country-driven mechanisms translating climate priorities into programmatic investment approaches. They are supported under the Green Climate Fund's (GCF) Readiness Programme, which is the largest climate action capacity-building facility for developing countries. GCF already supports the Brazil Country Platform and the Caribbean Regional Platform.
evidenceBullets: India, 12 developing countries, and the AISCC announced plans for Country or Regional Platforms for Climate and Nature Finance at COP30.
The platforms are supported by the Green Climate Fund's (GCF) Readiness Programme.
The GCF Readiness Programme is the largest climate action capacity-building facility for developing countries.
Existing platforms supported by GCF include the Brazil Country Platform and the Caribbean Regional Platform.

sections.subtopic: Green Climate Fund and India's NDCs
sections.title: Global Climate Funds and Initiatives
sections.focus: Key international climate funds and partnerships including GCF, ARISE, Blue NDC Challenge, and LeadIT.
sections.rationale: These distinct international climate mechanisms and partnerships are grouped together to provide a solid base for matching or statement-based questions on global climate architecture.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Covers the Green Climate Fund (established COP 16, 50% mitigation/adaptation split), ARISE investment program (implemented through CIF), Blue NDC Challenge (launched by Brazil and France to integrate oceans into NDCs), and LeadIT (launched by India and Sweden for industry transition to net-zero by 2050).
evidenceBullets: Green Climate Fund (GCF) was established at COP 16 in Cancun (2010) and mandates a 50:50 investment split between mitigation and adaptation.
ARISE is implemented through Climate Investment Funds (CIF), which comprises the Clean Technology Fund and Strategic Climate Fund.
Blue NDC Challenge was launched by Brazil and France to place oceans at the heart of NDCs, recognizing oceans as carbon sinks that absorb up to 4 times more CO2 than terrestrial forests.
Leadership Group for Industry Transition (LeadIT) was launched jointly by India and Sweden in 2019 to achieve net-zero carbon emissions from high-emitting industries by 2050.

sections.subtopic: Green Climate Fund and India's NDCs
sections.title: India's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and Panchamrit
sections.focus: India's updated NDC targets for 2030, progress achieved, and the Panchamrit strategy announced at COP26.
sections.rationale: India's NDCs and Panchamrit are core UPSC topics. The quantitative targets, baseline years, and progress metrics are highly testable and form a coherent study block.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Details India's updated 2022 NDCs, including reducing emissions intensity of GDP by 45% from 2005 levels, achieving 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuels, and creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3 billion tonnes. Also covers the Panchamrit commitments made at COP26 Glasgow, including the net-zero target by 2070.
evidenceBullets: India's updated 2022 NDC targets a 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP from 2005 levels by 2030, with 36% already achieved between 2005 and 2020.
India targets 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy by 2030, and has achieved 51% as of September 2025.
India's updated NDC includes the 'LiFE' (Lifestyle for Environment) mass movement component as a key to achieving its quantitative targets.
Panchamrit commitments at COP26 Glasgow include reaching 500 GW non-fossil energy capacity by 2030 and achieving net-zero emissions by 2070.

sections.subtopic: Green Climate Fund and India's NDCs
sections.title: Greenhouse Gases Emission Intensity (GEI) Target Rules, 2025
sections.focus: The newly notified GEI Target Rules, obligated sectors, compliance mechanisms, and penalties.
sections.rationale: A specific, newly notified domestic regulatory framework. The sectors covered, the definition of GEI, and the roles of CPCB and BEE are prime targets for objective questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Outlines the GEI Target Rules 2025 notified by the Union Environment Ministry, proposing legally binding GHG emission targets for over 400 industrial units. It covers four primary energy-intensive sectors (aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, pulp & paper) and five additional sectors. Compliance is linked to the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023, with CPCB imposing environmental compensation for shortfalls.
evidenceBullets: GEI Target Rules 2025 establish legally binding GHG emission targets for four primary sectors: aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, and pulp & paper.
GEI targets are calculated using the Bureau of Energy Efficiency's (BEE) methodology specific to each obligated entity.
Obligated entities must meet targets annually under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023, or purchase certificates from the Indian Carbon Market (ICM).
Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) imposes environmental compensation equal to twice the average traded price of carbon credit certificates for non-compliance.

sections.subtopic: Carbon Credit Trading and Green Credit Program
sections.title: Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) and GHG Emissions
sections.focus: Mechanisms of the CCTS, India's emission statistics from BUR-4, and other market mechanisms like PAT and REC.
sections.rationale: This section covers the regulatory framework for carbon trading in India, including the roles of BEE and CERC, alongside crucial emissions data from BUR-4, making it highly testable for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) is notified under the Energy Conservation Amendment Act, 2022. It operates as an intensity-based baseline-and-credit system covering Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions. The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) is the administrator, and the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) is the regulator. India's 4th Biennial Update Report (BUR-4) notes total GHG emissions at 2,959 million tonnes of CO2 (excluding LULUCF), with the Energy sector contributing the highest (75.66%). Other market mechanisms include the PAT Scheme and Renewable Energy Certificates (REC).
evidenceBullets: CCTS is notified under the Energy Conservation Amendment Act (ECA), 2022.
BEE is the Administrator and CERC is the Regulator of Carbon Trading.
CCTS covers direct emissions (Scope 1) and indirect emissions from electricity/heat (Scope 2).
BUR-4 states Energy is the highest emitting sector (75.66%), followed by Agriculture (13.72%).
Carbon dioxide accounts for 80.53% of emissions, followed by Methane (13.32%).
PAT Scheme reduces Specific Energy Consumption in energy-intensive industries via ESCerts.

sections.subtopic: Carbon Credit Trading and Green Credit Program
sections.title: Green Credit Program (GCP) and Methodology
sections.focus: Objectives, administration, and calculation methodology of the Green Credit Program under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
sections.rationale: The Green Credit Program is a novel domestic initiative linked to Mission LiFE. Its specific administrative bodies (ICFRE), statutory backing (EPA 1986), and revised calculation methodologies are prime targets for objective questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Green Credit Program is an innovative market-based mechanism established under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, to incentivize voluntary environmental actions like afforestation and water conservation. It supports the Mission LiFE initiative. The Indian Council of Forestry Research and Education (ICFRE) is the administering body. Under the revised methodology for tree plantation, 1 Green Credit equals 1 new tree (over 5 years old) on degraded forest land with a minimum 40% canopy density. These specific credits are non-tradable and non-transferable. Eligible land parcels must be at least five hectares.
evidenceBullets: Established under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, with MoEFCC as the nodal agency.
Administered by the Indian Council of Forestry Research and Education (ICFRE), Dehradun.
Supports the Mission LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment) initiative.
Revised tree plantation methodology requires 5 years of restoration and a minimum 40% canopy density to claim credits.
1 Green Credit equals 1 new tree (over 5 years old), and these specific credits are non-tradable and non-transferable.
Identified land parcels for the program must be at least five hectares in size.

sections.subtopic: Carbon Credit Trading and Green Credit Program
sections.title: Article 6 of Paris Climate Agreement
sections.focus: National Designated Authority (NDA) announcement for Article 6 implementation.
sections.rationale: Although brief, the establishment of an NDA under the EPA 1986 for Article 6 of the Paris Agreement is a distinct and important regulatory update.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Ministry of Environment, Forests, and Climate Change (MoEFCC) announced the National Designated Authority (NDA) for the implementation of Article 6 of the Paris Agreement (2015) under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
evidenceBullets: MoEFCC announced the National Designated Authority (NDA) for implementing Article 6 of the Paris Agreement.
The implementation is notified under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.

sections.title: Article 6 of Paris Agreement and Carbon Markets
sections.focus: Mechanisms under Article 6 (6.2, 6.4, 6.8), the role of the National Designated Authority (NDA), and differences between Kyoto Protocol and Paris Agreement carbon trading.
sections.rationale: Article 6 mechanisms are highly examinable for UPSC, especially the distinction between market (6.2, 6.4) and non-market (6.8) approaches, and the transition from Kyoto's CDM to Paris's PACM.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Article 6 enables countries to meet climate goals through voluntary cooperation by establishing international carbon compliance markets. Article 6.2 is a decentralized approach for bilateral cooperation trading ITMOs. Article 6.4 is a centralized Paris Agreement Crediting Mechanism (PACM) succeeding Kyoto's CDM. Article 6.8 introduces non-market approaches. The NDA, a 21-member committee headed by the Environment Ministry Secretary, recommends eligible projects like green hydrogen and CCUS. Unlike Kyoto which limited participation to developed countries, Paris includes all countries and allocates 5 percent of Article 6.4 proceeds to the Global Adaptation Fund.
evidenceBullets: NDA is a 21-member committee headed by the Secretary, Environment Ministry, authorizing projects under Article 6.4.
Article 6.2 involves decentralized bilateral trading of Internationally Transferred Mitigation Outcomes (ITMOs) with corresponding NDC adjustments.
Article 6.4 establishes a centralized global carbon market (PACM), succeeding the Kyoto Protocol's Clean Development Mechanism (CDM).
Article 6.8 focuses on non-market approaches with no trading of emission reductions.
Paris Agreement allocates 5% of proceeds from Article 6.4 transactions to the Global Adaptation Fund, whereas Kyoto directed CDM proceeds to the Adaptation Fund.
Paris Agreement restricts legacy credit use to post-2013 credits, unlike Kyoto which allowed older credits.

sections.title: Montreal Protocol and Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)
sections.focus: Key provisions of the Montreal Protocol, Kigali Amendment, characteristics of HFCs, and India's achievements in phasing out Ozone Depleting Substances.
sections.rationale: The Montreal Protocol and Kigali Amendment are classic UPSC topics. The distinction between ODS and non-ODS (like HFCs), and the specific recovery timelines are prime targets for objective questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Montreal Protocol (signed 1987, implemented under the 1985 Vienna Convention) is a legally binding treaty to eliminate Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS) like CFCs and Halons. The 2016 Kigali Amendment aims to phase down HFCs, which are non-ODS but potent greenhouse gases. HFCs are short-lived climate pollutants with an average atmospheric lifetime of 15 years, warming the planet 3,790 times as much as CO2 over 20 years. The ozone layer is expected to recover to 1980 values by 2040 globally, 2045 over the Arctic, and 2066 over the Antarctic. India phased out CFCs, CTC, and halons by 2010, ahead of schedule.
evidenceBullets: Montreal Protocol is implemented under the Vienna Convention (adopted in 1985).
Kigali Amendment (2016) targets the phase-down of HFCs, which are non-ODS but potent greenhouse gases.
Ozone layer recovery to 1980 values is expected by 2040 globally, 2045 over the Arctic, and 2066 over the Antarctic.
HFCs are synthetic gases used for cooling, with an average atmospheric lifetime of 15 years, and warm the planet 3,790 times as much as CO2 over a 20-year period.
India phased out CFCs, Carbon Tetrachloride (CTC), and halons for controlled use by 2010, ahead of Montreal Protocol schedules.

sections.title: Net Zero Framework for Global Shipping
sections.focus: IMO's Net-Zero Framework, MARPOL Annex VI, and mandatory compliance measures for international shipping.
sections.rationale: International maritime emissions and IMO regulations are emerging environmental topics. The specific thresholds and mechanisms (Global Fuel Standard, MARPOL) are highly testable.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) approved the Net-Zero Framework for Global Shipping, aiming for net-zero emissions close to 2050. Shipping accounts for almost 3 percent of global GHG emissions. The Framework will be included in Annex VI of MARPOL (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships). It is the first framework to combine mandatory emissions limits and GHG pricing across an entire sector. It applies to large ocean-going ships over 5,000 gross tonnage (emitting 85 percent of international shipping CO2). Compliance requires adhering to a Global Fuel Standard to reduce greenhouse gas fuel intensity (GFI) and a Global Economic Measure involving remedial units and financial rewards.
evidenceBullets: The Net-Zero Framework for Global Shipping was approved by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) aiming for net-zero by around 2050.
The framework will be included in Annex VI of MARPOL, which prevents air pollution from ships.
Shipping accounts for almost 3% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
The framework becomes mandatory for large ocean-going ships over 5,000 gross tonnage.
Ships must comply with a Global Fuel Standard to reduce their annual greenhouse gas fuel intensity (GFI).
An IMO Net-Zero Fund will be established to collect pricing contributions from emissions.

sections.subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
sections.title: International Maritime Organization (IMO) and Key Conventions
sections.focus: IMO's structure, membership, and major maritime conventions like SOLAS, MARPOL, and the Hong Kong Convention.
sections.rationale: This is a distinct institutional topic covering IMO basics and its associated conventions, which are highly testable for UPSC Prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is a UN specialized agency responsible for shipping safety and preventing marine pollution. Established via a 1948 Geneva convention (in force 1958), it has 176 members, including India. Its highest technical body is the Maritime Safety Committee (MSC). Key conventions include SOLAS (1974), MARPOL, and the Hong Kong Convention (2009) for safe and environmentally sound ship recycling.
evidenceBullets: IMO is a UN specialized agency for shipping safety and marine/atmospheric pollution prevention.
India is one of the 176 Member States of the IMO.
The Maritime Safety Committee (MSC) is the IMO's highest technical body.
Key conventions include SOLAS (1974), MARPOL, and the Hong Kong Convention (2009) for ship recycling.

sections.subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
sections.title: Carbon Capture and Utilisation (CCU) and Ocean-based CCUS
sections.focus: Technologies for capturing CO2 (DAC, industrial) and utilizing it (EOR, fuels, chemicals), plus ocean-based storage methods.
sections.rationale: Covers the DST roadmap, CCU pathways, and ocean-based CCUS techniques. The intersection of Science & Tech and Environment makes it a prime area for conceptual questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Carbon Capture and Utilisation (CCU) involves capturing CO2 from industrial sources or via Direct Air Capture (DAC) and using it as feedstock. Direct utilization includes Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) and industrial uses, while CO2-to-Products involves converting CO2 into chemicals (urea, plastics), fuels (methanol via Fischer-Tropsch), and mineral carbonates (cement). Ocean-based CCUS includes Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE) and Ocean Fertilisation to boost CO2 absorption.
evidenceBullets: The Department of Science and Technology (DST) launched India's first R&D Roadmap for Net Zero through CCUS.
Direct Air Capture (DAC) isolates CO2 directly from ambient air using a gas trapping system.
Direct Utilisation of CO2 includes Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) and food/healthcare applications.
CO2-to-Products includes conversion to methanol/fuels via Fischer-Tropsch synthesis and mineral carbonates for building materials.
Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE) accelerates CO2 absorption by adding pulverized minerals like lime.
Ocean fertilisation promotes phytoplankton growth by adding micronutrients like phosphorus and nitrogen.

sections.subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
sections.title: Methane Emissions and the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC)
sections.focus: Methane's properties as a GHG, major emission sources, and the role of the CCAC.
sections.rationale: Methane is a critical Short-Lived Climate Pollutant (SLCP). The CCAC's mandate and India's emission status are prime UPSC targets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Global Methane Status Report 2025 was released by UNEP and the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC). The CCAC, founded in 2012 within UNEP, targets short-lived climate pollutants (methane, black carbon, HFCs, tropospheric ozone); India joined in 2019. Methane is the second biggest driver of climate change, over 80 times more potent than CO2 over 20 years, but persists for only 7-12 years. 60% of emissions are human-driven (agriculture, fossil fuels, landfills), while wetlands are the largest natural source. India is the 3rd largest emitter.
evidenceBullets: The Global Methane Status Report 2025 was released by UNEP and the CCAC.
CCAC (founded 2012, convened within UNEP) targets short-lived climate pollutants: methane, black carbon, HFCs, and tropospheric ozone.
India joined the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) in 2019.
Methane is over 80 times more potent than CO2 over a 20-year period but persists for only 7-12 years.
60% of methane emissions are anthropogenic; wetlands are the largest natural source.
India is the world's 3rd largest methane emitter, primarily driven by stubble burning.

sections.subtopic: IMO Conventions and Carbon Capture Technologies
sections.title: Glacier Preservation and Glacial Loss
sections.focus: Recent declarations of dead glaciers and the environmental consequences of glacial melting.
sections.rationale: Specific environmental events (dead glaciers) and geographical consequences (albedo, sea level, GLOFs) form a distinct, testable cluster.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Nepal's Yala Glacier was recently declared dead after shrinking 66% since the 1970s. Other dead glaciers include Pizol (Switzerland), Clark (US), Ayoloco (Mexico), and Okjokull (Iceland), which had the world's first glacier funeral in 2019. Consequences of glacial loss include reduced albedo effect (increasing heat absorption), a 2 cm global sea level rise since 2001, loss of freshwater (glaciers store nearly 75% of Earth's freshwater), and increased risks of Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) and avalanches.
evidenceBullets: Nepal's Yala Glacier was declared dead after shrinking 66% since the 1970s.
Okjokull glacier in Iceland had the world's first glacier funeral in 2019.
Glacial loss reduces the albedo effect, leading to increased heat absorption.
Melting glaciers have contributed nearly 2 cm to global sea level rise since 2001.
Glaciers store nearly three-quarters of Earth's freshwater.

sections.subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
sections.title: Glacier Conservation Initiatives (Global and India)
sections.focus: UN and Indian initiatives for glacier preservation and Himalayan ecosystem research.
sections.rationale: Distinct topic covering international and national efforts for glacier conservation, highly testable for prelims regarding specific years, locations, and projects like ICE-CRUNCH.
sections.sectionExcerpt: 2025 designated as the International Year of Glaciers' Preservation; Decade of Action for Cryospheric Sciences (2025–2034)... ICE-CRUNCH: A collaborative study between scientists of India and Switzerland. India’s first High-Altitude Climate Research Station: In Nathatop, Jammu and Kashmir for Climate research... affiliated with the World Meteorological Organization’s (WMO) Global Atmospheric Watch (GAW) Programme.
evidenceBullets: 2025 is designated as the International Year of Glaciers' Preservation.
The Decade of Action for Cryospheric Sciences spans from 2025 to 2034.
ICE-CRUNCH is an Indo-Swiss collaborative study on ice nucleating particles in the North-Western Himalayas.
India's first High-Altitude Climate Research Station is located in Nathatop, Jammu and Kashmir, and is affiliated with the WMO's Global Atmospheric Watch Programme.

sections.subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
sections.title: India's Polar Research Stations and Maitri II
sections.focus: India's upcoming and existing research bases in Antarctica and the Arctic.
sections.rationale: Standalone topic on India's scientific presence in polar regions, frequently asked in UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Union Finance Ministry granted approval to Maitri II, new research station in Eastern Antarctica. This will be India’s 4th research base, expected to be operational by January 2029. It would be established as a green research base powered using renewable energy sources... India’s other Polar research stations: Maitri (Antarctica), Bharati (Antarctica), Dakshin Gangotri (first scientific base station of India situated in Antarctica now decommissioned), and Himadri (Arctic).
evidenceBullets: Maitri II is India's upcoming 4th research base in Eastern Antarctica, expected to be operational by 2029.
Maitri II will be a green research base powered by solar and wind energy.
India's Antarctic stations include Dakshin Gangotri (decommissioned), Maitri, and Bharati.
Himadri is India's active research station in the Arctic.
The Indian Antarctic Act was enacted in 2022 to provide a legal framework for India's polar activities.

sections.subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
sections.title: Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) and Net-Zero Banking Alliance
sections.focus: International frameworks for corporate climate disclosures and the banking sector's net-zero transition.
sections.rationale: Groups two related financial and corporate climate initiatives that are important for environment and economy intersections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) launched the Integrity Matters Checklist, a UN-endorsed framework designed to align corporate climate disclosures with the United Nations’ net-zero standards. Established: 1997... Net-Zero Banking Alliance: It has been dissolved after several member banks withdrew. Established in 2021 under UN Environment Programme's Finance Initiative.
evidenceBullets: GRI launched the Integrity Matters Checklist to align corporate climate disclosures with UN net-zero standards.
GRI was established in 1997 as a non-profit body setting sustainability reporting frameworks.
The Net-Zero Banking Alliance was established in 2021 under the UNEP Finance Initiative but has recently been dissolved.
The Net-Zero Banking Alliance aimed to encourage banks to reduce the carbon footprint of their loans and investments.

sections.subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
sections.title: UAE Consensus and COP28 Outcomes
sections.focus: Key targets and agreements adopted under the UAE Consensus at COP28.
sections.rationale: Major international climate agreement outcome, distinct from other news items and highly relevant for global climate governance questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: UAE Consensus: A recent UN report highlights the UAE Consensus as a key milestone advancing global clean energy transition. Adopted at COP28 (2023), marked the first-ever Global Stocktake under the Paris Agreement. Urges nations to transition away from fossil fuels in a just, fair, and balanced way. Sets goals to triple renewable energy and double energy efficiency by 2030. Reinforces the collective aim to keep 1.5°C within reach and achieve net zero emissions by 2050.
evidenceBullets: The UAE Consensus was adopted at COP28 in 2023, marking the first-ever Global Stocktake under the Paris Agreement.
It sets global goals to triple renewable energy and double energy efficiency by 2030.
It urges nations to transition away from fossil fuels to achieve net zero emissions by 2050.
The consensus reinforces the collective aim to keep global warming within 1.5 degrees Celsius.

sections.subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
sections.title: Climate Monitoring Projects: AmazonFACE and NASA OCO
sections.focus: Scientific projects monitoring CO2 absorption, forest resilience, and atmospheric carbon.
sections.rationale: Groups two specific scientific monitoring initiatives related to carbon sinks and atmospheric CO2, testing knowledge of environmental monitoring tools.
sections.sectionExcerpt: AmazonFACE: Scientists in Brazil have launched the AmazonFACE (Free-Air CO2 Enrichment) project near Manaus. It seeks to simulate the atmosphere of the future and assess how tropical forests adapt to rising CO2... NASA’ Orbiting Carbon Observatory (OCO): The USA administration plans to shut down NASA’s OCO missions... Key Findings: Amazon rainforest emits more CO2 than it absorbs. Boreal forests in Canada, Russia, and melting permafrost areas absorb more CO2 than they emit.
evidenceBullets: AmazonFACE is a Brazilian project simulating future atmospheres to assess how tropical forests adapt to rising CO2 and temperatures.
NASA's Orbiting Carbon Observatory (OCO) missions are key data sources for tracking atmospheric carbon.
OCO findings reveal that the Amazon rainforest currently emits more CO2 than it absorbs.
Boreal forests and melting permafrost areas in Canada and Russia currently absorb more CO2 than they emit.

sections.subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
sections.title: Climate Tipping Points
sections.focus: Definition, mechanisms, and examples of climate tipping points as identified by the IPCC.
sections.rationale: Core geographical and environmental concept, clearly separated under its own heading, essential for understanding irreversible climate changes.
sections.sectionExcerpt: About climate tipping point: These are critical thresholds in a system that, when exceeded, can lead to a significant change in the state of the system, often with an understanding that the change is irreversible... Tipping points are interlinked, meaning crossing one may accelerate collapse of others... Tipping points identified by IPCC: Amazon rainforest dieback, permafrost collapse, Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) slowdown, Boreal Forest shift, Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets collapse risks.
evidenceBullets: Climate tipping points are critical thresholds that lead to significant, often irreversible, system changes when exceeded.
Tipping points are interlinked, meaning crossing one can create cascading effects that threaten planetary stability.
IPCC-identified tipping points include Amazon dieback, permafrost collapse, AMOC slowdown, and ice sheet collapse.
Warm-water coral reefs have recently crossed their thermal tipping point, triggering irreversible dieback.

sections.subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
sections.title: Arctic Amplification
sections.focus: Causes and impacts of the Arctic warming faster than the rest of the globe.
sections.rationale: Core geographical concept with specific mechanisms (albedo, lapse rate, water vapour) and direct impacts on the Indian monsoon.
sections.sectionExcerpt: About Arctic Amplification: A phenomenon where climate in Arctic warms more quickly than the rest of the Earth. Factors for Arctic Amplification: Reduced Albedo due to melting of reflective ice... Lapse rate feedback (warming from GHG most pronounced near the surface...); Water Vapour Triple Effect... Atmospheric Heat transport from tropics. Impacts on India: Disruption of Indian Monsoon; Sea level rise etc.
evidenceBullets: Arctic Amplification is the phenomenon where the Arctic warms significantly faster than the rest of the Earth.
Reduced albedo from melting reflective ice acts as a feedback loop driving Arctic Amplification.
Lapse rate feedback in the Arctic causes greenhouse gas warming to be most pronounced near the surface, unlike in the tropics.
The Water Vapour Triple Effect contributes to amplification by creating more cloud cover and acting as a greenhouse gas.
Arctic Amplification impacts India by disrupting the Indian Monsoon and contributing to sea level rise.

sections.title: Planetary Boundaries
sections.focus: Concept of Planetary Boundaries, the 9 specific processes, and their current transgression status according to the Planetary Health Check 2025.
sections.rationale: This is a distinct scientific concept regarding ecological thresholds, separate from the organizational details of UNEP.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Planetary Health Check (PHC) 2025, released by the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research (PIK), provides a scientific assessment regarding Planetary Boundaries (PBs). PBs are ecological thresholds defining the safe limits... 7 of the 9 PBs have been transgressed except Atmospheric Aerosol Loading & Stratospheric Ozone Depletion and Ocean Acidification crossed its safe operating space for the first time.
evidenceBullets: Planetary Health Check 2025 released by Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research (PIK).
Planetary Boundaries are 9 ecological thresholds defining humanity's safe operating space.
7 of 9 boundaries have been transgressed.
Atmospheric Aerosol Loading and Stratospheric Ozone Depletion have not been transgressed.
Ocean Acidification crossed its safe operating space for the first time.

sections.title: United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)
sections.focus: UNEP's genesis, funding, hosted entities (IPCC, IPBES), administered multilateral agreements, and key reports.
sections.rationale: UNEP is a major international organization with extensive factual details (conventions, reports) that form a distinct, highly examinable cluster.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Location: Nairobi, Kenya. Genesis: 1972, following UN Conference on Human Environment... Hosts the secretariats of various conventions and Entities: IPBES, IPCC... Major Global Multilateral Agreements under UNEP: CITES, CMS, Vienna, Basel, CBD, Rotterdam, Stockholm, Minamata... Reports: Emission Gap, Adaptation Gap, Global Environment Outlook 7.
evidenceBullets: Established in 1972 following the UN Conference on Human Environment, headquartered in Nairobi, Kenya.
Relies on voluntary contributions for over 95% of funding; Environment Fund is its core fund.
Hosts secretariats for IPBES and IPCC (jointly with WMO).
Administers major agreements including CITES, CMS, Vienna Convention, Basel, CBD, Rotterdam, Stockholm, and Minamata Conventions.
Publishes key reports: Emission Gap Report, Adaptation Gap Report, Global Environment Outlook, and State of Finance for Forests.
Supported the Common Carbon Metric for assessing building operations' carbon footprint.

sections.title: Global Environment Facility (GEF)
sections.focus: Institutional framework, genesis, and the five major environmental conventions funded by GEF.
sections.rationale: GEF is a highly examinable multilateral fund. UPSC frequently asks about the specific conventions it funds.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Global Environment Facility (GEF) is located in Washington, D.C., USA. It is the world's largest multilateral fund for the environment, established in 1992 during the Rio Earth Summit. The 1st Assembly took place in India in 1998. It acts as a financial mechanism for 5 major international environmental conventions: UNFCCC, UNCBD, UNCCD, Minamata Convention on Mercury, and Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs).
evidenceBullets: Established in 1992 during the Rio Earth Summit.
Acts as a financial mechanism for UNFCCC, UNCBD, UNCCD, Minamata Convention, and Stockholm Convention.
World's largest multilateral fund for environment, located in Washington, D.C.

sections.title: World Meteorological Organisation (WMO)
sections.focus: WMO's establishment, Global Atmosphere Watch Programme, and key findings from its Greenhouse Gas Bulletin and other reports.
sections.rationale: WMO reports (like Greenhouse Gas Bulletin and State of the Global Climate) and its specialized agency status are prime targets for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: WMO is a specialized UN agency established in 1950, located in Geneva, Switzerland, with 193 Member States including India. Initiatives include the Global Atmosphere Watch (GAW) Programme. Key reports include the Greenhouse Gas Bulletin (noting atmospheric CO2 reached 423.9 ppm in 2024 and long-lived greenhouse gases increased radiative forcing by 54%), State of the Global Climate, State of Global Water Resources, and Air Quality and Climate Bulletin.
evidenceBullets: Established in 1950 as a specialized agency of the United Nations, headquartered in Geneva.
Coordinates global atmospheric monitoring networks through the Global Atmosphere Watch (GAW) Programme.
Publishes the Greenhouse Gas Bulletin, State of the Global Climate, and State of Global Water Resources.
Greenhouse Gas Bulletin 2024 reported CO2 levels at 423.9 ppm and a 54% increase in radiative forcing from long-lived greenhouse gases.

sections.title: State and Trends of Carbon Pricing 2025
sections.focus: World Bank's report on carbon pricing and the definitions of various carbon pricing instruments.
sections.rationale: Carbon pricing mechanisms (ETS, Carbon Taxes, Crediting) are core economy-environment intersection topics for UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The State and Trends of Carbon Pricing 2025 report is released by the World Bank Group. Carbon pricing puts a financial cost on greenhouse gas emissions. Instruments include Emissions Trading Systems (government sets a cap on emissions), Carbon Taxes (government levies a fee on emissions), and Carbon Crediting Mechanisms (tradable credits generated through voluntary emission reduction activities).
evidenceBullets: State and Trends of Carbon Pricing 2025 report is released by the World Bank Group.
Emissions Trading Systems involve the government setting a cap on GHG emissions or emission intensity.
Carbon Taxes involve the government levying a fee on covered entities for their GHG emissions.
Carbon Crediting Mechanisms generate tradable credits through voluntary activities that reduce emissions.

sections.subtopic: Energy Transition and Climate Risk Indices
sections.title: State of the Cryosphere Report 2025
sections.focus: Publisher and sector coverage of the report.
sections.rationale: A brief but distinct report requiring knowledge of its publisher and sector focus.
sections.sectionExcerpt: State of the Cryosphere Report 2025 - Released by: International Cryosphere Climate Initiative - Sector Wise Coverage: Power followed by industry sector have highest coverage.
evidenceBullets: Released by the International Cryosphere Climate Initiative.
Power and industry sectors have the highest coverage in the report.

sections.subtopic: Energy Transition and Climate Risk Indices
sections.title: Energy Transition Index (ETI) 2025
sections.focus: Publisher, global rankings, India's rank, and assessment parameters.
sections.rationale: Highly examinable index by WEF with specific parameters and ranking shifts for India.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Energy Transition Index (ETI), 2025 - Released by: World Economic Forum (WEF) - Ranks countries on their progress towards energy transition from fossil fuels to clean energy. - Sweden ranked first followed by Finland, Denmark and Norway. - India's rank dropped from rank 63 in 2024 to rank 71 in 2025. - Takes into account two main aspects: System Performance (energy security, equity & sustainability) and Transition Readiness (regulation, infrastructure, investment etc.).
evidenceBullets: Released by the World Economic Forum (WEF).
Sweden ranked first in the 2025 index.
India's rank dropped to 71 in 2025 from 63 in 2024.
Assesses System Performance (energy security, equity, sustainability) and Transition Readiness (regulation, infrastructure, investment).

sections.subtopic: Energy Transition and Climate Risk Indices
sections.title: Climate Risk Index (CRI) 2026
sections.focus: Publisher, ranking basis, and India's historical and current rankings.
sections.rationale: Important index by Germanwatch focusing on the human and economic toll of extreme weather.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Climate Risk Index (CRI) 2026 - Published by: Germanwatch - Ranks countries based on human and economic toll of extreme weather events. - Introduced in 2006 as an annual global climate impact index. - India ranked 15th in CRI 2024 and 9th in CRI for 1995-2024.
evidenceBullets: Published by Germanwatch.
Ranks countries based on the human and economic toll of extreme weather events.
India ranked 15th in CRI 2024 and 9th for the period 1995-2024.

sections.subtopic: Energy Transition and Climate Risk Indices
sections.title: Climate Change Performance Index (CCPI) 2026
sections.focus: Publishers, top rankings, India's rank, and the four measurement categories.
sections.rationale: A major climate index with multiple publishers, specific measurement categories, and notable ranking details such as an empty top 3.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Climate Change Performance Index (CCPI) 2026 - Published by: Germanwatch, New Climate Institute & Climate Action Network (CAN). - Compares climate performance of 63 countries and the EU, which together account for over 90% of global GHG emissions. - None of the countries are in the top 3. Denmark is 4th, followed by UK & Morocco. - India ranks 23rd, going from a high performer to a medium one in this year's CCPI. - Measures performance in 4 categories: GHG Emissions, Renewable Energy, Energy Use and Climate Policy.
evidenceBullets: Published by Germanwatch, New Climate Institute, and Climate Action Network (CAN).
Evaluates 63 countries and the EU, covering over 90% of global GHG emissions.
No country achieved the top 3 ranks; Denmark is ranked highest at 4th.
India ranks 23rd, dropping from a high performer to a medium performer.
Measures performance across 4 categories: GHG Emissions, Renewable Energy, Energy Use, and Climate Policy.

sections.title: Basel, Rotterdam, and Stockholm (BRS) Conventions
sections.focus: Recent COP outcomes, specific chemical additions, and core mandates of the BRS conventions.
sections.rationale: The BRS conventions are frequently tested together due to their overlapping focus on hazardous chemicals and wastes. The recent COP additions (specific chemicals) and core mechanisms (PIC, POPs) are prime UPSC targets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The 17th Basel, 12th Rotterdam, and 12th Stockholm Convention meetings in Geneva yielded key updates. Basel (hazardous wastes) launched work on textile waste and updated POPs guidelines. Rotterdam (PIC for hazardous chemicals) added Carbosulfan and Fenthion to Annex III. Stockholm (POPs) added Chlorpyrifos, LC-PFCAs, and MCCPs to Annex A, and amended UV-328 exemptions. India is a party to all three. Basel enforces Prior Informed Consent (PIC) for transboundary waste but excludes radioactive waste. Rotterdam promotes informed decisions on international trade of banned/restricted chemicals without outright banning them. Stockholm aims to protect health and environment from persistent organic pollutants.
evidenceBullets: Basel Convention (1989) enforces Prior Informed Consent (PIC) for transboundary hazardous wastes, excludes radioactive waste, and India is a party.
Rotterdam Convention (2004) creates a legally binding PIC procedure for international trade of certain hazardous chemicals/pesticides but does not outright ban them.
Stockholm Convention (2004) targets Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs); India ratified it in 2006.
Rotterdam COP-12 added Carbosulfan and Fenthion to Annex III.
Stockholm COP-12 added Chlorpyrifos, LC-PFCAs, and MCCPs to Annex A, and amended exemptions for UV-328 (a phenolic benzotriazole used as a UV stabilizer).

sections.title: Minamata Convention and Mercury Pollution
sections.focus: COP-6 outcomes, Minamata Convention provisions, and characteristics/toxicity of Mercury.
sections.rationale: Mercury pollution is a classic environmental chemistry topic. The COP-6 targets (dental amalgams, skin-lightening) and the bioaccumulation of methylmercury are highly testable.
sections.sectionExcerpt: COP-6 to the Minamata Convention on Mercury agreed to phase out mercury-based dental amalgams by 2034, eliminate mercury-added skin-lightening products, and strengthen action on artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASGM). The Minamata Convention is a legally binding treaty (effective 2017) with UNEP secretariat support; India is a member. Mercury is a naturally occurring heavy metal, liquid at room temperature, sourced from coal ash, thermal plants, and gold mining. Emitted mercury converts into highly toxic methylmercury, which bioaccumulates in aquatic food chains. There is no known safe level of mercury exposure for humans.
evidenceBullets: The Minamata Convention (effective 2017) is a legally binding treaty to protect human health and the environment from mercury; India is a member.
COP-6 agreed to phase out mercury-based dental amalgams by 2034 and eliminate mercury-added skin-lightening products.
Mercury is the only common metal that is liquid at room temperature; anthropogenic sources include coal-based thermal power plants and gold mining.
Mercury converts into highly toxic methylmercury, leading to bioaccumulation in fish and animals.
There is no known safe level of mercury exposure for humans.

sections.title: Global Plastic Pollution Treaty
sections.focus: INC negotiations, country groupings (Like Minded vs. High Ambition), and the historical evolution of plastic pollution treaties.
sections.rationale: The ongoing negotiations for a legally binding plastic treaty are highly relevant. The divide between production-restricting countries and those opposing it (including India) is a key geopolitical and environmental issue.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee (INC) talks for a Global Plastic Pollution Treaty, mandated by a 2022 UNEA resolution, recently adjourned. The treaty aims for an international legally binding instrument addressing the full plastic lifecycle. Disagreements persist: Like Minded Countries (including India, China, Saudi Arabia) reject restrictions on plastic production, whereas the High Ambition Coalition (around 100 countries, including the EU) argues production caps are essential. The evolution of global plastic regulation includes MARPOL Annex V (1988) banning ship-based plastic discharges, the 2019 Basel Convention amendments bringing plastic waste under PIC rules, and SDG 12 & 14.1.
evidenceBullets: The Global Plastic Pollution Treaty is being negotiated by the INC under a 2022 UNEA resolution to create a legally binding instrument addressing the plastic lifecycle.
Like Minded Countries, including India, China, and Saudi Arabia, reject restrictions on plastic production.
The High Ambition Coalition, including the European Union, advocates for essential production caps on plastics.
MARPOL Annex V (1988) was the first treaty banning plastics in ship-based discharges.
The 2019 Basel Convention Amendments brought plastic waste under global notification and Prior Informed Consent (PIC) rules.

sections.title: Plastic and Microplastic Pollution
sections.focus: Characteristics, types, and impacts of microplastics, along with India's regulatory framework (PWM Rules).
sections.rationale: Covers the foundational concepts of microplastics (primary vs secondary) and the specific regulatory timeline/thickness limits of India's Plastic Waste Management Rules, which are highly testable in UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Types: Microplastic (up to 5 millimetres in diameter); Nanoplastics (less than 1,000 nanometers)... Primary Microplastics: Intentionally manufactured. E.g. Plastic pellets, Microbeads... Secondary Microplastics: From breakdown of larger plastic items. E.g. Microfibers... PWM (Amendment) Rules, 2021: Raised thickness limit (50 microns to 75 microns starting September 30, 2021, and then to 120 microns from December 31, 2022) for carry bags.
evidenceBullets: Microplastics are up to 5 millimetres in diameter, while nanoplastics are less than 1,000 nanometers.
Primary microplastics are intentionally manufactured (e.g., microbeads in cosmetics), whereas secondary microplastics result from the breakdown of larger items (e.g., microfibers).
Microplastics reduce plants' photosynthetic ability by around 12% and cause biomagnification in marine life.
The Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 raised the thickness limit of plastic carry bags to 120 microns effective December 31, 2022.

sections.title: Plastic-Related Chemical Terms and Bioremediation
sections.focus: Specific polymers, plasticizers (Phthalates), and plastic-degrading bacteria in the news.
sections.rationale: UPSC frequently asks about specific chemicals (like Phthalates, PBAT) and bioremediation agents (Ideonella sakaiensis) in the Science & Tech or Environment sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Bacteria Ideonella sakaiensis: Plastic eating bacteria that can degrade PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)... Polybutylene Adipate Terephthalate (PBAT): Biodegradable, chemical-free thermoplastic copolymer made from renewable resources, like corn starch and sugarcane... Pthalates: Ortho-phthalates, or “phthalates,” are chemicals used in plastic products (commonly in specific type of plastic named polyvinyl chloride) to make the material soft and less brittle... Categorized as hazardous endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs); affect reproductive health.
evidenceBullets: Ideonella sakaiensis is a plastic-eating bacteria capable of degrading PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate).
PBAT is a biodegradable thermoplastic copolymer derived from renewable resources like corn starch and sugarcane.
Phthalates are plasticizers added to plastics like PVC to make them soft and less brittle, but they do not permanently bind to the plastic surfaces.
Phthalates are categorized as hazardous endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) that negatively affect reproductive health.

sections.title: Urban Air Pollution and GRAP
sections.focus: GRAP stages, IQAir report findings, WHO vs NAAQS standards, and India's mitigation initiatives.
sections.rationale: Air pollution is a perennial UPSC topic. The specific AQI thresholds for GRAP stages, the comparison between WHO and NAAQS standards, and recent schemes (PM E-DRIVE, SATAT) are prime targets for objective questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP)... Emergency response mechanism based on AQI level of Delhi implemented by CAQM... Stage 1: Poor category (AQI 201 to 300)... Stage 4: Severe + category (AQI 451+)... Causes: Winter weather conditions like temperature inversion; Narrow streets with tall buildings (street-canyon effect) trapping pollutants... Comparison of Air Quality Standards: PM2.5 WHO 2021 Guideline is 5 µg/m³, India (NAAQS) is 40 µg/m³... Steps taken to curb Air Pollution: Promotion of electric mobility under PM E-DRIVE Scheme... SATAT initiative for creating Compressed Bio-Gas (CBG) ecosystem.
evidenceBullets: GRAP is an emergency response mechanism implemented by CAQM, originally created on Supreme Court directions.
GRAP Stage 4 (Severe + category) is triggered when the AQI exceeds 451.
The street-canyon effect, caused by narrow streets with tall buildings, traps pollutants and exacerbates urban air pollution.
India's NAAQS limit for PM2.5 is 40 µg/m³, which is significantly higher than the WHO 2021 guideline of 5 µg/m³.
The SATAT initiative aims to create a Compressed Bio-Gas (CBG) ecosystem to help curb air pollution.

sections.title: Cloud Seeding
sections.focus: Scientific weather modification technique and specific seeding agents.
sections.rationale: A highly specific scientific concept with clear factual anchors regarding the chemicals used and the nature of the process.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Scientific weather modification technique involving dispersing agents like silver iodide into moisture-bearing clouds to induce precipitation. Conducted on existing natural clouds and does not create clouds. Seeding Agents: Silver Iodide (AgI), most common material, known for its efficient ice nucleating properties. Other chemicals used are potassium iodide (KI), sulfur dioxide (SO2), frozen carbon dioxide (dry ice).
evidenceBullets: Cloud seeding is conducted on existing natural clouds and does not create new clouds.
Silver Iodide (AgI) is the most common seeding agent due to its efficient ice nucleating properties.
Other chemicals used include potassium iodide, sulfur dioxide, and dry ice.

sections.title: National Air Quality Index (AQI)
sections.focus: AQI categories, ranges, and the specific pollutants monitored.
sections.rationale: Highly examinable topic for UPSC prelims, specifically testing the knowledge of the 8 monitored pollutants and the 6 AQI categories.
sections.sectionExcerpt: AQI categorizes air quality into six ranges: Good (0-50), Satisfactory (51-100), Moderate (101-200), Poor (201-300), Very Poor (300-400), and Severe (>400). Monitored Pollutants: PM10, PM2.5, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb.
evidenceBullets: AQI has 6 categories ranging from Good to Severe, with Severe defined as an AQI greater than 400.
The index monitors exactly 8 pollutants: PM10, PM2.5, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, and Pb.

sections.title: Commission for Air Quality Management in NCR and Adjoining Areas (CAQM)
sections.focus: Statutory backing, nodal ministry, jurisdiction, and overriding powers of the CAQM.
sections.rationale: Important statutory body with specific jurisdiction and unique overriding powers over state bodies, making it a prime target for polity and environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Statutory body under Commission for Air Quality Management in National Capital Region and Adjoining Areas Act 2021. Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change. Protects and improves air quality in NCR and Adjoining Areas (Haryana, Punjab, Rajasthan and Uttar Pradesh). Its orders override those of state governments, CPCB, state PCBs, and others in case of conflict. Chairperson must have at least 15 years in environment protection or 25 years in administration.
evidenceBullets: CAQM is a statutory body under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
Its jurisdiction covers the NCR and adjoining areas in Haryana, Punjab, Rajasthan, and Uttar Pradesh.
CAQM orders override those of state governments, CPCB, and state PCBs in case of conflict.
The Chairperson requires at least 15 years of experience in environment protection or 25 years in administration.

sections.title: Swachh Vayu Sarveshan Award 2025 & NCAP
sections.focus: Award categories, winning cities, and National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) targets.
sections.rationale: Combines the awards and the parent program (NCAP) which share the same context, targets, and implementing framework.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Awards announced by MoEFCC recognizing best-performing cities among 130 NCAP cities. Category 1 (>10 lakh) won by Indore; Category 2 (3-10 lakh) by Amravati; Category 3 (<3 lakh) by Dewas. NCAP (launched 2019) targets a reduction in PM10 level up to 40% or achievement of national ambient air quality standards (60 microgram/m3) by 2025-26 from 2019-20 levels. Implemented by CPCB and monitored via the PRANA portal.
evidenceBullets: Indore ranked 1st in Category 1 (Population >10 lakh) of the Swachh Vayu Sarveshan Award 2025.
NCAP targets up to a 40% reduction in PM10 levels by 2025-26 from 2019-20 levels.
NCAP covers 131 non-attainment and Million Plus Cities across 24 States/UTs.
The PRANA portal monitors the implementation of NCAP.

sections.title: Aerosols and Urban Wind Stilling Effect
sections.focus: Types of aerosols, their climate impacts (Twomey Effect), and the creation of Urban Aerosol Clean Islands.
sections.rationale: Core geography and environment concepts linking aerosols to solar dimming, cloud formation, and urban microclimates.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Aerosols are tiny solid or liquid particles suspended in the atmosphere. Primary aerosols are directly emitted (sea spray, dust); secondary are formed from gases through chemical reactions (sulfates). They act as condensation nuclei, causing cloud droplets to be smaller and longer-lived, leading to solar dimming. The Twomey Effect describes how increased anthropogenic aerosols make clouds brighter by creating more tiny droplets. Urban Wind Stilling Effect creates Urban Aerosol Clean Islands where weakened surface winds due to urban structures block long-range dust and pollutant entry, resulting in lower aerosol levels than nearby rural areas.
evidenceBullets: Secondary aerosols are formed from gases through chemical reactions, such as sulfates from industrial emissions.
The Twomey Effect describes how increased anthropogenic aerosols make clouds brighter by creating more tiny droplets that reflect more sunlight.
Aerosols act as condensation nuclei, causing cloud droplets to be smaller and longer-lived.
The Urban Wind Stilling Effect creates Urban Aerosol Clean Islands by acting as an invisible barrier that slows long-range dust and pollutant entry into cities.

sections.title: Polluted River Stretches and Water Quality Monitoring
sections.focus: CPCB's role in monitoring polluted river stretches and the National Water Quality Monitoring Programme.
sections.rationale: A distinct shift to water pollution at the end of the packet; covers CPCB's assessment report and the NWMP.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) released assessment report on Polluted River Stretches for Restoration of Water Quality - 2025. CPCB executes National Water Quality Monitoring Programme (NWMP) with State Pollution Control Boards/Pollution Control Committees and has been identifying the country's Polluted River Stretches since 2009.
evidenceBullets: CPCB released the Polluted River Stretches for Restoration of Water Quality - 2025 assessment report.
CPCB executes the National Water Quality Monitoring Programme (NWMP) in collaboration with State Pollution Control Boards.

sections.title: Polluted River Stretches (PRS) and Water Quality Criteria
sections.focus: Definition of PRS, Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) criteria, and geographical distribution of polluted stretches in India.
sections.rationale: The criteria for PRS (BOD > 3 mg/L) and the geographical distribution (Maharashtra highest) are highly testable factual points for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Two or more polluted locations on a river in a continuous sequence are considered as stretch and identified as PRS. Criteria: Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) exceeds 3 milligrams/litre (mg/L). BOD is an indicator of water quality, measuring oxygen needed to break down organic matter. Categories: Five Priority Classes (I to V) based on maximum BOD observed... Total number of PRS has decreased from 351 (in 2018) to 296 (in 2025). Geographic Distribution: Maharashtra has highest number (54) of PRS.
evidenceBullets: PRS is identified when two or more polluted locations occur in a continuous sequence on a river.
The primary criterion for identifying a Polluted River Stretch is a Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) exceeding 3 mg/L.
PRS are categorized into Five Priority Classes (I to V) based on the maximum BOD observed.
The total number of PRS in India has decreased from 351 in 2018 to 296 in 2025.
Maharashtra has the highest number of Polluted River Stretches (54) in India.

sections.title: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and Environmental Compensation
sections.focus: Statutory origins, functions, and powers of the CPCB and SPCBs, particularly regarding Environmental Compensation.
sections.rationale: CPCB's statutory backing (Water Act vs Air Act vs EPA) and the Supreme Court ruling on Environmental Compensation are classic UPSC polity/environment overlaps.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Genesis: Statutory body under Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. Entrusted with powers and functions under Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. Ministry: Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change... Power to impose Environmental Compensation (EC): As per Supreme Court ruling, PCBs can impose or collect EC under Water Act, 1974 and Air Act, 1981 in case of environmental damage by erring entity or when damage is imminent, not for every violation.
evidenceBullets: CPCB is a statutory body established under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.
CPCB is also entrusted with powers and functions under the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.
CPCB provides technical services to the Environment Ministry under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) are constituted under the same acts to implement environmental laws within state jurisdictions.
Per a Supreme Court ruling, PCBs can impose Environmental Compensation (EC) under the Water Act and Air Act for environmental damage, which is distinct from punitive action.

sections.title: Framework and Programmes for River Rejuvenation
sections.focus: Legal frameworks and major national programmes (Namami Gange, NMCG, NGRBA, NRCP) for river conservation.
sections.rationale: Institutional structures like NMCG (registered society) and NGRBA (replacing National Ganga Council) are frequent targets for statement-based questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Legal Framework: Water Act, 1974... Environment Protection Act, 1986... River Rejuvenation Programmes: Namami Gange Programme: Superseded Ganga Action Plan (GAP) launched in 1985 implemented by National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) with River basin is the unit of planning and management. NMCG: Registered as a society in 2011 under Societies Registration Act, 1860, acts as the implementation arm of National Ganga River Basin Authority (NGRBA)... NGRBA: Constituted under EPA, 1986 replacing National Ganga Council (chaired by PM) in 2016... Centrally Sponsored Scheme of National River Conservation Plan (NRCP): In identified stretches of rivers, excluding in Ganga basin.
evidenceBullets: The Namami Gange Programme is implemented by the National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG), using the river basin as the unit of planning.
NMCG was registered as a society in 2011 under the Societies Registration Act, 1860.
NMCG acts as the implementation arm of the National Ganga River Basin Authority (NGRBA).
NGRBA was constituted under the Environment Protection Act, 1986, replacing the National Ganga Council in 2016.
The National River Conservation Plan (NRCP) is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme covering identified river stretches excluding the Ganga basin.

sections.title: Desalination Technologies and Processes
sections.focus: Mechanisms, merits, and demerits of Thermal and Membrane desalination technologies, and recent innovations.
sections.rationale: Science & Tech application questions often test the differences between thermal (LTTD) and membrane (RO) technologies, as well as recent indigenous developments.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Recently, IIT Bombay Scientists develop Lotus leaf-like Solar Evaporators for Salt-water Treatment, through a new hydrophobic Graphene-based material... Thermal Technology Concept: Heating of saline water and collecting condensed vapor... Example: Low Temperature Thermal Desalination (LTTD) plants in Kavaratti, Minicoy and Agatti Islands in Lakshadweep. Membrane Technology Concept: Feedwater is pumped through semi-permeable membranes to filter out dissolved solids. Usage: Brackish water desalination... Example: Nemmeli Seawater Desalination Plant, Tamil Nadu.
evidenceBullets: IIT Bombay scientists developed Lotus leaf-like Solar Evaporators using a hydrophobic Graphene-based material for water desalination.
Thermal desalination involves heating saline water and collecting condensed vapor, commonly used for seawater desalination.
Low Temperature Thermal Desalination (LTTD) plants are operational in Lakshadweep islands like Kavaratti, Minicoy, and Agatti.
Membrane technology uses semi-permeable membranes to filter dissolved solids, primarily for brackish water desalination.
Membrane technologies are susceptible to fouling and may not fully remove Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) or pathogens.

sections.title: Revised Classification of Industries by CPCB
sections.focus: The introduction of a new color-coded category for industrial classification by the CPCB.
sections.rationale: The addition of the Blue category to the existing Red, Orange, Green, and White classifications is a specific, testable update.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) directed State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) to adopt revised classification of industries. About Revised Classification: Sectors classified into Red, Orange, Green, White and new Blue category.
evidenceBullets: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has directed SPCBs to adopt a revised classification of industries.
The revised classification introduces a new Blue category alongside the existing Red, Orange, Green, and White categories.

sections.subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
sections.title: Classification of Industries based on Pollution Index (PI)
sections.focus: Criteria, categories, and background of industrial classification by CPCB/MoEFCC.
sections.rationale: Highly examinable for Prelims, particularly matching categories with PI scores and examples, and understanding the specific exemptions for White and Blue categories.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Revised methodology followed based on Pollution Index (PI) - ranges from 0 to 100 with increasing value indicating increased degree of pollution load. PI is determined based on Precautionary Principle... formulated in 2016... Categories: Red (PI > 80): No Red category industries shall normally be permitted in ecologically fragile area. Orange (55 <= PI < 80). Green (25 <= PI < 55). White (PI < 25): Non-polluting; do not require Environmental Clearance (EC) and Consent. Blue: Includes Essential Environmental Services (ESSs) for management of waste... Originated in 1989 with Doon Valley (Uttarakhand) Notification.
evidenceBullets: Pollution Index (PI) ranges from 0 to 100, formulated in 2016 based on the Precautionary Principle evaluating water, air, and hazardous waste.
Red Category (PI > 80) industries are normally not permitted in ecologically fragile or protected areas.
White Category (PI < 25) industries are non-polluting and do not require Environmental Clearance (EC) or Consent.
Blue Category includes Essential Environmental Services (ESSs) like Municipal Solid Waste Management and sewage treatment plants.
The classification of industries originated in 1989 with the Doon Valley Notification issued by MoEFCC.

sections.subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
sections.title: Environment Protection (Management of Contaminated Sites) Rules, 2025
sections.focus: Key provisions, exclusions, and liabilities under the new Contaminated Sites Rules.
sections.rationale: New environmental rules are prime targets for UPSC, especially the specific exclusions (radioactive, mining, oil spills) and the application of the polluter pays principle.
sections.sectionExcerpt: MoEFCC notified Environment Protection (Management of Contaminated Sites) Rules, 2025. Contaminants Covered: Hazardous substances under Hazardous and Other Wastes Rules, 2016. Exclusions: Contamination from radioactive waste, mining, oil spills at sea, and solid waste dumps... Polluter Identification: SPCBs identify the polluter. If land is sold, the new owner is liable. Clean-Up Plan: Polluter must carry out a clean-up plan... Initial assessment costs may be covered by Central Government from Environmental Relief Fund under Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991.
evidenceBullets: Notified by MoEFCC to manage sites polluted by hazardous waste disposal.
Explicitly excludes contamination from radioactive waste, mining, oil spills at sea, and solid waste dumps.
SPCBs identify the polluter; if the contaminated land is sold, the new owner assumes liability.
The polluter must execute and fund a clean-up plan through an approved agency.
Initial assessment costs may be covered by the Environmental Relief Fund under the Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991.

sections.subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
