sections.title: Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Amendment Rules, 2025
sections.focus: Introduction of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) for non-ferrous metals and recycling targets.
sections.rationale: EPR frameworks are frequently tested. The specific inclusion of non-ferrous metals, the progressive targets, and the certificate validity are key testable facts.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Amendment Rules, 2025 were notified by MoEFCC... Introduces comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for Scrap of Non-Ferrous Metals (including aluminium, copper and zinc and its alloys)... Targets: Every producer shall be responsible for recycling target structure, which progressively increases from 10% in 2026-2027 to 75% by 2032-2033. Central Pollution Control Board: Shall generate an EPR certificate... Validity of the EPR certificate: Two years from the end of financial year when generated.
evidenceBullets: Introduces an Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework specifically for Scrap of Non-Ferrous Metals like aluminium, copper, and zinc.
Recycling targets progressively increase from 10% in 2026-2027 to 75% by 2032-2033.
The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) is responsible for generating EPR certificates through a portal.
EPR certificates are valid for two years from the end of the financial year in which they are generated.

sections.subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
sections.title: WHO Global Conference on Air Pollution and Health
sections.focus: Objectives and India's commitments at the WHO Global Conference.
sections.rationale: International conferences and India's specific targets (like NCAP) are standard Prelims material.
sections.sectionExcerpt: WHO Global Conference on Air Pollution and Health: Co-organized by WHO and Columbia and other UN Agencies like UNEP, WMO etc. Objectives: Accelerating action for clean air, clean energy access and climate change mitigation. Key Highlights: Over 50 countries, committed to shared goal of reducing health impacts of air pollution by 50% by 2040. India reaffirmed its commitment by 2040 under National Clean Air Programme.
evidenceBullets: Co-organized by WHO, Columbia, UNEP, and WMO to accelerate action for clean air and climate change mitigation.
Over 50 countries committed to reducing the health impacts of air pollution by 50% by 2040.
India reaffirmed its 2040 commitment under the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP).

sections.subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
sections.title: Smog Eating Technology
sections.focus: Mechanism and application of photocatalytic coatings for pollution reduction.
sections.rationale: Science and Technology applied to Environment is a high-yield UPSC topic. The use of TiO2 and UV light is a highly testable mechanism.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Smog Eating Technology: Delhi Government ordered study on use of Smog Eating photocatalytic coatings on roads. Objective: Reduce nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and harmful hydrocarbons in public spaces... Mechanism - Photocatalytic activity: Uses light energy to break down harmful pollutants & organic waste. Usually uses titanium dioxide (TiO2) coatings as photocatalysis under UV light to decompose NOx.
evidenceBullets: Delhi Government is studying Smog Eating photocatalytic coatings on roads to reduce NO2 and hydrocarbons.
The technology uses light energy (photocatalytic activity) to break down harmful pollutants and organic waste.
It typically utilizes titanium dioxide (TiO2) coatings under UV light to decompose nitrogen oxides (NOx).

sections.subtopic: Industry Classification and Hazardous Waste Rules
sections.title: Global Outlook Council on Water
sections.focus: Launch and association of the Global Outlook Council on Water.
sections.rationale: Short factual snippet about a new international initiative linked to the G20, suitable for a statement in a multi-statement question.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Global Outlook Council on Water: The President of South Africa announced its launch as a G20 Presidential Legacy Initiative.
evidenceBullets: Launched by the President of South Africa.
Established as a G20 Presidential Legacy Initiative.

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: Global Observatory on Water Investments (GOCWI)
sections.focus: Global Water Partnership's initiative to mobilize financial momentum for water security.
sections.rationale: A distinct international initiative focused on water investments and security, suitable for a standalone factual question.
sections.sectionExcerpt: It forms a core pillar of the Global Water Partnership’s (GWP) Global Transformation Agenda on Water Investments. GWP is a multi-stakeholder action network and intergovernmental organisation dedicated to equitable, sustainable, and efficient management of water resources.
evidenceBullets: Forms a core pillar of the Global Water Partnership's (GWP) Global Transformation Agenda.
GWP is an intergovernmental organisation and multi-stakeholder network.
Aims to mobilise 15 billion for water security by 2030.
Serves as a high-level platform to sustain political and financial momentum for water investments.

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: River Cities Alliance (RCA)
sections.focus: Institutional framework and initiatives for river-sensitive urban planning in Indian cities.
sections.rationale: A major national initiative involving multiple ministries and specific frameworks like the Urban River Management Plans (URMP), highly relevant for UPSC governance and environment sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Joint initiative of Ministry of Jal Shakti and Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, launched in 2021. Objective: Strengthen institutional capacity, inter-city collaboration, and support Urban River Management Plans (URMPs).
evidenceBullets: Joint initiative of the Ministry of Jal Shakti and Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs launched in 2021.
Currently has 145 member cities, and any river city can join at any time.
Urban River Management Plan (URMP) Framework was launched in 2020 by NIUA and NMCG.
Chhatrapati Sambhaji Nagar’s Kham River Restoration Mission was globally recognised by the WRI Ross Centre Prize for Cities.

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: Dumpsite Remediation Accelerator Programme (DRAP)
sections.focus: SBM-U 2.0 initiative targeting legacy waste management and dumpsite remediation.
sections.rationale: Covers critical urban waste management schemes, specific targets (Lakshya Zero Dumpsites), and environmental technologies (Biocapping, Biomining).
sections.sectionExcerpt: It was launched under the Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban (SBM-U) 2.0. It is a year-long, targeted initiative to achieve the goals of Lakshya Zero Dumpsites by September 2026. Objective: Prioritize high-impact locations, covering ~8.8 crore MT of legacy waste.
evidenceBullets: Launched under Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban (SBM-U) 2.0 by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA).
Aims to achieve Lakshya Zero Dumpsites by September 2026.
Prioritizes sites containing over 45,000 MT of legacy wastes, with no minimum threshold for UTs and North Eastern States.
Key technologies include Biocapping (transforming dumpsites into green zones) and Biomining (using microorganisms to extract economic materials).

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: National Level Pollution Response Exercise (NATPOLREX-X)
sections.focus: Indian Coast Guard's exercise for marine oil spill preparedness.
sections.rationale: A specific defense and environmental response exercise, easily testable as a factual match-the-following or statement-based question.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Indian Coast Guard (ICG) conducted its 10th edition. A biennial flagship exercise which aims to evaluate and enhance India’s national preparedness to respond to marine oil spill incidents.
evidenceBullets: Conducted by the Indian Coast Guard (ICG).
It is a biennial flagship exercise.
Aims to evaluate and enhance India's preparedness to respond to marine oil spill incidents.

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: Ground-Level Ozone (GLO)
sections.focus: Formation mechanisms, sources, and impacts of Ground-Level Ozone.
sections.rationale: A highly testable environmental chemistry concept. UPSC frequently asks about the precursors (NOx and VOCs) and impacts of ground-level ozone.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Secondary, short-lived pollutant that remains in atmosphere for only hours to weeks. Created by chemical reactions between oxides of nitrogen (Nox) and volatile organic compounds (VOC).
evidenceBullets: It is a secondary, short-lived pollutant.
Created by chemical reactions between oxides of nitrogen (NOx) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs).
Natural sources include soil-based NOx emissions, wildfire-induced CO, and biospheric methane.
Safe eight-hourly ozone standard is 100 micrograms/cubic metre.
Worsens bronchitis, triggers asthma, and is a major component of smog.

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: Secondary Pollutants
sections.focus: Definition, formation, and examples of secondary atmospheric pollutants.
sections.rationale: Core environmental science concept distinguishing primary from secondary pollutants, supported by recent CREA study findings.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Formed in atmosphere due to chemical/physical interactions between primary pollutants themselves or between primary pollutants and other atmospheric components. Examples: Ozone (O3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3).
evidenceBullets: Account for nearly one-third of India's PM2.5 pollution according to a CREA study.
Formed by interactions between primary pollutants, sunlight, and atmospheric conditions.
Examples include Ozone (O3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), and nitric acid (HNO3).

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: Toxic Heavy Metals
sections.focus: Characteristics, sources, and specific health impacts of heavy metal pollution.
sections.rationale: Heavy metal pollution (Lead, Arsenic, Cadmium) and their specific physiological impacts are recurring themes in UPSC Prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Heavy metals/metalloids are nondegradable and accumulate over decadal timescales in soils. Characteristics: High atomic weight ranging from 63.5 to 200.6 and density >4000 kg/m3.
evidenceBullets: Characterized by high atomic weight and density greater than 4000 kg/m3.
They are nondegradable and bioaccumulate in soils over decadal timescales.
Cadmium exposure can cause kidney and bone damage, and lung cancer.
Lead exposure causes developmental and neurobehavioral effects on fetuses.
Arsenic is highly toxic in its inorganic form and can cause skin lesions, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes.

sections.subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
sections.title: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS)
sections.focus: Properties, applications, and environmental persistence of PFAS.
sections.rationale: Emerging pollutants of high global concern ('Forever Chemicals'). Their chemical properties and everyday applications make them highly relevant for Science & Tech and Environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Chemicals having carbon-fluorine bonds, which are very strong and do not degrade easily. They can resist grease, oil, water, and heat. Impact: Persists in environment (known as ‘Forever Chemicals’).
evidenceBullets: Contain very strong carbon-fluorine bonds that do not degrade easily.
Known as 'Forever Chemicals' due to their persistence in the environment.
They resist grease, oil, water, and heat.
Commonly used in cookware, food packaging, fabrics, and foams.
Researchers recently discovered nearly 20 species of bacteria capable of breaking them down.

sections.subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
sections.title: Green Firecrackers
sections.focus: Composition, certification, and types of green firecrackers (SWAS, SAFAL, STAR).
sections.rationale: High probability for UPSC prelims due to specific chemical compositions (absence of barium, use of potassium nitrate) and acronyms (SWAS, SAFAL, STAR) that are frequently tested.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Easing temporarily the blanket ban on firecrackers in Delhi-NCR, Supreme Court allowed use of government-approved green crackers devoid of harmful chemicals and reducing air pollution. About Green Firecrackers: Composition: Low Thermite amount, minimum usage of Aluminium. Does not contain barium substance, used in firecrackers to add green colour. Uses Potassium Nitrate as oxidant. Benefit: Reduce at least 30 % emissions using particulate matter. Identification and Certification: CSIR NEERI Logo. Types of Green Firecrackers: SWAS (Safe Water & Air Releaser): Emit very fine water droplets that absorb dust. SAFAL (Safe Minimal Aluminium): Contain safe amount of aluminium & are quieter. STAR (Safe Thermite Cracker): Free from potassium nitrate and sulfur; emit minimal smoke.
evidenceBullets: Green firecrackers do not contain barium, which is conventionally used to add green color.
They use Potassium Nitrate as an oxidant and have low thermite and minimal aluminium.
They reduce particulate matter emissions by at least 30%.
They are identified and certified by the CSIR NEERI logo.
SWAS (Safe Water & Air Releaser) emits fine water droplets to absorb dust.
STAR (Safe Thermite Cracker) is free from potassium nitrate and sulfur.

sections.subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
sections.title: Flue Gas Desulphurisation (FGD)
sections.focus: Mechanism and policy regarding FGD in coal-fired power plants.
sections.rationale: Important industrial pollution control technology. UPSC often asks about specific pollutants targeted (SO2) and the chemical reagents used in such processes.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A study commissioned by Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser recommended rollback of 2015 policy mandating installation of FGD systems in all of India's coal-fired plants. Rather, it recommended FGD to only those plants using imported coal or high (>0.5%) sulphur coal. About FGD: Involves removal of SO2 (Sulphur Dioxide) in exhaust gases from coal-fired power plants before being released to atmosphere. Uses scrubbing technique involving alkaline reagent (typically a sodium- or calcium-based alkaline reagent). Types of FGD Systems: Dry Sorbent Injection, Wet Limestone Based, Sea Water Based, etc.
evidenceBullets: FGD involves the removal of Sulphur Dioxide (SO2) from the exhaust gases of coal-fired power plants.
It uses a scrubbing technique that typically involves a sodium- or calcium-based alkaline reagent.
A recent study recommended limiting the FGD mandate to plants using imported or high-sulphur (>0.5%) coal rather than all plants.

sections.subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
sections.title: Taj Trapezium Zone (TTZ)
sections.focus: Geographical coverage, statutory backing, and regulatory framework of the TTZ.
sections.rationale: Combines geography (heritage sites), environment (pollution control), and statutory bodies (EPA 1986), making it a classic UPSC multi-statement question candidate.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Supreme Court directed NEERI to assess the impact of neighbouring glass industry on Taj Mahal. SC in past issued orders from time to time for TTZ. About TTZ: Location: 10,400 sq km area (shaped like trapezoid) around Taj Mahal for preventing its pollution. Coverage: Three World Heritage Sites- Taj Mahal, Agra Fort, and Fatehpur Sikri. Taj Trapezium Zone Pollution (Prevention and Control) Authority: Under Environment Protection Act, 1986. Categories of Industries: Red, Green, Orange, and White based on pollution potential, as defined by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB).
evidenceBullets: The TTZ covers a 10,400 sq km trapezoid-shaped area to prevent pollution around the Taj Mahal.
It encompasses three World Heritage Sites: Taj Mahal, Agra Fort, and Fatehpur Sikri.
The TTZ Pollution Authority is established under the Environment Protection Act, 1986.
Industries in the zone are categorized into Red, Green, Orange, and White by the CPCB based on their pollution potential.

sections.subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
sections.title: Environmental Flow (E-Flow)
sections.focus: Definition and ecological significance of E-Flow in river systems.
sections.rationale: Conceptual topic related to river ecology and hydrology. Good for a single conceptual or definitional question.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Union Jal Shakti Minister led a meeting focused on (E-flow) of Ganga River and its tributaries. About E-Flow: Refers to quantity, timing, and quality of water flow required to sustain freshwater ecosystems and livelihoods that depend on them. Benefits: Maintains ecological integrity of rivers and their estuaries. Extensive interventions in river flow like construction of dams, pollution, and encroachments have severely impacted ecological balance of rivers.
evidenceBullets: E-Flow refers to the quantity, timing, and quality of water flow needed to sustain freshwater ecosystems and dependent livelihoods.
It is crucial for maintaining the ecological integrity of rivers and their estuaries against interventions like dams and encroachments.

sections.subtopic: Green Firecrackers and Air Quality Reports
sections.title: Air Quality and Pollution Reports
sections.focus: Key findings and publishing bodies of recent environmental reports (SoGA, AQLI, UNCTAD).
sections.rationale: UPSC frequently tests the publishing agencies and key findings of major global environmental indices and reports.
sections.sectionExcerpt: State of Global Air Report (SoGA) 2025 Released by: Health Effects Institute (HEI) and Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME), USA. Mortality: Around 2 million deaths in 2023 linked to air pollution contributing 52% global share. Ozone Pollution: India has third highest exposure. 2025 Annual Update of the Air Quality Life Index (AQLI) Developer: Michael Greenstone and others at Energy Policy Institute at University of Chicago (EPIC). Measures the impact of particulate air pollution on life expectancy. Key Highlights: Air pollution meeting WHO guidelines could extend Indian residents' lives by 3.5 years on average, with Delhi seeing an 8.2-year gain. South Asia continues to be World's most polluted region. Global Trade Update on Mobilising trade to curb Plastic Pollution Released by: UNCTAD.
evidenceBullets: The State of Global Air Report (SoGA) 2025 is released by the Health Effects Institute (HEI) and IHME (USA).
According to SoGA 2025, India has the third highest exposure to Ozone Pollution globally.
The Air Quality Life Index (AQLI) is developed by the Energy Policy Institute at the University of Chicago (EPIC) and measures particulate pollution's impact on life expectancy.
Meeting WHO air quality guidelines could extend Delhi residents' lives by 8.2 years according to the AQLI.
The Global Trade Update on Mobilising trade to curb Plastic Pollution is released by UNCTAD.

sections.subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
sections.title: 10 Years of SDGs: Progress and Related Reports
sections.focus: India's and global progress on SDGs after 10 years, key achievements, and major global/national reports tracking SDG implementation.
sections.rationale: This section covers the foundational knowledge of SDGs, recent index rankings (India breaking into the top 100), and crucial reports released by UN bodies and NITI Aayog, which are highly testable in UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) completed 10 years of adoption since September 2015. In 2015, all United Nations countries adopted 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. Sets out 17 SDGs and 169 targets. For the first time, India ranks among top 100 in SDG Index at 99 in 2025 with a score of 67 out of 100. Sustainable Development Report (SDR) 2025 Released by: SDG Transformation Centre, under Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN). Sustainable Development Goals Report (SDGR) 2025 Prepared by: UN DESA. SDG India Index: Released by: NITI Aayog. Voluntary National Review (VNR) Report 2025: Released by: NITI Aayog.
evidenceBullets: SDGs were adopted in 2015 (17 goals, 169 targets), succeeding the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
India ranked 99th (score 67/100) in the 10th Sustainable Development Report (SDR) 2025, entering the top 100 for the first time.
The Sustainable Development Report (SDR) is released by the UN Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN).
The Sustainable Development Goals Report (SDGR) is the only official UN report monitoring global progress, prepared by UN DESA.
NITI Aayog releases the SDG India Index, District SDG Index for NER, and India's Voluntary National Review (VNR) Report.
MoSPI launched the National Indicator Framework (NIF) Progress Report 2025.

sections.subtopic: SDG Progress and Financing for Development
sections.title: Financing for Sustainable Development and the Sevilla Commitment
sections.focus: The outcomes of the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development (FFD4) and the new financing mechanisms introduced under the Sevilla Commitment.
sections.rationale: The Sevilla Commitment introduces several new economic and environmental financing mechanisms (Debt swaps, SCALED, Solidarity Levies) which are prime targets for UPSC economy and environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Recently, the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development (FFD4) adopted the final outcome document Compromiso de Sevilla, also known as Sevilla Commitment, to address the SDG financing gap in developing countries. Adopted by consensus, lays out a path to close the $4 trillion annual SDG financing gap. United States decided to exit the process entirely. New financing mechanisms announced: Debt-for-Development Swap Programme, Debt Swaps for Development Hub (led by Spain and the World Bank), Blended finance platform SCALED, Coalition for Global Solidarity Levies (led by France, Kenya and Barbados) aims to tax premium-class flying and private jets.
evidenceBullets: The Sevilla Commitment was adopted at the 4th International Conference on Financing for Development (FFD4) to close the $4 trillion annual SDG financing gap.
The United States exited the Sevilla Commitment process entirely.
Debt-for-Development Swap Programme cancels or reduces debt in exchange for government spending on development goals.
SCALED is a blended finance platform combining public, philanthropic, and private capital for sustainable development.
Coalition for Global Solidarity Levies (led by France, Kenya, Barbados) aims to tax premium-class flying and private jets for climate action.
Debt Swaps for Development Hub is led by Spain and the World Bank to scale up debt swaps and lower debt service burdens.

sections.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
sections.title: Evolution of Financing for Sustainable Development
sections.focus: Key international agreements and frameworks for sustainable development financing including Monterrey, Doha, and Addis Ababa.
sections.rationale: Provides a distinct historical timeline of UN financing frameworks, which is highly testable for international environmental and development conventions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Monterrey Consensus (2002) - Marked beginning of Financing for Development process at United Nations. It focused on increased Official Development Assistance (ODA)... Doha Declaration (2008) - It reaffirmed Monterrey Consensus... laid the foundation for Green Climate Fund. Addis Ababa Action Agenda (2015) - It introduced new financing frameworks for sustainable development such as Integrated National Financing Frameworks (INFFs), Technology Bank for Least Developed Countries etc.
evidenceBullets: The Monterrey Consensus (2002) marked the beginning of the UN Financing for Development process, focusing on ODA and governance reforms.
The Doha Declaration (2008) laid the foundation for the Green Climate Fund and added gender and environment understandings to financing.
The Addis Ababa Action Agenda (2015) introduced Integrated National Financing Frameworks (INFFs) and the Technology Bank for Least Developed Countries.

sections.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
sections.title: India's Renewable Energy Sector Status and Targets
sections.focus: India's RE milestones, NDC targets, Panchamrita goals, and current installed capacity statistics.
sections.rationale: Core UPSC syllabus topic containing critical data points such as achieving 50% non-fossil capacity 5 years ahead of schedule and global rankings.
sections.sectionExcerpt: In 2025 India's renewable energy sector achieved two historic milestones - renewable energy met 51.5% of total electricity demand (July 2025) and share of installed electricity capacity of non-fossil fuel sources reached 51% (September 2025). Achieving 50% of cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy resources by 2030 (achieved five years ahead)... Installed electricity capacity of non-fossil fuel sources: 256.09 GW... Global Ranking: 4th in Renewable Energy Installed Capacity.
evidenceBullets: India achieved its NDC target of 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuels five years ahead of the 2030 deadline.
Panchamrita targets for 2030 include meeting 50% of energy requirements from RE and reaching 500 GW of non-fossil energy capacity.
Solar holds the largest share (39.4%) of India's non-fossil fuel installed capacity, followed by Wind (10.6%) and Hydro (10%).
India ranks 4th globally in Renewable Energy Installed Capacity and Wind Power, and 3rd in Solar Power capacity.
The top 5 renewable energy states in India are Rajasthan, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Maharashtra.

sections.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
sections.title: International Solar Alliance (ISA) and Recent Initiatives
sections.focus: ISA's 8th Session highlights, SUNRISE initiative, membership rules, and the 'Towards 1000' strategy.
sections.rationale: ISA is a major India-led international organization. The 'Towards 1000' strategy, SUNRISE initiative, and universal UN member eligibility are prime targets for prelims statements.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Mauritius Became 1st African Country to sign ISA's CPF... Launch of SUNRISE (Solar Upcycling Network for Recycling, Innovation & Stakeholder Engagement) - a global initiative on circularity and end-of-life (EoL) management of solar PV and batteries... Following 2020 amendment to its Framework Agreement, all UN members are now eligible to join. Mission: Guided by its Towards 1000 strategy, which aims to Mobilize USD 1,000 billions of investments... Deliver energy access to 1,000 million people...
evidenceBullets: Mauritius became the first African country to sign the ISA's Country Partnership Framework (CPF).
SUNRISE is a global initiative launched at the 8th ISA Assembly focusing on circularity and end-of-life management of solar PV and batteries.
Following a 2020 amendment, all UN members are eligible to join the ISA, which currently has 104 members that have signed and ratified the agreement.

ISA's 'Towards 1000' strategy aims to mobilize USD 1,000 billion in investments, deliver energy access to 1,000 million people, install 1,000 GW of solar capacity, and mitigate 1,000 million tonnes of CO2 annually by 2030.
Key ISA initiatives include the SolarX Startup Challenge, STAR-C Initiative, Global Solar Facility, and Green Hydrogen Innovation Centre.

sections.subtopic: India's RE Status and International Solar Alliance
sections.title: Solar Panel Efficiency Norms and ALMM
sections.focus: MNRE's easing of efficiency norms for off-grid rural solar projects and the Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (ALMM).
sections.rationale: Policy changes regarding ALMM and Distributed Renewable Energy (DRE) are highly relevant for schemes and rural electrification topics.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) eased solar panel efficiency norms for off-grid rural projects allowing lower efficiency modules to be included in Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (ALMM)... New Standard: Both technologies at 18% efficiency. Scope of Change: Applies only to off-grid solar applications below 200W like Solar lanterns, Micro solar grids... Introduction of Distributed Renewable Energy (DRE) category: To support smaller manufacturers & Wider rural electrification.
evidenceBullets: MNRE eased solar panel efficiency norms to 18% for both Crystalline silicon and Cadmium Telluride technologies for off-grid rural projects below 200W.
The Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (ALMM) is an MNRE-approved list of solar PV modules and manufacturers.
The relaxed efficiency norms apply only to off-grid applications like solar lanterns and micro solar grids, and do not affect larger systems like rooftop solar or pumps.
A new Distributed Renewable Energy (DRE) category was introduced to support smaller manufacturers and promote wider rural electrification.

sections.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
sections.title: Solar Power Technologies and Agriphotovoltaics (APVs)
sections.focus: Mechanisms of solar power generation and the dual-use model of Agriphotovoltaics.
sections.rationale: Covers foundational solar energy technologies and the specific, highly testable concept of Agriphotovoltaics (APVs) which bridges agriculture and renewable energy.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Solar power utilizes photovoltaic cells to produce direct current (DC), which is converted to alternating current (AC) by an inverter. Other technologies include Solar Thermal Energy for heat and Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) using mirrors and lenses. Agriphotovoltaics (APVs) or Solar Farming combines agricultural production and electricity generation on the same land, utilizing solar panels, tracking systems, and shade-tolerant crops to maximize land-use efficiency.
evidenceBullets: Photovoltaic cells produce direct current (DC) which is converted to alternating current (AC) by an inverter.
Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) harnesses energy by focusing sunlight using mirrors, lenses, and tracking devices.
Agriphotovoltaics (APVs) combine agricultural production and electricity production on the same land area.
APV systems utilize shade-tolerant crops to protect against strong solar irradiation and winds.

sections.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
sections.title: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) and Certification Scheme
sections.focus: Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI), hydrogen color typology, and NGHM targets.
sections.rationale: A major national mission with specific targets, nodal agencies, certification criteria, and a highly testable color-coded hydrogen typology.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Centre launched the Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI) under NGHM, with MNRE as the ministry and the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) as the nodal authority. 'Green' certification requires an emission intensity of 2 kg CO2eq/kg H2 or less. Green hydrogen is produced via electrolysis using renewable energy. The text details various hydrogen types including Turquoise (Pyrolysis of Methane) and Pink (Electrolysis via Nuclear). NGHM targets 5 MMT per annum by 2030 and includes the SIGHT programme and Green Hydrogen Hubs like Deendayal, V.O. Chidambaranar, and Paradip ports.
evidenceBullets: The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) is the Nodal Authority for the Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India (GHCI).
Green Certification is conditional on meeting an average emission intensity requirement of 2 kg CO2eq/kg H2 or less.
Turquoise Hydrogen is produced through Pyrolysis using Methane, while Pink Hydrogen uses Electrolysis powered by Nuclear Energy.
NGHM targets the production of 5 MMT per annum of Green Hydrogen by 2030.
Deendayal Port, V.O. Chidambaranar Port, and Paradip Port have been recognized as Green Hydrogen Hubs under NGHM.
Green hydrogen production requires up to 9 litres of water per kg of hydrogen and demands high electricity.

sections.subtopic: Solar Technologies and Green Hydrogen Mission
sections.title: National Bioenergy Programme (NBP) and Revised Guidelines
sections.focus: Objectives, components, and revised financial assistance guidelines of the National Bioenergy Programme.
sections.rationale: Important scheme for waste-to-energy and biomass utilization, featuring specific financial assistance criteria and components relevant to renewable energy and rural development.
sections.sectionExcerpt: MNRE updated guidelines for the National Bioenergy Programme (NBP), launched in 2022 to utilize surplus biomass for power generation. It provides Central Financial Assistance (CFA), with 20% extra for special categories like the North East Region and SC/ST beneficiaries. The NBP has three components: Waste to Energy (using pyrolysis and plasma gasification), Biomass (briquettes/pellets), and Biogas (95% methane and CO2). Revised guidelines offer easier approvals, two-stage CFA disbursal, IoT monitoring, and stubble burning support.
evidenceBullets: The National Bioenergy Programme provides 20% more Central Financial Assistance (CFA) for special categories like the North East Region, hilly states, and SC/ST beneficiaries.
The Waste to Energy Programme supports projects generating Biogas, BioCNG, Power, or Syngas through technologies like pyrolysis and plasma gasification.
Biogas is composed of 95% methane (CH4) and CO2, with traces of N2, H2, H2S, and O2.
Revised guidelines for Waste to Energy introduce a two-stage CFA release: 50% after State Pollution Control Board consent and the remainder after 80% capacity achievement.
The Biomass Programme supports stubble burning mitigation by allowing pellet producers in NCR to choose between MNRE or CPCB schemes.

sections.title: Ethanol Blended Petrol (EBP) Programme and Ethanol Properties
sections.focus: India's ethanol blending targets, properties of ethanol, feedstocks allowed under the National Policy on Biofuels, and government initiatives promoting blending.
sections.rationale: The EBP programme is a flagship initiative with updated targets (20% by 2025-26). Ethanol's chemical properties, by-products (DDGS), and India's global standing are highly relevant for Prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The EBP Programme, launched in 2003, aims to mix ethanol with petrol for cleaner fuel. The National Policy on Biofuels advanced the 20% blending target (E20) to 2025-26. Ethanol is a renewable, high-octane fuel produced via fermentation of sugars or petrochemical processes, but is 27% less energy-dense than petrol. India is the world's third-largest producer and consumer of ethanol. Initiatives include PM JIVAN Yojana for 2G ethanol, Ethanol Interest Subvention Schemes, and GST reduction to 5%.
evidenceBullets: Target of 20% ethanol blending in petrol was advanced to 2025-26 from 2030 under the National Policy on Biofuels.
Ethanol is 27% less energy-dense than petrol but has a high-octane rating.
India is the world's third-largest producer and consumer of ethanol, after the USA and Brazil.
Distillers' dried grains (DDGS) are a by-product of grain ethanol reused as animal feed.
PM JIVAN Yojana facilitates Second Generation (2G) ethanol projects.
GST on ethanol meant for the EBP Programme was reduced from 18% to 5%.

sections.title: Generations of Biofuels: 1G to 4G
sections.focus: Classification of biofuels into first, second, third, and fourth generations based on their feedstocks.
sections.rationale: UPSC frequently asks about the classification of biofuels and the specific feedstocks that fall under 1G, 2G, and 3G categories.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Biofuels are categorized into four generations based on their feedstock. 1st Generation uses edible feedstocks like wheat, corn, sugarcane, and food-grade oils. 2nd Generation utilizes lignocellulosic biomass and waste, such as wheat bran, animal fats, used cooking oil, and Jatropha. 3rd Generation is derived from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass. 4th Generation involves genetically engineered microorganisms like Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
evidenceBullets: 1st Generation biofuels use edible feedstocks like wheat, corn, sugarcane, and palm oil.
2nd Generation biofuels use non-edible lignocellulosic biomass and waste, including Jatropha curcas and used cooking oil.
3rd Generation biofuels are produced from microalgae and cyanobacteria biomass.
4th Generation biofuels utilize genetically engineered microorganisms such as Escherichia coli.

sections.title: Alternative Biofuels: Bioethanol, E-Methanol, and Compressed Biogas (CBG)
sections.focus: Recent developments, production processes, and applications of Bioethanol (bamboo-based), E-Methanol, and CBG.
sections.rationale: Emerging alternative fuels like E-Methanol and CBG are critical for India's energy transition. The specific composition of CBG and the production process of E-Methanol are prime targets for science and technology questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: India's first Bamboo-based Bio-Ethanol Plant was inaugurated in Assam; bamboo is a 2G ethanol feedstock due to high lignocellulose. The world's first commercial E-Methanol plant opened in Denmark. E-Methanol is produced by combining green hydrogen (from water electrolysis) with captured CO2. NITI Aayog's 'Methanol Economy' Programme promotes its use. India's First Cooperative Multi-Feed CBG Plant opened in Maharashtra. CBG is prepared from raw biogas (anaerobic digestion of biomass), which mainly contains 55-60% Methane and 35-40% Carbon dioxide.
evidenceBullets: Bamboo is an attractive 2G ethanol feedstock due to its high lignocellulose content and non-food nature.
E-Methanol is a low-carbon fuel produced by combining green hydrogen with captured carbon dioxide.
NITI Aayog spearheads the 'Methanol Economy' Programme in India, promoting blending of DME (Di-methyl Ether) in LPG.
Raw biogas is produced by anaerobic digestion of biomass and waste sources.
Biogas primarily consists of Methane (55-60%) and Carbon dioxide (35-40%), along with impurities like H2S.

sections.title: Compressed Biogas (CBG) Initiatives
sections.focus: Purification process of raw biogas and key Indian schemes promoting CBG.
sections.rationale: A brief but distinct topic covering the technical definition of CBG and two major schemes (GOBAR-DHAN, SATAT) that are highly relevant for UPSC environment and energy questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Raw biogas is purified to remove H2S, CO2, and water vapor, increasing methane concentration to 90%, and is then compressed to about 200-250 bar pressure to get CBG. Initiatives taken in India to promote CBG include the GOBAR-DHAN scheme to convert cattle dung and solid waste to Bio-CNG and compost, and the SATAT initiative for setting up CBG plants.
evidenceBullets: CBG production requires purifying raw biogas to 90% methane and compressing it to 200-250 bar pressure.
The GOBAR-DHAN scheme focuses on converting cattle dung and farm solid waste into Bio-CNG (CBG) and compost.
The SATAT initiative aims to set up Compressed Biogas (CBG) plants for affordable transportation.

sections.title: Geothermal Energy and National Policy (NPGE, 2025)
sections.focus: Geothermal energy basics, India's potential and provinces, and the National Policy on Geothermal Energy 2025.
sections.rationale: A major topic with high examinability for UPSC, covering geographical locations (Puga, Chumathang, 10 provinces), technical details (enthalpy), and comprehensive policy frameworks.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Geothermal energy harnesses Earth's crust heat. High-enthalpy (~200°C) resources suit electricity generation; low-medium (100-180°C) suit direct use. India's potential is 10,600 MW. GSI identified 381 hot springs and 10 provinces (Himalayan, Naga-Lushai, Andaman are orogenic). Puga and Chumathang in Ladakh are promising. NPGE 2025 (MNRE) aims to make geothermal a major renewable pillar for net-zero by 2070. It covers EGS, AGS, abandoned oil wells, and mineral by-products (silica, lithium). Financing includes RE-RTD (up to 100% for govt, 70% for industry). Recent steps include SCCL's 20 kW pilot in Telangana and Northeast's 1st well at Dirang, Arunachal Pradesh.
evidenceBullets: High-enthalpy geothermal resources (~200°C) are suitable for electricity generation, while low-to-medium (100-180°C) are for direct use.
India's estimated geothermal potential is 10,600 MW, with Puga and Chumathang in Ladakh being the most promising sites.
India has 10 Geothermal Provinces; Himalayan, Naga-Lushai, and Andaman Nicobar are orogenic, while the rest are non-orogenic.
NPGE 2025 allows extracting geothermal energy from abandoned oil/gas wells and recovering mineral by-products like lithium and cesium under the MMDR Act.
Exploration leases for geothermal can be granted for 3-5 years, and development leases for up to 30 years.
Northeast's first geothermal well is located at Dirang, Arunachal Pradesh, developed with Norway and Iceland.

sections.title: Wind Energy and ALMM (Wind)
sections.focus: Updates to the wind turbine approval system and India's wind energy sector statistics.
sections.rationale: Covers the transition from RLMM to ALMM (Wind) and key statistics about India's wind energy capacity and potential, which are standard fodder for prelims statements.
sections.sectionExcerpt: MNRE updated its wind turbine approval system, renaming RLMM to Approved List of Models and Manufacturers (Wind) or ALMM (Wind). It mandates sourcing key components from approved Indian suppliers and data localization. India ranks 4th globally in installed wind power capacity, contributing 4.69% to total electricity. Wind potential is 1164 GW at 150 meters above ground level.
evidenceBullets: MNRE updated the wind turbine approval system from RLMM to ALMM (Wind).
ALMM Wind mandates sourcing key components from approved Indian suppliers and requires data localization.
India ranks 4th in the world for installed wind power capacity.
India's wind potential is estimated at 1164 GW at 150 meters above ground level by the National Institute of Wind Energy.

sections.title: Draft Guidelines for Underground Coal Gasification (UCG)
sections.focus: Draft guidelines for UCG mining and mine closure plans.
sections.rationale: A very brief section introducing the draft guidelines for UCG and the specific requirement of an escrow account with the CCO.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Draft Guidelines for UCG deal with the preparation of mining and mine closure plans for UCG blocks. To ensure scientific mine closure, companies will be required to maintain an escrow account with the Coal Controller Organization (CCO).
evidenceBullets: Draft Guidelines for UCG require companies to maintain an escrow account with the Coal Controller Organization (CCO) to ensure scientific mine closure.

sections.title: Coal Gasification and Coal Controller's Organization (CCO)
sections.focus: CCO's mandate, Syngas composition, and the Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS).
sections.rationale: A distinct concept cluster covering coal administration and gasification technology/schemes before the shift to energy efficiency.
sections.sectionExcerpt: CCO collects coal production data and acts as the appellate authority for disputes over coal grade and size. Coal Gasification converts coal into syngas (CO, H2, CO2, CH4). The Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS) launched in 2024 aims to achieve 100 million tonnes of coal gasification by 2030.
evidenceBullets: CCO acts as the appellate authority for disputes between consumers and owners regarding the declaration of coal grade and size.
Syngas is a mixture of carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen (H2), carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and other gases.
The Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS) aims to achieve 100 million tonnes of coal gasification by 2030.

sections.title: ADEETIE Scheme
sections.focus: Objectives, targets, and financial components of the ADEETIE scheme for MSMEs.
sections.rationale: A standalone Ministry of Power scheme targeting energy efficiency specifically in MSMEs.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Assistance in Deploying Energy Efficient Technologies in Industries & Establishments (ADEETIE) helps MSMEs reduce energy consumption by 30-50%. Implemented by BEE under the Ministry of Power for 3 years (FY26-FY28) with a ₹1000 crore outlay. It offers 5% interest subvention for Micro/Small and 3% for Medium enterprises.
evidenceBullets: ADEETIE is implemented by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) under the Ministry of Power.
It targets MSMEs with a Udyam ID to reduce energy consumption by 30-50%.
The scheme provides a 5% interest subvention for Micro and Small Enterprises and 3% for Medium Enterprises.
The budgetary outlay is ₹1000 crore over a 3-year duration (FY 2025-26 to FY 2027-28).

sections.title: CAFE Norms and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)
sections.focus: Applicability and timelines of CAFE norms, and BEE's statutory framework and reports.
sections.rationale: Groups the Corporate Average Fuel Efficiency norms with the parent regulatory body (BEE) and its related indices.
sections.sectionExcerpt: CAFE-3 norms will be in force from April 2027 to March 2032, applicable to passenger cars up to 9 seats and 3,500 kg. BEE operates under the Energy Conservation Act 2001. BEE, along with the Alliance for an Energy Efficient Economy, released the State Energy Efficiency Index (SEEI) 2024.
evidenceBullets: CAFE-3 norms will come into force from 1st April 2027 until 31st March 2032.
CAFE norms apply to passenger cars with a seating capacity not exceeding 9 persons and a gross vehicle weight not exceeding 3,500 kg.
BEE was established under the legislative framework of the Energy Conservation Act 2001.
The State Energy Efficiency Index (SEEI) 2024 was developed by BEE in collaboration with the Alliance for an Energy Efficient Economy.

sections.title: Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRCs) and National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF)
sections.focus: Guidelines for BRCs and the structural/financial details of the NMNF.
sections.rationale: BRCs are a direct component of the NMNF; they share the same ministry and overarching goals.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Ministry of Agriculture released guidelines for 10,000 BRCs under NMNF, providing Rs 1 lakh financial assistance per centre. NMNF is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme (90:10 for Hilly/NE, 60:40 for others) running till 2025-26, aiming to reach 1 crore farmers and establish 15,000 clusters. 30,000 Krishi Sakhis are deployed to scale up practices.
evidenceBullets: BRCs provide Rs 1 lakh financial assistance for setting up cluster-level enterprises to supply biological inputs.
NMNF is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme with a 90:10 funding ratio for Hilly & North Eastern States and 60:40 for all other States.
NMNF aims to reach 1 crore farmers and initiate Natural Farming in a 7.5 lakh Ha area by 2025-26.
30,000 Krishi Sakhis (Community Resource Persons) are utilized for the easy scaling up of Natural Farming practices.

sections.title: Natural Farming Practices and Comparison with Organic Farming
sections.focus: Core components of natural farming (Beejamrit, Jivamrit, Whapasa) and its distinctions from organic farming.
sections.rationale: Focuses purely on the agronomic concepts, techniques, and comparative analysis of farming systems.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Natural Farming is a chemical-free, low-input system relying on on-farm inputs like Desi Cow products. Components include Beejamrit (seed treatment), Jivamrit (bio-stimulant), Mulching, and Whapasa (activating earthworms). Unlike Organic Farming, Natural Farming uses no external inputs, avoids compost/minerals for soil correction, and relies on surface decomposition rather than plowing.
evidenceBullets: Beejamrit involves the treatment of seeds using cow dung, urine, and lime-based formulations.
Whapasa refers to activating earthworms in the soil for water vapour condensation.
Organic Farming involves off-farm purchased inputs, whereas Natural Farming relies strictly on on-farm inputs based on the Desi Cow.
Natural Farming does not allow the use of compost, vermicompost, or mined minerals for soil correction, unlike Organic Farming.

sections.title: Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS)
sections.focus: FAO's GIAHS programme and newly added sites.
sections.rationale: A distinct international agricultural initiative mentioned at the end of the packet.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Six new sites were added to the FAO's GIAHS Programme, including a traditional agroforestry system for the erva-mate plant in Brazil, specialised farming systems in China (pearl mussels, white tea, pears), and an ancestral system preserving vital food crops in Mexico.
evidenceBullets: GIAHS is a programme operated by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).
Newly added GIAHS sites include an erva-mate agroforestry system in Brazil and pearl mussel farming in China.

sections.title: Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS)
sections.focus: Concept of GIAHS, its genesis, purpose, and the specific recognized sites in India.
sections.rationale: GIAHS is a distinct international agricultural framework with specific Indian sites that are highly examinable for UPSC prelims, especially for matching sites to their respective states.
sections.sectionExcerpt: GIAHS are resilient agroecosystems characterized by remarkable agrobiodiversity, traditional knowledge, invaluable cultures, and beautiful landscapes. Launched at the World Summit for Sustainable Development in 2002, the programme protects family farming and traditional systems. There are 95 sites globally, including three in India: Saffron Heritage of Kashmir, Koraput Traditional Agriculture in Odisha, and Kuttanad Below Sea Level Farming System in Kerala.
evidenceBullets: Launched at the World Summit for Sustainable Development in 2002.
Protects traditional agricultural systems from climate change, community displacements, and biodiversity loss.
India has three GIAHS sites: Saffron Heritage of Kashmir, Koraput Traditional Agriculture (Odisha), and Kuttanad Below Sea Level Farming System (Kerala).

sections.title: Agroforestry and Model Rules 2025
sections.focus: Model Rules 2025 for tree felling, agroforestry systems in India, and related government initiatives.
sections.rationale: Agroforestry is a key component of sustainable agriculture. The new Model Rules 2025, various agroforestry systems, and India's Bonn Challenge pledge form a solid cluster for policy and conceptual questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Government issued Model Rules 2025 for felling trees on agricultural land, overseen by State Level Committees. Applicants register in the National Timber Management System. Agroforestry occupies approximately 8% of India's land. Systems include Agrisilviculture (crops and trees), Agri-horticulture, and Silvi-pasture. Initiatives include the National Agroforestry Policy 2014, SubMission on Agroforestry, and India's Bonn Challenge pledge to restore 13 million hectares of degraded land by 2020.
evidenceBullets: Model Rules 2025 require Felling Permits for agricultural lands with more than 10 trees, and NOCs for 10 or fewer trees.
Applicants must register in the National Timber Management System with land ownership details.
Agroforestry occupies approximately 8% of India's geographical land area.
India joined the Bonn Challenge to restore 13 million hectares of degraded and deforested land by 2020.
Agroforestry systems include Agrisilviculture (combines crops with tree crops) and Silvi-pasture (integrates livestock, forage, and trees).

sections.title: Environment Audit (EA) Rules, 2025
sections.focus: The framework, tiers, and statutory backing of the newly notified Environment Audit Rules, 2025.
sections.rationale: New environmental rules are prime targets for UPSC. The distinction between EA and EIA, the two-tiered system, and the statutory origin under the EPA 1986 are highly testable facts.
sections.sectionExcerpt: MoEFCC notified the Environment Audit (EA) Rules, 2025 under the Environment Protection Act (1986). EA differs from EIA as it determines conformity to an approved Environmental Management Plan post-establishment. It features a Two-Tiered System: Tier-1 (government regulator review by CPCB/SPCB) and Tier-2 (Environment auditor-based). An Environment Audit Designate Agency (EADA) oversees certification and registration of auditors.
evidenceBullets: EA Rules 2025 were developed under the Environment Protection Act (1986).
EA differs from EIA as EIA is conducted prior to project establishment, while EA checks conformity to the Environmental Management Plan.
Introduced initially in 1992 under the Environment (Protection) Rules, requiring annual environmental statements to SPCBs.
Features a Two-Tiered System: Tier-1 (CPCB/SPCB review) and Tier-2 (Environment auditor-based mechanism).
Environment Audit Designate Agency (EADA) is responsible for certification, registration, and oversight of Environment Auditors.

sections.title: Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) and Ex-Post Facto Clearance
sections.focus: Supreme Court rulings on ex-post facto environmental clearance for mining projects.
sections.rationale: Judicial interventions in environmental governance, such as rulings on ex-post facto EC, are frequently tested in UPSC Polity and Environment sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Supreme Court reviewed and overturned its May 2025 decision in Vanashakti v. Union of India, which had restrained the Centre from granting ex-post facto environmental clearance (EC) to mining projects or regularising actions contravening the 2006 EIA notification. An ex-post facto EC allows a project proponent to continue without obtaining an EC prior to project commencement.
evidenceBullets: The Supreme Court overturned its decision that restrained the Centre from granting ex-post facto environmental clearance to mining projects.
Ex-post facto EC allows a project to continue without having obtained an Environmental Clearance prior to its commencement.

sections.title: Environmental Clearance (EC) in India
sections.focus: EIA Notification 2006, project categorization, and Supreme Court rulings on ex post facto clearance.
sections.rationale: A distinct, highly testable concept regarding environmental jurisprudence and administrative categorization of projects.
sections.sectionExcerpt: EC is required for projects significantly impacting the environment under the EIA Notification, 2006. Projects are categorized into Category A (Central level, MoEF&CC) and Category B (State level, SEIAA). Prior clearance is mandatory, and the Supreme Court (Common Cause v. UoI, 2017) ruled that ex post facto or retrospective clearance is alien to environmental jurisprudence.
evidenceBullets: EIA Notification 2006 lists over 39 activities requiring prior EC.
Category A projects are handled by MoEF&CC at the Central level.
Category B projects are handled by SEIAA at the State level.
Supreme Court held in Common Cause v. UoI (2017) that ex post facto environmental clearance is invalid.

sections.title: Great Nicobar Island Project (GNIP) and Tribal Rights
sections.focus: Infrastructure project components, nodal agencies, and the conflict between PAT 1956 and FRA 2006 regarding tribal rights.
sections.rationale: Combines geography (Galathea Bay, Ramsar site), tribal governance (PAT vs FRA), and major infrastructure initiatives, making it a prime target for multi-statement UPSC questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The GNIP is a massive Greenfield infrastructure project conceived by NITI Aayog, with ANIIDCO as the nodal agency. It includes a transshipment port at Galathea Bay, an airport, a power plant, and a township. A conflict exists over forest land diversion, with the administration citing the Protection of Aboriginal Tribes Act, 1956 (PAT) to bypass the Forest Rights Act, 2006 (FRA) which mandates Gram Sabha consent. Galathea Bay is a Ramsar Site and nesting site for the Leatherback Sea Turtle.
evidenceBullets: Tribal Councils in A&N Islands have limited advisory/executive powers, unlike Sixth Schedule autonomous councils.
PAT 1956 allows unilateral forest land diversion, conflicting with FRA 2006's mandate for Gram Sabha consent.
GNIP was conceived by NITI Aayog; ANIIDCO is the nodal agency.
Project includes an International Transshipment Port at Galathea Bay.
Galathea Bay is a Ramsar Site and a key nesting site for the endangered Leatherback Sea Turtle.

sections.title: Dam Safety in India and Sedimentation Vulnerabilities
sections.focus: Dam statistics, sedimentation issues, Dam Safety Act 2021, and related national/international initiatives.
sections.rationale: Contains rich factual data (India's global rank, largest reservoir) and institutional frameworks (Dam Safety Act, DRIP, ICOLD) highly relevant for Prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: India is the 3rd largest dam-owning nation, with Maharashtra having the highest number of large dams and Indira Sagar being the largest by volume. Many reservoirs have lost nearly 50% capacity due to sedimentation. The Dam Safety Act, 2021 established the NCDS, NDSA, SCDS, and SDSO. Initiatives include DRIP (funded by World Bank and AIIB), SHAISYS, DHARMA, and the National Centre for Earthquake Safety of Dams at MNIT Jaipur.
evidenceBullets: India is the 3rd largest dam-owning nation (after China and USA), with Maharashtra having the highest number of large dams.
Indira Sagar Dam (Narmada River, MP) is India's largest reservoir by volume.
Dam Safety Act, 2021 created a 4-tier institutional framework: NCDS, NDSA, SCDS, and SDSO.
DRIP Phase II and III are implemented with financial assistance from the World Bank and AIIB.
National Centre for Earthquake Safety of Dams is located at MNIT Jaipur.
World Commission on Dams was established by the World Bank and IUCN in 1998.

sections.title: Seed Management and Draft Seeds Bill, 2025
sections.focus: Key provisions of the Draft Seeds Bill, 2025, including mandatory registration and the SATHI portal.
sections.rationale: Legislative updates in agriculture are a staple in UPSC exams. The SATHI portal and exemptions for farmers' varieties are key testable facts.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Draft Seeds Bill, 2025 seeks to replace the Seeds Act, 1966, and Seeds (Control) Order, 1983 to regulate seed quality. It mandates that no unregistered seed can be sold, excepting farmers' varieties and export-only seeds. It requires mandatory onboarding on the SATHI portal (created by MoAFW and NIC) for complete seed traceability. Central and State Seed Committees will be constituted.
evidenceBullets: Draft Seeds Bill, 2025 replaces the Seeds Act, 1966, and Seeds (Control) Order, 1983.
Un-registered seeds cannot be sold, except for farmers' varieties and seeds produced exclusively for export.
SATHI portal (MoAFW + NIC) ensures complete traceability of seeds from origin to farmer.
Seed varieties must conform to the Indian Minimum Seed Certification Standards.

sections.subtopic: Draft Seeds Bill and Sand Mining
sections.title: Draft Seeds Bill 2025 and India's Seed Sector
sections.focus: Provisions of the Draft Seeds Bill 2025, comparison with the 1966 Act, classification of seeds, and government initiatives.
sections.rationale: The seed sector is a highly examinable topic for UPSC, especially the classification of seeds (Breeder, Foundation, Certified), farmer rights under the new bill, and traditional landraces.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Comparative Analysis of Seeds Act, 1966 and Draft Seeds Bill, 2025... Mandatory registration for every seed variety before trade... Farmers can save, exchange, and sell their seeds... Overview of India's Seed Sector: 5th largest Seed Industry... Recognizes three generations of seeds: Breeder Seed, Foundation Seed, Certified Seed... Key Initiatives: Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Act, 2001... Seed Village Programme... Landraces are genetically diverse crop populations...
evidenceBullets: Draft Seeds Bill 2025 mandates registration for every seed variety before trade and incorporates biotech norms.
Farmers can save, use, exchange, or sell their seeds without brand registration, but cannot sell under a brand name.
Breeder Seed is the progeny of the nucleus seed; Foundation Seed is the progeny of breeder seed; Certified Seed is the progeny of foundation seed.
Odisha launched a roadmap to integrate Landraces (genetically diverse crop populations evolved with traditional practices) into formal seed systems.
Seed Village Programme (Beej Gram Yojana) aims to improve farmers' saved seed quality.

sections.subtopic: Draft Seeds Bill and Sand Mining
sections.title: Sand Mining and Environmental Regulations
sections.focus: Supreme Court rulings, environmental impacts, and the legal framework governing sand mining in India.
sections.rationale: Sand mining regulations, especially the mandatory nature of replenishment studies and environmental clearances (Deepak Kumar case), are critical for environment and ecology questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Supreme Court (SC) observed that replenishment data is a mandatory prerequisite for environmental clearance in addition to District Survey Report (DSR)... Deepak Kumar vs State of Haryana (2012): SC made environmental clearance mandatory for all minor mineral extractions, including sand... EIA Notification (2016 Amendment): Cluster-based assessments and making replenishment studies a key part of DSR.
evidenceBullets: Supreme Court ruled that a replenishment study is a mandatory prerequisite for environmental clearance for sand mining.
Deepak Kumar vs State of Haryana (2012) made environmental clearance mandatory for all minor mineral extractions, including sand.
EIA Notification (2016 Amendment) introduced cluster-based assessments and made replenishment studies a key part of the District Survey Report (DSR).
Unrestricted sand mining causes riverbank erosion, lowers groundwater tables, and increases flood risks.

sections.subtopic: Draft Seeds Bill and Sand Mining
sections.title: Miscellaneous Initiatives: BRICS, UN-India, and RECLAIM
sections.focus: Recent international and national frameworks related to land restoration, capacity building, and mine closure.
sections.rationale: Short, factual news items that often appear as match-the-following or statement-based questions in Prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: BRICS Land Restoration Partnership: Launched at 15th BRICS Agriculture Meet in Brazil to address land degradation... UN-India Global Capacity-Building Initiative: Share India's development experiences with Global South nations... Implemented through new UN India SDG Country Fund and ITEC... RECLAIM Framework: Coal Controller Organisation under Coal Ministry and Heartfulness Institute launched it for inclusive community engagement throughout mine closure.
evidenceBullets: BRICS Land Restoration Partnership was launched at the 15th BRICS Agriculture Meet in Brazil to address land degradation and desertification.
UN-India Global Capacity-Building Initiative shares India's development experiences with the Global South, implemented via the UN India SDG Country Fund and ITEC.
RECLAIM Framework was launched by the Coal Controller Organisation and Heartfulness Institute to guide community engagement during mine closure and post-closure phases.

sections.title: Mine Closure Frameworks: RECLAIM, L.I.V.E.S., and ARTHA
sections.focus: Frameworks and guides for responsible mine closures and green financing.
sections.rationale: This section covers specific frameworks and guidelines introduced for sustainable mine closure and financing, forming a distinct examinable cluster.
sections.sectionExcerpt: RECLAIM Framework for Mine Closure: REACH OUT, ENVISION, CO DESIGN, LOCALISE, ACT, INTEGRATE, MAINTAIN. L.I.V.E.S. and ARTHA unveiled at Star Rating Award Ceremony for Coal and Lignite Mines. L.I.V.E.S is a comprehensive practitioner’s guide to serve as benchmark for responsible and sustainable mine closures. ARTHA is a Green Financing Framework for channeling investments towards transforming reclaimed mines into productive and environmentally friendly assets.
evidenceBullets: RECLAIM stands for Reach out, Envision, Co-design, Localise, Act, Integrate, Maintain.
L.I.V.E.S is a practitioner's guide serving as a benchmark for responsible and sustainable mine closures.
ARTHA is a Green Financing Framework designed to transform reclaimed mines into productive assets.

sections.title: Uniform Renewable Energy Tariff (URET)
sections.focus: The scrapping of URET by the Ministry of Power and its implications.
sections.rationale: A specific policy decision regarding renewable energy pricing that was revoked, making it a standalone conceptual point.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Uniform Renewable Energy Tariff (URET) scrapped by Ministry of Power. Aims to provide uniform tariff to consumers by averaging competitive bid tariffs of similar renewable energy projects within central pools. Reasons for revocation: Procurers were reluctant to sign Power Sale Agreements due to uncertainty of tariffs over the three-year fixed period causing stalled projects.
evidenceBullets: URET was scrapped by the Ministry of Power.
It aimed to average competitive bid tariffs of similar RE projects to provide a uniform tariff.
It was revoked because procurers were reluctant to sign Power Sale Agreements due to tariff uncertainty over a three-year fixed period.

sections.title: UNDP Equator Initiative Award
sections.focus: Recognition of Bibi Fatima SHG and the Equator Initiative's mandate.
sections.rationale: An important international biodiversity and community-led conservation award with a specific Indian winner.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Bibi Fatima Women’s self-help group (SHG) from a small village in Dharwad district was one of the winners of the ‘Equator Initiative Award’, also referred to as Nobel Prize for Biodiversity Conservation. Presented biennially under Equator Initiative of United Nations Development Programme (UNDP). The initiative provides opportunities for Indigenous Peoples and local communities to address land degradation, biodiversity conservation and livelihood improvement.
evidenceBullets: Bibi Fatima Women's SHG from Dharwad won the UNDP Equator Initiative Award.
The award is also referred to as the Nobel Prize for Biodiversity Conservation.
It is presented biennially by the UNDP to recognize Indigenous Peoples and local communities.

sections.title: Sustainable Energy for All (SEforALL) & UNOPS
sections.focus: NTPC's tie-up with SEforALL and the organizational details of UNOPS.
sections.rationale: Covers an international energy transition organization and its parent UN agency details, relevant for international organizations questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: NTPC ties up with SEforALL for energy transition roadmap. Launched by former UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon in 2011. It is an independent organization, hosted by UNOPS. Global mandate: Accelerate progress on energy transition in emerging and developing countries. About UN office for Project Services (UNOPS): Now a separate, self-financing UN Agency which was part of UNDP until 1994. Headquarter: Copenhagen, Denmark.
evidenceBullets: NTPC partnered with SEforALL to create an energy transition roadmap.
SEforALL is an independent organization hosted by UNOPS.
UNOPS is a self-financing UN Agency headquartered in Copenhagen, Denmark.

sections.title: Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO) and Renewable Consumption Obligation (RCO)
sections.focus: Transition from RPO to RCO under the Energy Conservation Act 2001.
sections.rationale: Core statutory obligations for renewable energy consumption involving multiple acts (ECA 2001, Electricity Act 2003) and regulatory bodies.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Power issued a revised draft notification on RCO under Energy Conservation Act (ECA) 2001 establishing mandatory year-wise Renewable Energy (RE) consumption targets from 2024-25 to 2029-30. RPO, established under Electricity Act, 2003, mandates electricity distribution companies, captive power producers and open-access consumers to annually purchase a certain minimum amount of RE. While RPO focuses on actual purchase, RCO focuses on actual consumption from RE sources. Creates binding obligations with penalties for non-compliance overseen by Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).
evidenceBullets: RCO draft notification was issued under the Energy Conservation Act (ECA) 2001.
RPO was established under the Electricity Act, 2003.
RPO focuses on the purchase of electricity from RE sources, whereas RCO focuses on actual consumption.
Penalties for non-compliance with RCO are overseen by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).

sections.title: Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF)
sections.focus: SAF blending mandates, feedstocks, and the Indian Oil-Air India MoU.
sections.rationale: Emerging alternative fuel technology with specific blending targets and feedstock details.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Indian Oil signed a MoU with Air India for supply of sustainable aviation fuel. An alternative fuel made from non-petroleum feedstocks that reduces emissions from air transportation. Can be blended up to 50% in conventional aviation turbine fuel (ATF or jet fuel). India mandated 1% SAF blending in jet fuel sold to international airlines from 2027. Raw material: Corn grain, Oil seeds, Algae, Agricultural residues, Forestry residues, Wastewater treatment sludge etc.
evidenceBullets: Indian Oil and Air India signed an MoU for the supply of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF).
SAF can be blended up to 50% in conventional aviation turbine fuel.
India has mandated a 1% SAF blending in jet fuel for international airlines starting in 2027.
SAF is produced from non-petroleum feedstocks like corn grain, algae, and agricultural residues.

sections.title: Green Municipal Bonds (GMB)
sections.focus: India's first certified GMB by Ghaziabad Nagar Nigam and municipal bond basics.
sections.rationale: Financial instrument for urban environmental projects with constitutional backing (Article 243W).
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ghaziabad Nagar Nigam pioneered India's first certified GMB issued under Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban, raising ₹150 crore for development of Tertiary Sewage Treatment Plant (TSTP). TSTP was developed under Public-Private Hybrid Annuity Model (PPP-HAM). Municipal bonds refer to non-convertible debt securities issued by municipal body or others established for such purposes and entrusted with functions under Article 243W of Constitution. Green bonds are used to raise funds specifically for climate mitigation, adaptation and other environment friendly projects.
evidenceBullets: Ghaziabad Nagar Nigam issued India's first certified Green Municipal Bond.
The funds raised were used for a Tertiary Sewage Treatment Plant under the PPP-HAM model.
Municipal bonds are non-convertible debt securities issued by bodies entrusted with functions under Article 243W of the Constitution.

sections.title: Gas Hydrates
sections.focus: Characteristics, locations, and significance of gas hydrates as an unconventional hydrocarbon source.
sections.rationale: A distinct scientific and geographical topic regarding unconventional energy sources, highly relevant for UPSC environment and geography questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Gas hydrates are crystalline solids composed of water and gas, containing large amounts of methane. They are classified as unconventional hydrocarbons because extracting them requires advanced technologies. They exist in huge quantities in marine sediments below the sea floor and within the permafrost layer in the Arctic. In India, huge quantities are found around the Andaman Islands and in Krishna-Godavari offshore. China is constructing the world's first permanent undersea research station to study them.
evidenceBullets: Gas hydrates are crystalline solids composed of water and gas that contain large amounts of methane.
They are classified as unconventional hydrocarbons requiring advanced and non-traditional extraction technologies.
Found in marine sediments directly below the sea floor and within the Arctic permafrost layer.
In India, significant quantities are located around the Andaman Islands and in the Krishna-Godavari offshore basin.
China is building the world's first permanent undersea research station to study gas hydrates.

sections.title: International Energy Agency (IEA)
sections.focus: Genesis, membership criteria, and flagship reports of the International Energy Agency.
sections.rationale: A major international organization with specific, testable membership criteria (like OECD prerequisite and 90-day reserves) and important flagship reports.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The International Energy Agency (IEA), located in Paris, France, was founded in 1974 to ensure the security of oil supplies. It has 32 Member countries and 13 Association countries, with India being an associate member. To be a full member, a country must be an OECD member, maintain crude oil reserves equivalent to 90 days of the previous year's net imports, and have a demand restraint programme to reduce oil consumption by up to 10%. Flagship publications include the World Energy Outlook, Global Methane Tracker, Delivering Sustainable Fuels, and Global EV Outlook.
evidenceBullets: IEA was founded in 1974 to ensure the security of oil supplies and is headquartered in Paris, France.
India is an associate member of the IEA, not a full member.
Full membership requires OECD membership, accessible crude oil reserves equivalent to 90 days of the previous year's net imports, and a 10% demand restraint programme.
Flagship publications include the World Energy Outlook.
Recent reports include the Global Methane Tracker 2025, Delivering Sustainable Fuels, and Global EV Outlook 2025.

sections.title: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics (ICRISAT)
sections.focus: Genesis, purpose, and key achievements of ICRISAT.
sections.rationale: A distinct agricultural research organization with specific achievements like the pigeon pea hybrid and Africa Food Prize, making it a prime candidate for agriculture-related questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: ICRISAT is a non-profit research centre established in the 1970s under the CGIAR consortium, located in Hyderabad, Telangana. Its purpose is to improve food security in semi-arid regions and support smallholder farmers in drylands globally. Key achievements include being awarded the Africa Food Prize in 2021 and developing the world's first pigeon pea hybrid. Recently, ICRISAT collaborated with RIS to launch the ICRISAT Centre of Excellence for South-South Cooperation.
evidenceBullets: Located in Hyderabad, Telangana, and established as a non-profit research centre under the CGIAR consortium in the 1970s.
Aims to improve food security in semi-arid regions and support smallholder farmers in drylands.
Awarded the Africa Food Prize in 2021 and developed the world's first pigeon pea hybrid.
Launched the ICRISAT Centre of Excellence for South-South Cooperation in collaboration with Research and Information System for Developing Countries (RIS).

sections.title: Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI)
sections.focus: Status, functions, and achievements of SECI in India's renewable energy sector.
sections.rationale: An important national body for renewable energy implementation with specific Navratna status and regulatory roles under CERC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Established in 2011 for the implementation of the National Solar Mission, SECI is a Navratna Central Public Sector Undertaking under the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE). It is the designated Renewable Energy Implementing Agency (REIA) for MNRE schemes and functions as a Category-I power trading licensee under CERC. SECI implements special programmes like the greening of islands (Lakshadweep, A&N Islands) and high-altitude grid-linked capacity in Ladakh.
evidenceBullets: SECI is a Navratna Central Public Sector Undertaking under the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE).
Established in 2011 specifically for the implementation of the National Solar Mission.
Functions as a Category-I (highest) power trading licensee under the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC).
Implements special regional models such as the greening of islands (Lakshadweep, A&N) and a proposed 5 GW high-altitude grid-linked capacity in Ladakh.

sections.title: Related Reports and Indices in News
sections.focus: Key environmental and agricultural reports and their publishing organizations.
sections.rationale: A cluster of miscellaneous but highly testable reports and indices often asked in UPSC prelims as match-the-following questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Several key reports were recently released: The Production Gap Report 2025 by the Stockholm Environment Institute (SEI), Climate Analytics, and IISD highlights the discrepancy between governments' planned fossil fuel production and levels consistent with limiting warming to 1.5°C or 2°C. The Global Electricity Mid-Year Insights 2025 was released by the energy think tank Ember, and The State of Food and Agriculture (SOFA) 2025 Report was released by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).
evidenceBullets: The Production Gap Report 2025 is released by the Stockholm Environment Institute (SEI), Climate Analytics, and IISD.
The 'Production Gap' refers to the discrepancy between planned fossil fuel production and global production levels consistent with limiting warming to 1.5°C or 2°C.
The Global Electricity Mid-Year Insights 2025 is released by the energy think tank Ember.
The State of Food and Agriculture (SOFA) 2025 Report is released by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).

sections.title: International Conference on Disaster Resilient Infrastructure (ICDRI) & CDRI
sections.focus: CDRI's structure, ICDRI 2025 outcomes, and India's coastal vulnerability mitigation initiatives.
sections.rationale: CDRI is a major India-led global initiative. Its structure, funds (IRAF), and related coastal initiatives (ICZMP, CVI) are highly relevant for UPSC Prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: African Union joined India's CDRI as the 54th member at the 7th ICDRI in France. CDRI was launched by India at the 2019 UN Climate Action Summit to promote infrastructure resilience. It features initiatives like IRIS and the Infrastructure Resilience Accelerator Fund. Indian coastal initiatives include CRZ, ICZMP, and INCOIS's Coastal Vulnerability Index.
evidenceBullets: African Union joined CDRI as the 54th member at the 7th ICDRI in France.
CDRI was launched by India at the 2019 United Nations Climate Action Summit.
India is the permanent co-chair of the CDRI Governing Council.
CDRI initiatives include Infrastructure for Resilient Island States (IRIS) and Infrastructure Resilience Accelerator Fund (IRAF).
Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services (INCOIS) developed the Coastal Vulnerability Index (CVI) and Multi-Hazard Vulnerability Maps.

sections.title: Disaster Risk Reduction (DRR) Financing and UNDRR
sections.focus: India's DRR financing under the 15th Finance Commission, Sendai Framework, and UNDRR's role.
