sections.rationale: The 15th Finance Commission's methodology for disaster funds (NDMF/SDMF) and international frameworks like Sendai are core disaster management topics.
sections.sectionExcerpt: UNDRR released the Global Assessment Report titled Resilience Pays. India's DRR financing under the 15th Finance Commission exceeds INR 2.32 trillion, using a new methodology based on a Disaster Risk Index. It recommended the creation of NDMF and SDMF, with the Centre contributing 75% for general states and 90% for NE/Himalayan states. The Sendai Framework (2015-30) succeeded the Hyogo Framework.
evidenceBullets: UNDRR released the Global Assessment Report (GAR) titled Resilience Pays.
15th Finance Commission recommended the creation of National and State Disaster Mitigation Funds (NDMF and SDMF).
Centre contributes 75% to SDMF for general states and 90% for North-Eastern/Himalayan states.
Sendai Framework for DRR (2015-30) is the successor to the Hyogo Framework for Action.
UNDRR, headquartered in Geneva, is the lead UN agency for coordinating DRR and achieving Sendai Framework goals.

sections.title: C-FLOOD Platform for Flood Forecasting
sections.focus: Features, technology, and developing agencies of the C-FLOOD web-based platform.
sections.rationale: Technological interventions in disaster management, specifically flood forecasting tools and their developing agencies, are frequent targets for objective questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: C-FLOOD is a web-based platform providing 2-day advance flood inundation forecasts up to the village level using advanced 2-D hydrodynamic modelling. It was jointly developed by C-DAC, Central Water Commission (CWC), Ministry of Jal Shakti, and National Remote Sensing Centre (NRSC).
evidenceBullets: C-FLOOD provides 2-day advance flood inundation forecasts up to the village level.
The platform utilizes advanced 2-D hydrodynamic modelling for flood inundation maps and water level predictions.
It was jointly developed by C-DAC, Central Water Commission (CWC), Ministry of Jal Shakti, and NRSC.

sections.title: Cloudbursts and Orographic Lifting
sections.focus: Definition, meteorological mechanisms (orographic lift), and disaster risk reduction strategies for cloudbursts.
sections.rationale: This section covers a distinct meteorological phenomenon, detailing specific IMD criteria and the geographical mechanisms that cause it.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Extreme precipitation in a short period, sometimes accompanied by hail/thunder, creating flood conditions. Occurs in plains as well, but more common in mountainous zones, due to orographic lift. India Meteorological Department (IMD) terms a rain event as cloud burst if 10 cm rainfall is received at a station in one hour. Challenges with Tackling: Climate Change (E.g., every 1°C rise lets air hold ~7% more moisture). Measures: Cloudburst Disaster Risk Reduction (DRR) Strategy – National Disaster Management Plan (2019) including Landslide Hazard Zonation (LHZ).
evidenceBullets: The IMD defines a cloudburst as an event where 10 cm of rainfall is received at a station in one hour.
Cloudbursts are more common in mountainous zones due to orographic lift, where moist air is forced to rise, cooling and condensing rapidly.
A violent upward flow of air prevents condensing raindrops from falling until the lack of upward air causes the whole stored water to fall at once.
Climate change exacerbates cloudbursts, as every 1°C rise in temperature allows the air to hold approximately 7% more moisture.

sections.title: Glacial Lakes and Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs)
sections.focus: Types of glacial lakes, causes of GLOFs, and India's monitoring and mitigation strategies.
sections.rationale: Groups the formation of glacial lakes, the mechanics of outburst floods, and specific Indian monitoring frameworks (CWC, NGRMP) into a single coherent topic.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Glacial lakes: Water formed by melting glaciers, usually at or near glacier's base. Two primary types: Supraglacial lakes (Formed by meltwater in depressions on glaciers) and Moraine-dammed lakes (Formed by meltwater at glacier toes, dammed by loose debris or ice-cores, prone to sudden failure). GLOFs: Flood resulting from sudden and rapid release of water from a glacial lake. Major Causes: Glacial Surging, Moraine Dam Failure, Seismic Activities. Central Water Commission (CWC) monitors 902 glacial lakes and water bodies in the Himalayas. National Glacial Lake Outburst Flood (GLOF) Risk Mitigation Project (NGRMP): In Arunachal Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim and Uttarakhand.
evidenceBullets: Supraglacial lakes form in depressions on glaciers, while moraine-dammed lakes form at glacier toes and are highly prone to sudden failure.
GLOFs can be triggered by glacial surging (rapid short-term advance of a glacier), moraine dam failure, seismic activities, and human activities.
The Central Water Commission (CWC) monitors 902 glacial lakes and water bodies in the Himalayas.
The National GLOF Risk Mitigation Project (NGRMP) covers the states of Arunachal Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh, Sikkim, and Uttarakhand.
Synthetic Aperture Radar imagery is utilized to automatically detect changes in the size of glacial lakes.

sections.title: Tsunamis and NDMA Guidelines
sections.focus: Causes of tsunamis, wave characteristics (wavelength, amplitude, shoaling), and NDMA mitigation guidelines.
sections.rationale: Focuses on the physical characteristics of tsunami waves across different ocean depths and the institutional mitigation measures recommended by the NDMA.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Series of extremely long waves caused by large and sudden displacement of ocean, usually result of an earthquake below or near ocean floor. Causes: Earthquakes, landslides, volcanic eruptions, meteorites, underwater explosions. Wavelength: Speed & wavelength primarily depends on ocean depth, not distance from wave's source. Wavelength in deep ocean is ~200 km and reduces to less than 20 km near coast due to shoaling. Wave Height (Amplitude): Not noticed by ships in deep oceans because their amplitude is negligible. When it approach shallow water, wave amplitude increases. NDMA Guidelines for Tsunami: Mandate a multi-tiered institutional structure... Developing Natural buffers like bio shields, mangrove regeneration etc.
evidenceBullets: Tsunamis can be caused by earthquakes, landslides, volcanic eruptions, meteorites, and underwater explosions.
The speed and wavelength of a tsunami primarily depend on ocean depth rather than the distance from the wave's source.
In deep oceans, tsunami wave amplitude is negligible and often goes unnoticed by ships.
As tsunami waves approach shallow coastal waters, their wavelength decreases due to shoaling, and their wave amplitude increases significantly.
NDMA guidelines recommend developing natural buffers such as bio-shields and mangrove regeneration to mitigate tsunami impacts.

sections.title: Oil Spills and Marine Pollution Frameworks
sections.focus: Hazards of chemical spills (Calcium Carbide), impacts of oil spills, and national/global regulatory frameworks.
sections.rationale: Consolidates marine pollution incidents, including oil and chemical spills, with their corresponding legal and regulatory frameworks (MARPOL, BUNKER, NOSDCP).
sections.sectionExcerpt: Calcium Carbide: A hazardous chemical that reacts violently with seawater to produce highly flammable acetylene gas. Use of calcium carbide for ripening fruits has been banned under Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011. Key Framework on Oil Spills: Merchant Shipping Act (1958) adopts MARPOL conventions. National Oil Spill Disaster Contingency Plan (1996): Indian Coast Guard coordinates oil spill response in India's maritime zones as a nodal agency. GLOBAL: MARPOL (1973): International convention preventing marine pollution from ships. BUNKER Convention (2001): Holds shipowners liable for bunker oil pollution. Impact: Poisons algae, disrupting food chains; Affect benthic organisms. E.g., clams and mussels.
evidenceBullets: Calcium carbide reacts violently with seawater to produce highly flammable acetylene gas and is banned for fruit ripening in India under FSSAI regulations.
The Indian Coast Guard acts as the nodal agency coordinating oil spill responses under the National Oil Spill Disaster Contingency Plan (1996).
The Merchant Shipping Act (1958) adopts MARPOL conventions for comprehensive maritime governance and pollution control in India.
The BUNKER Convention (2001) holds shipowners liable for bunker oil pollution and mandates compulsory insurance coverage.
Oil spills negatively impact benthic organisms like clams and mussels and poison algae, which disrupts marine food chains.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: Key Technologies to Clean Oil Spills
sections.focus: Methods and technologies used for oil spill remediation.
sections.rationale: Distinct topic on environmental disaster mitigation technologies, highly testable for prelims regarding specific applications like Oilzapper and Milkweed.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Bioremediation: E.g., Oilzapper & Oilivorous-S, developed by The Energy and Resources Institute (TERI); use bacteria to convert harmful hydrocarbons into harmless carbon dioxide and water. Sorbents: E.g., Milkweed plant's fiber, commonly found in Rajasthan, can be effectively used as a sorbent material. Oil Boom: Involves using large floating booms with skirts extending into the water to physically isolate the spill area. Skimmers: These machines scrape oil from the water surface.
evidenceBullets: Oilzapper and Oilivorous-S are bioremediation technologies developed by TERI that use bacteria to convert hydrocarbons into carbon dioxide and water.
Milkweed plant fiber can be effectively used as a sorbent material for oil spills.
Oil booms physically isolate spills, while skimmers scrape oil from the water surface.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: National Crisis Management Committee (NCMC)
sections.focus: Statutory status, composition, and mandate of the NCMC.
sections.rationale: Important institutional body for disaster management, recently granted statutory status, making it highly relevant for polity and governance questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Home Affairs Notified Disaster Management NCMC (Procedure) Rules, 2025. Background: Disaster Management Act (Amendment) Act, 2025, granted statutory status to NCMC. Composition: Headed by Cabinet Secretary, with members including Home, Defence Secretary, etc. Powers and Functions: Nodal body to deal with major disasters, evaluate preparedness, etc.
evidenceBullets: NCMC was granted statutory status under the Disaster Management Act (Amendment) Act, 2025.
It is headed by the Cabinet Secretary.
NCMC acts as the nodal body to deal with major disasters, evaluate preparedness, and lay down policies for Disaster Management.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: Winter Fog Experiment (WiFEX)
sections.focus: Objectives and leading institutions of WiFEX.
sections.rationale: Specific scientific initiative by MoES, suitable for a standalone factual question.
sections.sectionExcerpt: One of the world's few long-term open-field experiments focused solely on fog. Institutions: Led by Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology under the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES). Objective: To develop better now-casting (next 6 hours) and forecasting of winter fog.
evidenceBullets: WiFEX is led by the Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology under the Ministry of Earth Sciences.
Its objective is to develop better now-casting and forecasting of winter fog.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: Regional Integrated Multi-Hazard Early Warning System (RIMES)
sections.focus: Origin, membership, and functions of RIMES.
sections.rationale: International disaster management body, relevant for IR and environment/disaster management intersections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Declaration on Disaster Preparedness adopted at 4th Ministerial Conference of RIMES, held in Colombo, Sri Lanka. Intergovernmental body that provides early warning services, disaster risk reduction strategies, and capacity building to Member States. Evolved post 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami. Membership: 22 member states and 26 collaborating states. Operates from its regional early warning centre at Asian Institute of Technology in Thailand.
evidenceBullets: RIMES is an intergovernmental body that evolved post the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami.
It operates from its regional early warning centre at the Asian Institute of Technology in Thailand.
The Declaration on Disaster Preparedness was adopted at the 4th Ministerial Conference of RIMES in Colombo, Sri Lanka.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: LICOMK++ Ocean Simulator
sections.focus: Capabilities and origin of the LICOMK++ system.
sections.rationale: Specific technological development in oceanography and climate modeling.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Chinese researchers have developed a high-resolution ocean simulation system, named LICOMK++, offering enhanced capabilities for modelling ocean dynamics and climate patterns. Hailed as an ocean 'microscope' due to its ultra-high-resolution capabilities. Enables scientists to analyze complex processes like eddies and heat transport with clarity.
evidenceBullets: LICOMK++ is a high-resolution ocean simulation system developed by Chinese researchers.
It is hailed as an ocean 'microscope' to analyze complex processes like eddies and heat transport, improving predictions for extreme weather events.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: Weather Derivatives (WD)
sections.focus: Launch, mechanism, and utility of Weather Derivatives in India.
sections.rationale: Intersection of economy and agriculture/disaster management. Highly conceptual and testable.
sections.sectionExcerpt: India is to launch its first WD, with National Commodity and Derivatives Exchange Ltd (NCDEX) partnering with India Meteorological Department (IMD) to develop rainfall-based derivative products. It will help farmers and allied sectors hedge against risks like irregular rainfall, heatwaves, etc. Unlike traditional derivatives based on financial assets, WD utilise meteorological parameters, like rainfall and temperature, as underlying asset, tied to a predefined weather index.
evidenceBullets: India's first Weather Derivatives are being launched by NCDEX in partnership with IMD.
They utilize meteorological parameters like rainfall and temperature as underlying assets, unlike traditional financial derivatives.
They help farmers and allied sectors hedge against risks like irregular rainfall and heatwaves.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: Disaster Alert Systems (SACHET, Cell Broadcast, EW4All)
sections.focus: National and global disaster early warning and alert systems.
sections.rationale: Groups related alert systems (SACHET, Cell Broadcast, EW4All) which are highly relevant for disaster preparedness and institutional frameworks.
sections.sectionExcerpt: SACHET (India's SMS-Based Alert System): Operationalized by National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA). Developed by Centre of Development of Telematics C-DOT using the Common Alerting Protocol (CAP)... Cell Broadcast (Near Real-Time Emergency Alerts): Being developed by C-DOT, CB alerts are broadcast directly to all mobile phones in affected area... Early Warnings for All (EW4All): Aim: Ensure universal protection... by 2027. Launched: At COP27 of UNFCCC in 2022... Jointly led by WMO, UNDRR, ITU and IFRC.
evidenceBullets: SACHET is an SMS-based alert system operationalized by NDMA and developed by C-DOT using the Common Alerting Protocol.
Cell Broadcast alerts are faster than SMS and are broadcast directly to all mobile phones in an affected area.
Early Warnings for All (EW4All) was launched at COP27 in 2022 to ensure universal protection by 2027.
EW4All is jointly led by WMO, UNDRR, ITU, and IFRC.

sections.subtopic: Oil Spill Technologies and Alert Systems
sections.title: Terms/Concepts in News (Twister, Grey Rhino, Super Typhoon)
sections.focus: Definitions of specific disaster-related meteorological and conceptual terms.
sections.rationale: Glossary-style terms that frequently appear in UPSC prelims as match-the-following or definition-based questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: TWISTER: Violently spinning (250 Miles/Hr), funnel-shaped columns of air stretching from dark thunderclouds to ground... Develop when moist and warm air collides with dry and cold air... GREY RHINO EVENT: Defined as highly probable, high impact but yet neglected systemic disruptions. These are not random surprises (like Black Swan events)... SUPER TYPHOON: Tropical cyclone with maximum wind speed exceeding 185 kph or more than 100 knots.
evidenceBullets: Twisters form when moist, warm air collides with dry, cold air, creating atmospheric instability and strong updrafts.
A Grey Rhino event is a highly probable, high-impact, but neglected systemic disruption, unlike random Black Swan events.
A Super Typhoon is a tropical cyclone with maximum wind speeds exceeding 185 kph.

sections.title: Tropical Cyclones
sections.focus: Formation conditions and regional terminology of tropical cyclones.
sections.rationale: The conditions for cyclone formation and the matching of regional names (e.g., Willy-willy, Typhoon) are classic, high-yield UPSC Prelims geography questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Always form over oceans where sea surface temperature is greater than 26°C. Develop at latitudes usually greater than 5° from equator in the presence of Coriolis force. Terms for Tropical Cyclones: Hurricane – North Atlantic, Eastern North Pacific and South Pacific Ocean. Cyclone – Indian Ocean. Typhoon – Western North Pacific Ocean. Willy-willy: Eastern part of the Southern Indian Ocean.
evidenceBullets: Tropical cyclones require sea surface temperatures greater than 26°C to form.
They develop at latitudes usually greater than 5° from the equator due to the necessity of the Coriolis force.
Regional terms include Hurricane (North Atlantic/Pacific), Typhoon (Western North Pacific), and Willy-willy (Eastern part of the Southern Indian Ocean).

sections.title: Miscellaneous Disaster and Weather Phenomena
sections.focus: Definitions and characteristics of Earthquake Swarms, Swell Waves, Blowouts, and Mesoscale Convective Systems.
sections.rationale: These distinct, short topics are highly testable as definitions or matching pairs in Prelims, particularly terms like 'Kallakkadal' and 'Earthquake Swarm'.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Earthquake Swarm: Sequences of many earthquakes that occur in a relatively short period without a specific main shock. Swell Waves: Long-wavelength ocean waves travelling long distances away from their origin. Largely unaffected by local winds and often go undetected by satellites... Also called Kallakkadal waves in India. Blowout: Oil/gas well accident where underground pressure forces gas/oil to surface uncontrollably. Mesoscale Convective System (MCS): Collection of thunderstorms that act as a system. Can spread across entire state and last >12 hours.
evidenceBullets: Earthquake swarms are sequences of many earthquakes occurring in a short period without a specific main shock.
Swell waves, also known as Kallakkadal waves in India, are long-wavelength ocean waves largely unaffected by local winds.
A blowout is an uncontrolled release of gas or oil from a well due to underground pressure or valve failure.
A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) is a collection of thunderstorms acting as a system that can last over 12 hours.

sections.title: Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services (INCOIS)
sections.focus: Genesis, mandate, and key services provided by INCOIS.
sections.rationale: INCOIS is a critical scientific organization under the Ministry of Earth Sciences. Its specific services, such as the Tsunami Early Warning System and Potential Fishing Zone Advisory, are standard UPSC targets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Established as an autonomous body in 1999 under Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES). Unit of the Earth System Science Organization (ESSO). Mandate: Provide ocean information and advisory services to society, industry, government agencies and scientific community... Other INCOIS Services: Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) Advisory, Tsunami Early Warning System, Ocean State Forecast (OSF), Coral Bleaching Alerts System etc.
evidenceBullets: INCOIS is an autonomous body established in 1999 under the Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES).
It operates as a unit of the Earth System Science Organization (ESSO).
Key services include the Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) Advisory, Tsunami Early Warning System, Ocean State Forecast, and Coral Bleaching Alerts System.

sections.title: Disaster-Related Reports and Indices
sections.focus: Key indices including the Global Drought Outlook, Disaster Risk Index (DRI), and District Flood Severity Index.
sections.rationale: Reports and indices are high-yield for UPSC Prelims. The DRI's connection to the 15th Finance Commission and the specific components of the DFSI are strong factual anchors for questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Global Drought Outlook, 2025 Released by: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). Disaster Risk Index (DRI)... Genesis: 15th Finance Commission (2021–26) formally adopted it to guide disaster funding. It integrated state’s DRI score into a formula for distributing NDRF and state contingency funds. Components: DRI uses probability of hazards (score of 70) and vulnerability (score of 30) to arrive at a composite score. District Flood Severity Index Developed by: Researchers from IIT Delhi and Gandhinagar... Consists of six factors classified in two groups: Variables representing occurrence of floods... Variables representing damage due to floods.
evidenceBullets: The Global Drought Outlook, 2025 is released by the OECD.
The Disaster Risk Index (DRI) was formally adopted by the 15th Finance Commission to guide disaster funding and distribute NDRF funds.
The DRI composite score is based on the probability of hazards (70) and vulnerability (30).
The District Flood Severity Index was developed by IIT Delhi and Gandhinagar researchers to quantify flood severity using occurrence and damage variables.

sections.title: Global Geoparks Network (GGN) & UNESCO Global Geoparks
sections.focus: UNESCO Global Geoparks framework, GGN membership criteria, and India's current status.
sections.rationale: A distinct topic on UNESCO's geological conservation framework. It is highly testable for statement-based questions regarding UGGP criteria, reassessment periods, and India's lack of recognized sites.
sections.sectionExcerpt: GGN is a non-profit International Association founded under UNESCO, which establishes ethical standards to be adopted by Global Geoparks. Introduced in 2015 as a key component of the International Geosciences and Geoparks Programme (IGGP). Geoparks: Single, unified geographical areas where landscapes of international geological significance are managed with a holistic concept... UGGP status is not permanent; it is reassessed every four years. Mandatory Networking: Membership of the GGN is obligatory for UGGPs. Current Status: 229 UNESCO Global Geoparks in 50 countries (none in India).
evidenceBullets: GGN is a non-profit International Association under UNESCO that establishes ethical standards for Global Geoparks.
UNESCO Global Geoparks (UGGP) were introduced in 2015 under the International Geosciences and Geoparks Programme (IGGP).
UGGP status is not permanent and must be reassessed every four years.
Membership in the Global Geoparks Network (GGN) is obligatory for all UGGPs.
There are currently 229 UNESCO Global Geoparks globally, but none are located in India.

sections.title: Polymetallic Sulphides & International Seabed Authority (ISA)
sections.focus: India's deep-sea exploration contracts, characteristics of polymetallic sulphides/nodules, and the mandate/initiatives of the ISA.
sections.rationale: A high-yield UPSC topic combining geography (ocean ridges), economy (marine resources), and international relations (ISA, UNCLOS). Excellent for mapping questions (Carlsberg Ridge, Afanasy-Nikitin) and institutional questions (ISA).
sections.sectionExcerpt: India signed a 15-year exploration contract for polymetallic sulphides (PMS) with International Seabed Authority (ISA) in Carlsberg Ridge. This is the first licence granted globally for exploring polymetallic sulphur nodules in the Carlsberg Ridge... Other Indian Contracts: Polymetallic Nodules in the Central Indian Ocean Basin and PMS in the Indian Ocean Ridge. Yet to be approved: Afanasy-Nikitin Sea (ANS) mount... claimed by Sri Lanka. Polymetallic sulphides are rich sources of metals such as copper, zinc, gold, and silver... ISA Location: Kingston, Jamaica. Established in 1994 under UNCLOS. Initiatives: Deep-Sea Biobank Initiative (DBI) 2025, AREA2030, Sustainable Seabed Knowledge Initiative (SSKI).
evidenceBullets: India signed a 15-year contract with the ISA for exploring polymetallic sulphides in the Carlsberg Ridge, located in the Arabian Sea.
India holds other exploration contracts for Polymetallic Nodules in the Central Indian Ocean Basin and PMS in the Indian Ocean Ridge.
India's exploration approval for the Afanasy-Nikitin Sea (ANS) mount is pending, as the territory is claimed by Sri Lanka.
Polymetallic sulphides are rich in copper, zinc, gold, and silver, and form where hot, mineral-rich fluids from the Earth's mantle enter the ocean.
The International Seabed Authority (ISA) is an autonomous organization established in 1994 under UNCLOS, headquartered in Kingston, Jamaica.
ISA initiatives include the Deep-Sea Biobank Initiative (DBI) 2025, AREA2030, and the Sustainable Seabed Knowledge Initiative (SSKI).

sections.title: Bharat Forecast System (BFS) & Mission Mausam
sections.focus: India's indigenous high-resolution weather forecast system, its technical features, and related meteorological initiatives.
sections.rationale: Important science and technology / geography topic focusing on indigenous forecasting capabilities, High-Performance Computing (HPC) systems, and Doppler radar applications.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Earth Sciences unveiled BFS, world's first indigenously developed high-resolution weather forecast systems. Developed by: Indian Institute of Tropical Meteorology (IITM), Pune... Enhances India's weather prediction resolution from 12 km to 6 km... Integrates real-time inputs from 40 Doppler Weather Radar. Positions India ahead of countries like US, UK, and EU by achieving 6 km resolution. Powered by High-Performance Computing (HPC) systems - Arka (IITM Pune) and Arunika (National Centre for Medium Range Weather Forecasting, Delhi). Mission Mausam: Launched in 2024 by Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES).
evidenceBullets: Bharat Forecast System (BFS) is the world's first indigenously developed high-resolution weather forecast system, created by IITM, Pune.
BFS improves India's weather prediction resolution from 12 km to 6 km, which is ahead of global models that operate at 9-14 km.
The system integrates real-time data from 40 Doppler Weather Radars, which use the Doppler effect to gather velocity data from particles.
BFS is powered by High-Performance Computing (HPC) systems named Arka (IITM Pune) and Arunika (NCMRWF, Delhi).
Mission Mausam was launched in 2024 by the Ministry of Earth Sciences to improve weather and climate services.

sections.title: Long-Period Average (LPA)
sections.focus: Definition, calculation period, and IMD's rainfall categorization based on LPA.
sections.rationale: A distinct meteorological concept used by IMD, highly testable for prelims regarding the specific percentages for normal, deficient, and excess rainfall.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast ‘above normal’ rainfall — 105% of the long-period average (LPA) of 87 cm (1971-2020)... Concept: It refers to the average rainfall recorded over a specific region during a defined time interval. Period: It is calculated over a long period, usually 30-50 years... Deficient: < 90% | Meaning: Drought-like conditions... Normal: 96% – 104% | Meaning: Ideal, expected rainfall.
evidenceBullets: LPA is calculated over a long period, usually 30-50 years, with the IMD currently using rainfall data from 1971 to 2020.
Normal rainfall is categorized as 96% to 104% of the LPA.
Deficient rainfall, indicating drought-like conditions, is defined as less than 90% of the LPA.
Above Normal rainfall is 105% to 110% of the LPA.

sections.title: True Polar Wander (TPW)
sections.focus: Concept of planetary reorientation, its drivers, and effects on Earth's rotation and day length.
sections.rationale: A specific geophysical phenomenon with clear causes (mass redistribution like dam construction, glacial melt) and measurable effects (longer days), making it a prime candidate for conceptual questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A recent study revealed that dam construction has shifted Earth’s rotational axis by over 1 meter since 1835 i.e., they have driven True Polar Wander (TPW). About True Polar Wander (TPW) or planetary reorientation: Rotation of the solid Earth (crust and mantle) about the liquid outer core... Effects of Shifting Poles: Longer Days as melting ice moves mass from the poles to the equator, making Earth slightly more flattened. This slows down its spin, causing longer days.
evidenceBullets: True Polar Wander (TPW) involves the rotation of the solid Earth (crust and mantle) about the liquid outer core to maintain rotational balance.
Natural drivers of TPW include glacial melting, tectonic plate shifts, and ocean swell, while human activities like dam construction also contribute.
Shifting poles move mass from the poles to the equator, making the Earth slightly more flattened and slowing its spin, which results in longer days.

sections.title: Pacific Ring of Fire and Reverse Faulting
sections.focus: Geological characteristics of the Pacific Ring of Fire, plate boundaries, and the mechanics of reverse faulting.
sections.rationale: Core geography topic covering plate tectonics, volcanism, and faulting mechanisms, rich in map-based and conceptual facts.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A magnitude 8.8 earthquake struck Russia’s Kamchatka Peninsula... potentially due to shallow reverse faulting... Reverse faulting (or thrust faulting) occurs when a block of Earth's crust is pushed up over another due to compressional forces... Pacific Ring of Fire: Also called Circum-Pacific Belt, it is a horseshoe-shaped belt around the rim of the Pacific Ocean... Accounts for ~75% of the world’s active volcanoes and ~90% of the world’s earthquakes.
evidenceBullets: Reverse faulting (thrust faulting) is caused by compressional forces pushing one block of Earth's crust over another.
The Pacific Ring of Fire accounts for approximately 75% of the world's active volcanoes and 90% of its earthquakes.
Convergent boundaries in the Ring of Fire create subduction zones, leading to volcanism and deep ocean trenches like the Mariana Trench.
Divergent boundaries, such as the East Pacific Rise, create seafloor spreading and rift valleys featuring hydrothermal vents.

sections.title: Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC)
sections.focus: Mechanism, significance, and potential impacts of the weakening of the AMOC.
sections.rationale: Highly important climate change topic. The mechanism of the global conveyor belt and the consequences of its slowdown are frequent targets for UPSC questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: AMOC: A system of ocean currents that circulates water within the Atlantic Ocean, bringing warm water north and cold water south. Driving Force: Differences in water temperature and salinity... Significance of AMOC: Influences rainfall patterns, including Indian monsoon. Heat Transport from the tropics to higher latitudes, moderating Europe’s climate... Potential Impacts of AMOC Slowdown: AMOC carbon feedback: Reduction of ocean carbon uptake... Colder temperatures in Europe...
evidenceBullets: AMOC is driven by differences in water temperature and salinity, circulating warm water north and cold water south.
Warm tropical water moves towards the poles, cools, becomes dense, and sinks in the North Atlantic before flowing south.
AMOC influences global climate patterns, including the Indian monsoon, and moderates Europe's climate by transporting heat from the tropics.
A slowdown in AMOC could lead to colder temperatures in Europe, reduced ocean carbon uptake, and sea-level rise across the U.S. East Coast.

sections.title: Delamination and Indian Plate Movement
sections.focus: The new theory of delamination explaining the splitting of the Indian Continental Plate.
sections.rationale: Introduces a new geological concept (Delamination) contrasting with traditional theories (Underplating, Subduction) regarding the Himalayas.
sections.sectionExcerpt: New research suggests that Indian Continental Plate is splitting apart and proposes a new theory explaining movement of the Indian Plate called ‘Delamination’. About Delamination: It is where the Indian plate's dense lower section peels away and sinks into Earth's mantle as it slides beneath the Eurasian plate... Two traditional theories explain this: Underplating: The denser Indian lower crust slides beneath the Eurasian crust. Subduction: One plate slides beneath another.
evidenceBullets: Delamination is a process where the dense lower section of a tectonic plate peels away and sinks into the Earth's mantle.
The Indian Continental Plate is bordered by four major plates: the Eurasian, Arabian, African, and Australian Plates.
Traditional theories for the Indian-Eurasian plate convergence are underplating (lower crust sliding beneath) and subduction (one plate sliding beneath another).

sections.title: Miscellaneous Geography Terms: Solar Tornado, Cold Pools, and Deep Seafloor
sections.focus: Definitions and characteristics of specific geographical and astronomical phenomena in the news.
sections.rationale: Groups short, distinct glossary-style terms that are often tested as 'Terms in News' in prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Term/Concept: Solar Tornado... made of ionized gas (plasma), held in place by magnetic fields... Term/Concept: Cold Pools... Small-scale areas of evaporatively cooled downdraft air that spread on the surface underneath precipitating clouds... Term/Concept: Earth’s Deep Seafloor... seabed below the depth at which light starts to decrease rapidly, typically around 200 meters...
evidenceBullets: Solar tornadoes (tornado prominences) are made of ionized gas (plasma) held by magnetic fields and can reach temperatures around 250,000 °C.
Cold pools form when evaporating rain cools the air, making it heavier, causing it to sink and spread across the ground like a shallow bubble.
The Deep Ocean is typically defined as the sea and seabed below 200 meters, where light rapidly decreases and the Twilight Zone begins.

sections.title: Deep Ocean Characteristics and Exploration
sections.focus: Physical conditions, biodiversity in the Mesopelagic Zone, and the significance of deep ocean exploration.
sections.rationale: Covers fundamental oceanography concepts and resource potential, highly relevant for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Deep ocean conditions include an average temperature of 4 degrees Celsius and extreme pressure. The Mesopelagic Zone (200m-1000m) contains about 90 percent of the world's fish by weight. Exploration is significant for energy sources, new drugs, polymetallic nodules, and climate change mitigation.
evidenceBullets: Average temperature is 4 degrees Celsius with pressure 40 to 110 times Earth's atmosphere.
Mesopelagic Zone (200m-1000m) holds about 90 percent of world's fish by weight.
Exploration yields energy sources like methane hydrate, polymetallic nodules, and new drugs.

sections.title: Polar Anticyclone
sections.focus: Formation mechanism and air circulation patterns of polar anticyclones.
sections.rationale: Core climatology concept detailing high-pressure system formation and wind direction.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Polar anticyclones are high-pressure wind systems created by intense cooling of surface air layers. Surface cooling causes dense air to sink, which is replaced by high-altitude air, building high pressure. Winds flow outward from the center, circulating clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere and anti-clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere.
evidenceBullets: Created by intense cooling of surface air causing dense air to sink.
High-pressure area surrounded by low-pressure zones with outward flowing winds.
Circulation is clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere and anti-clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere.

sections.title: Thirstwave
sections.focus: Definition, criteria, and factors driving extreme atmospheric thirst.
sections.rationale: Emerging climate terminology important for environment and geography sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A thirstwave involves periods of extreme atmospheric thirst that rapidly dry out the landscape, defined by high dryness and aggressive moisture demand. It occurs when daily evaporative demand exceeds its historical 90th percentile for at least 3 consecutive days. Factors driving this include temperature, wind speed, humidity, and sunshine.
evidenceBullets: Defined by high dryness and aggressive atmospheric demand for moisture.
Requires at least 3 consecutive days of evaporative demand greater than the historical 90th percentile.
Driven by temperature, wind speed, humidity, and sunshine.

sections.title: Mercator's Map Projection
sections.focus: Characteristics, distortions, and recent campaigns against the Mercator projection.
sections.rationale: Important cartography concept with contemporary relevance due to the African Union's campaign.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Designed in 1569 by Gerardus Mercator, this conformal map preserves local shapes and angles but distorts scale, making polar landmasses appear larger and equatorial regions smaller. For example, Africa appears roughly the size of Greenland despite being 14 times larger. The African Union endorsed the Correct the Map campaign to replace it with alternatives like the Equal Earth map.
evidenceBullets: Conformal map designed in 1569 that preserves local shapes and angles.
Distorts scale by enlarging landmasses near the poles and shrinking equatorial regions.
African Union endorsed the Correct the Map campaign to replace it with the Equal Earth map.

sections.title: Arabian Sea Mini Warm Pool
sections.focus: Definition, location, duration, and impact on the Indian Summer Monsoon.
sections.rationale: Crucial Indian monsoon mechanism and regional climate phenomenon.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Arabian Sea Mini Warm Pool (MWP) is a patch of unusually warm sea surface temperatures (exceeding 28.5 degrees Celsius) in the southeastern Arabian Sea near the Kerala coast. It forms annually during April and May before the Indian Summer Monsoon and acts as a self-correcting mechanism to help the monsoon recover from previous El Nino disruptions.
evidenceBullets: Located in the southeastern Arabian Sea near the Kerala coast with SST exceeding 28.5 degrees Celsius.
Forms annually during April and May just before the Indian Summer Monsoon.
Acts as a self-correcting mechanism helping the monsoon recover from previous El Nino disruptions.

sections.title: National Centre of Polar and Ocean Research (NCPOR)
sections.focus: Genesis, governing body, key roles, and newly launched facilities of NCPOR.
sections.rationale: Key Indian scientific institution involved in polar research and the Deep Ocean Mission.
sections.sectionExcerpt: NCPOR is an autonomous institution under the Ministry of Earth Sciences, established in 1998. It maintains India's research stations in Antarctica (Maitri, Bharati), the Arctic (Himadri), and the Himalayas (Himansh). It leads the Deep Ocean Mission and aids in implementing India's Arctic Policy and Antarctica Act. Recent facilities include Polar Bhavan and Sagar Bhavan.
evidenceBullets: Autonomous institution of the Ministry of Earth Sciences established in 1998.
Maintains research stations: Maitri and Bharati (Antarctica), Himadri (Arctic), and Himansh (Himalayas).
Leads the Deep Ocean Mission and implements India's Arctic Policy and Antarctica Act.
Recently launched Polar Bhavan (laboratories/museum) and Sagar Bhavan (ice core laboratories).

sections.subtopic: Geographical Features and Projects in India
sections.title: Geographical Features in News
sections.focus: Key natural geographical locations in India recently in the news, including mountain passes, glaciers, islands, peaks, forests, and seas.
sections.rationale: This section covers distinct natural geographical features across India, making it a coherent cluster for geography-based map and fact questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Covers Shipki La (Himachal Pradesh, Sutlej river), Gangotri Glacier (Uttarakhand, Bhagirathi river), Majuli Island (Brahmaputra, world's largest river island), Mt. Khangchendzonga (Sikkim, 3rd highest peak), Kancha Gachibowli Forest (Telangana), Kariyachalli Island (Gulf of Mannar), Barren Island Volcano (Andaman Sea, India's only active volcano), and the Andaman Sea (Seismic Zone V, Coral Triangle).
evidenceBullets: Shipki La is located in Kinnaur, Himachal Pradesh, on the India-Tibet border, where the Sutlej river enters India.
Majuli Island is the world's largest river island, formed by the Brahmaputra and Kherkutia Xuti/Subansiri rivers.
Mt. Khangchendzonga is the world's 3rd highest peak, located on the India-Nepal border in Sikkim.
Barren Island in the Andaman Sea is India's only active volcano.
Andaman Sea is part of Seismic Zone V and the Coral Triangle, bounded by Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, and Indonesia.

sections.subtopic: Geographical Features and Projects in India
sections.title: Rivers in News
sections.focus: Indian rivers recently in the news due to water tribunals, hydroelectric projects, and natural disasters.
sections.rationale: Groups all river-related news, focusing on origins, tributaries, and associated projects or tribunals, which is a classic UPSC geography topic.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Details major rivers in the news: Beas (Ravi and Beas Waters Tribunal, joins Sutlej), Chenab (Sawalkot HEP, formed by Chandra and Bhaga), Subansiri (Lower Hydroelectric Project, largest Brahmaputra tributary), Kopili (NW-57, Meghalaya/Assam), Siyom (Arunachal Pradesh HEPs), Mandovi/Mahadayi (water tribunal, Dudhsagar falls), and Subarnarekha (flash floods, NW-96, Hundru Falls).
evidenceBullets: Beas River originates at Beas Kund (Rohtang Pass) and joins the Sutlej at Harike, Punjab.
Chenab River is formed by Chandra and Bhaga rivers and is the largest tributary of the Indus.
Subansiri River, known as the Gold River, is the largest tributary of the Brahmaputra.
Mandovi/Mahadayi River originates in Bhimgad (Western Ghats), flows through Goa, Karnataka, and Maharashtra, and features the Dudhsagar waterfall.
Subarnarekha River flows through Jharkhand, Odisha, and West Bengal, and hosts the Hundru Falls.

sections.subtopic: Geographical Features and Projects in India
sections.title: Infrastructure and Projects in News
sections.focus: Major dams, hydroelectric projects, irrigation systems, and bridges in India.
sections.rationale: This section shifts focus from purely natural features to human-made geographical and infrastructure projects, forming a distinct study block.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Highlights key infrastructure projects: Ranjit Sagar Dam (Thein Dam) on the Ravi river (Punjab-J&K border), Etalin hydroelectric project on Dri and Tangon rivers (Dibang Valley, Arunachal Pradesh), Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Project (world's largest multi-stage lift irrigation on the Godavari river in Telangana), and the newly inaugurated Pamban Bridge connecting Rameswaram Island to mainland India.
evidenceBullets: Ranjit Sagar Dam (Thein Dam) is located on the Ravi River near the Punjab-Jammu & Kashmir border.
Etalin hydroelectric project is situated in the Dibang Valley, Arunachal Pradesh, on the Dri and Tangon rivers.
Kaleshwaram Lift Irrigation Project (KLIP) in Telangana is the world's largest multi-stage lift irrigation project, built on the Godavari River.
The new Pamban Bridge in Tamil Nadu replaces the original 2022-decommissioned cantilever bridge connecting Rameswaram Island to the mainland.

sections.title: Geographical Features in News
sections.focus: Key international geographical features including straits, biomes, peninsulas, glaciers, and shoals recently in the news.
sections.rationale: Covers diverse geographical locations like Davis Strait, Kamchatka Peninsula, and Drake Passage, which are highly testable for UPSC map-based questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Davis Strait Proto-Microcontinent: Located beneath the Davis Strait (connects Labrador Sea and Baffin Bay)... Arctic Biome: North of the Arctic Circle... Kamchatka Peninsula: Russian Far East on the Pacific Ring of Fire... Perito Moreno glacier: Los Glaciares National Park in Argentina... Drake Passage: Between Cape Horn and South Shetland Islands... Scarborough Shoal: A small atoll in the South China Sea.
evidenceBullets: Davis Strait connects the Labrador Sea and Baffin Bay.
Kamchatka Peninsula is located in the Russian Far East on the Pacific Ring of Fire, surrounded by the Bering Sea, Pacific Ocean, and Sea of Okhotsk.
Perito Moreno glacier, known as the 'White Giant', is located in Argentina's Los Glaciares National Park.
Drake Passage lies between Cape Horn and the South Shetland Islands, forming part of the Antarctic Circumpolar Current.
Scarborough Shoal (Huangyan Island/Panatag Shoal) is a small atoll in the South China Sea.

sections.title: Rivers and Water Bodies in News
sections.focus: Major international rivers, lakes, and seas in the news due to environmental changes or infrastructure projects.
sections.rationale: Rivers like the Congo and Blue Nile, and drying water bodies like the Aral Sea, are classic UPSC geography and environment topics.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Tulare Lake: San Joaquin Valley, California... Aral Sea: Boundary between Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan... Congo River: River Basin covers 9 countries... deepest river in the world and crosses equator twice... Blue Nile: Origin: lake Tana of Ethiopia... Great Ruaha River: Important tributary of Rufiji River... Seine River: Second longest river in France after Loire.
evidenceBullets: The Aral Sea is located on the boundary between Kazakhstan (North) and Uzbekistan (South).
The Congo River crosses the equator twice, is the deepest river in the world, and its basin holds the world's largest tropical peatland.
The Blue Nile originates from Lake Tana in Ethiopia and meets the White Nile at Khartoum to form the Nile.
The Great Ruaha River is a major tributary of the Rufiji River in Tanzania.
The Seine River flows through Paris and empties into the English Channel at Le Havre.

sections.title: Disaster Affected Areas in News
sections.focus: Global locations recently affected by natural disasters such as volcanic eruptions, earthquakes, cyclones, and floods.
sections.rationale: Matching volcanoes and disaster zones to their respective countries or regions is a frequent pattern in UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Mount Etna (Italy): Volcanic Eruption. Located in Sicily Island... Cyclone Asna: Tropical cyclone along the west coast, Arabian Sea... Mount Lewotobi Laki-laki (Indonesia): Volcanic Eruption. Located in Flores Island... Kilauea volcano (Hawaii, U.S.)... Hayli Gubbi (Ethiopia)... Mount Semeru (Indonesia): Highest volcano on Java Island.
evidenceBullets: Mount Etna is Europe's largest active volcano, located on Sicily Island, Italy.
Mount Lewotobi Laki-laki is a volcano located on Flores Island, Indonesia.
Kilauea volcano is situated on the southeastern part of the island of Hawaii.
Mount Semeru is a stratovolcano and the highest volcano on Java Island, Indonesia.
Hayli Gubbi is a volcano located in Ethiopia.

sections.title: Countries in News
sections.focus: Countries highlighted for significant environmental treaties, summits, or geopolitical developments.
sections.rationale: International summits, treaties (like the Falepili Union), and environmental initiatives linked to specific countries are highly relevant for current affairs.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Vanuatu: Approached the ICJ to classify environmental destruction as ecocide... Colombia: Second WHO Global Conference on Air Pollution and Health held in Cartagena... Denmark: India and Denmark reaffirm Green Strategic Partnership... Ethiopia: UN Food Systems Summit +4 Stocktake held in Addis Ababa... Tuvalu: Signed the 2023 Falepili Union Treaty with Australia... Ecuador: Indigenous groups criticize oil drilling expansion in the Amazon.
evidenceBullets: Vanuatu approached the International Court of Justice to classify environmental destruction as ecocide.
Colombia co-organized the Second WHO Global Conference on Air Pollution and Health in Cartagena.
Tuvalu signed the 2023 Falepili Union Treaty with Australia due to threats from rising sea levels.
Ethiopia hosted the UN Food Systems Summit +4 Stocktake in Addis Ababa.

sections.title: Protected Areas in News - Overview
sections.focus: Geographical mapping and awareness of recently highlighted protected areas in India.
sections.rationale: Tests map-based knowledge and basic awareness of various protected areas that were recently in the news.
sections.sectionExcerpt: List of Protected Areas highlighted on the map of India: Bhagirathi Eco Sensitive Zone (ESZ), Tsarap Chu Conservation Reserve, Dudhwa Tiger Reserve (DTR), Salkhan Fossil Park, Kaziranga National Park and Tiger Reserve, Keibul Lamjao National Park, Sundarbans Tiger Reserve (STR), Similipal Tiger Reserve, Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary, Male Mahadeshwara (MM) Hills Wildlife Sanctuary, Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve, Thattekad Bird Sanctuary, Mhadei Wildlife Sanctuary, Sahyadri Tiger Reserve.
evidenceBullets: Bhagirathi Eco Sensitive Zone (ESZ)
Tsarap Chu Conservation Reserve
Dudhwa Tiger Reserve (DTR)
Salkhan Fossil Park
Kaziranga National Park and Tiger Reserve
Keibul Lamjao National Park
Sundarbans Tiger Reserve (STR)
Similipal Tiger Reserve

sections.title: Kaziranga National Park and Tiger Reserve (Assam)
sections.focus: Ecological features, fauna, river systems, and recent census techniques at Kaziranga.
sections.rationale: Kaziranga is a highly examinable topic for UPSC due to its unique fauna (Rhino, Tiger density), geography, and recent use of Acoustic Monitoring.
sections.sectionExcerpt: First-ever grassland bird census was held here using Acoustic Monitoring Technique. Location: Brahmaputra floodplain, at foothills of Karbi-Anglong. River: Diffalu and Moradifalu. Forest type: Alluvial grasslands, semi-evergreen and moist deciduous forests, wetlands, and sandy chaurs. Key Fauna: One-Horned Rhino (70% of population), Tiger, Eastern swamp deer, Elephant, Hoolock gibbon, Capped langur and Gangetic River Dolphin etc. Recorded 3rd highest tiger density in India after Karnataka's Bandipur and Uttarakhand's Corbett National Park. Recognized as an Important Bird Area by BirdLife International.
evidenceBullets: First-ever grassland bird census was conducted using Acoustic Monitoring Technique.
Located on the Brahmaputra floodplain, at the foothills of Karbi-Anglong.
Diffalu and Moradifalu rivers flow through the park.
Hosts 70% of the One-Horned Rhino population.
Records the 3rd highest tiger density in India after Bandipur and Corbett.
Recognized as an Important Bird Area by BirdLife International.

sections.title: Mhadei Wildlife Sanctuary (Goa)
sections.focus: Geography, flora, and fauna of Mhadei Wildlife Sanctuary.
sections.rationale: Important due to the Supreme Court stay on projects and its strategic location in the Western Ghats.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Context: Recently, Supreme Court stayed projects here. Location: North Goa along Western Ghats, bordering Karnataka and Maharashtra. River: Mahadei (Mondovi) River flows through wildlife sanctuary. Fauna: Tigers, leopards, sloth bears, gaurs, sambar deer, etc. Forest type: Moist deciduous vegetation and some evergreen species, e.g. rare Ashoka tree with peculiar saffron colored flowers.
evidenceBullets: Located in North Goa along the Western Ghats, bordering Karnataka and Maharashtra.
Mahadei (Mondovi) River flows through the sanctuary.
Features moist deciduous and evergreen vegetation, including the rare Ashoka tree with saffron-colored flowers.

sections.title: Tsarap Chu Conservation Reserve (Himachal Pradesh)
sections.focus: Location, boundaries, and unique alpine fauna of India's largest conservation reserve.
sections.rationale: As India's largest conservation reserve, recently notified, with significant high-altitude fauna, it is a prime candidate for environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Himachal Pradesh notified it (India's largest conservation reserve) in Spiti Valley. Location: Confluence of Unam River and Charap Nala. Bounded by UT of Ladakh to north, the Kibber Wildlife Sanctuary extending up to Malang Nala and Lungar Lungpa to east, Kabjima Nala to south and Chandratal Wildlife Sanctuary to west. Fauna: Known for snow leopard, Tibetan wolf, bharal (blue sheep), Himalayan ibex, kiang (wild ass), and Tibetan argali, rare bird species such as Rose Finch, Tibetan Raven, etc.
evidenceBullets: India's largest conservation reserve, located in Spiti Valley, Himachal Pradesh.
Situated at the confluence of the Unam River and Charap Nala.
Bounded by Ladakh to the north, Kibber WLS to the east, and Chandratal WLS to the west.
Fauna includes snow leopard, Tibetan wolf, bharal (blue sheep), Himalayan ibex, kiang (wild ass), and Tibetan argali.

sections.title: Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary (Jharkhand)
sections.focus: Unique cultural practices and ecological significance of India's only wolf sanctuary.
sections.rationale: India's first and only dedicated wolf sanctuary; involves unique tribal conservation practices which makes it highly relevant for UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Witnessed unique practice of tribals from the 'Sarna faith' to avoid the Sal Forest during winter months. Location: India's first and only dedicated wolf sanctuary, located within the Palamau Tiger Reserve in Jharkhand. River: Burha River flow through Mahuadanr valley.
evidenceBullets: India's first and only dedicated wolf sanctuary.
Located within the Palamau Tiger Reserve in Jharkhand.
Tribals from the 'Sarna faith' practice avoiding the Sal Forest during winter months.
Burha River flows through the Mahuadanr valley.

sections.title: Male Mahadeshwara (MM) Hills Wildlife Sanctuary (Karnataka)
sections.focus: Ecological corridor role and forest types of MM Hills Wildlife Sanctuary.
sections.rationale: Crucial ecological corridor connecting the Eastern and Western Ghats, making it significant for landscape-level conservation questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Location: Southeast Karnataka. Forms connecting corridor between Biligiri Ranganatha Swamy Temple (BRT) Wildlife Sanctuary and Cauvery Wildlife Sanctuary. Forest type: Dominated by dry deciduous forests and Patches of Semi-evergreen, Shola forests at higher altitudes and scrub forests in fringe zones. Fauna: Tiger, Elephant, Leopard, Wild dog (dhole), Sloth bear, etc. Important corridor in the Eastern Ghats-Western Ghats landscape.
evidenceBullets: Located in Southeast Karnataka.
Forms a connecting corridor between BRT Wildlife Sanctuary and Cauvery Wildlife Sanctuary.
Serves as an important corridor in the Eastern Ghats-Western Ghats landscape.
Features dry deciduous forests, semi-evergreen patches, and Shola forests at higher altitudes.

sections.title: Thattekad Bird Sanctuary
sections.focus: Ecological features, location, and recent faunal survey of Thattekad Bird Sanctuary.
sections.rationale: A distinct protected area with specific geographical and ecological facts, such as being Kerala's first bird sanctuary and its association with the Periyar and Idamalayar rivers.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Kerala Thattekad Bird Sanctuary - Context: Faunal survey adds nine new species in Thattekad Bird Sanctuary. Location: Ernakulam District, Kerala. Kerala's first bird sanctuary, recognized as one of richest ecological zones in Western Ghats. Forest type: Tropical Evergreen Forests, Tropical Semi-evergreen forests, Moist Deciduous forests, Riparian Forests... River: Periyar and Idamalayar rivers.
evidenceBullets: Located in Ernakulam District, Kerala.
Recognized as Kerala's first bird sanctuary.
Situated along the Periyar and Idamalayar rivers.
Forest types include Tropical Evergreen, Semi-evergreen, Moist Deciduous, and Riparian.

sections.title: Sahyadri Tiger Reserve
sections.focus: Translocation of tigers, location, and ecological profile of Sahyadri Tiger Reserve.
sections.rationale: An important tiger reserve in the Western Ghats with specific reservoirs and a recent translocation context, making it highly examinable for environment and geography.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Maharashtra Sahyadri Tiger Reserve - Context: MoEFCC granted approval for Translocation of 8 Tigers from Tadoba-Andhari Tiger Reserve and Pench Tiger Reserve to Sahyadri Tiger Reserve... Northern most tiger habitat in Western Ghats. Notified in 2010 by amalgamating the Chandoli National Park and Koyna Wildlife Sanctuary. Reservoirs: 'Shivsagar' reservoir of Koyana River and 'Vasant Sagar' reservoir of Warana River.
evidenceBullets: Northernmost tiger habitat in the Western Ghats.
Formed in 2010 by amalgamating Chandoli National Park and Koyna Wildlife Sanctuary.
Contains the 'Shivsagar' reservoir of the Koyana River and the 'Vasant Sagar' reservoir of the Warana River.
Received approval for tiger translocation from Tadoba-Andhari and Pench Tiger Reserves.

sections.title: Keibul Lamjao National Park
sections.focus: Unique floating park ecosystem, Loktak Lake, and Sangai deer habitat.
sections.rationale: Highly examinable due to its unique status as the world's only floating national park, its association with Loktak Lake (Ramsar/Montreux), and as the habitat for the Sangai deer.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Manipur Keibul Lamjao National Park - Context: Study links land use to water pollution in Manipur's Loktak Lake. Location: Bishnupur and Thoubal district of Manipur. Only floating park in the world. Largest area of Phumdi (floating Islands of vegetation) in Loktak lake is in this park. Loktak lake, a Ramsar site and Montreux Record site... Fauna: Brow-antlered deer (Sangai).
evidenceBullets: The only floating national park in the world.
Located in Loktak Lake, which is both a Ramsar site and a Montreux Record site.
Characterized by Phumdis, which are floating islands of vegetation.
Primary habitat for the Brow-antlered deer, also known as Sangai.

sections.title: Similipal Tiger Reserve
sections.focus: Notification as a National Park, geographical features, and tribal populations of Similipal.
sections.rationale: Rich in geographical facts including rivers, waterfalls, and tribal demographics, making it a prime candidate for UPSC geography and environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Odisha Similipal Tiger Reserve - Context: Odisha notified Similipal Tiger Reserve as National Park making it largest in Odisha, leaving behind Bhitarkanika. Location: Mayurbhanj District, Odisha in Chottanagpur region. Rivers: Burhabalanga, Palpala Bandan, Salandi, Kahairi, and Deo... Waterfalls: Joranda and Barehipani. Tribes Found: Kolha, Santhala, Bhumija... Note: Similipal is a Biosphere reserve under the UNESCO Man and the Biosphere (MAB) Programme.
evidenceBullets: Now the largest National Park in Odisha, surpassing Bhitarkanika.
Drained by rivers such as Burhabalanga, Salandi, and Deo.
Features the Joranda and Barehipani waterfalls.
Inhabited by tribes including Kolha, Santhala, Mankadia, and Sahara.
Recognized as a Biosphere Reserve under the UNESCO MAB Programme.

sections.title: Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve
sections.focus: Composition, location, and associated protected areas of Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve.
sections.rationale: Spans two states and includes multiple wildlife sanctuaries and tiger reserves, which is a common format for UPSC matching questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Tamil Nadu Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve - Context: SC Orders Probe into Violations of Forest and Wildlife Laws in Tamil Nadu's Agasthyamalai Landscape. Location: Located in Southern Western Ghats in Tamil Nadu and Kerala. Includes 3 WLSs: Shendurney, Peppara and Neyyar (Kerala), and Kalakad Mundanthurai Tiger Reserve (Tamil Nadu)... Rivers: Thamirabarai River.
evidenceBullets: Located in the Southern Western Ghats across Tamil Nadu and Kerala.
Includes Shendurney, Peppara, and Neyyar Wildlife Sanctuaries in Kerala.
Includes Kalakad Mundanthurai Tiger Reserve in Tamil Nadu.
Associated with the Thamirabarai River.

sections.title: Dudhwa Tiger Reserve
sections.focus: Location, habitat, and recent leopard population growth in Dudhwa Tiger Reserve.
sections.rationale: An important protected area in the Terai region with specific river associations and habitat types.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Uttar Pradesh Dudhwa Tiger Reserve (DTR) - Context: Leopard population in DTR has grown by 198.91% since 2022. Location: Terai region of Uttar Pradesh, along Indo-Nepal Border. Includes Dudhwa National Park and Katerniaghat and Kishanpur. Forest type: Typical Tarai-Bhabar habitat of upper Gangetic plains... Rivers: Mohana, Suheli, Joraha, Nagro etc.
evidenceBullets: Located in the Terai region of Uttar Pradesh along the Indo-Nepal Border.
Comprises Dudhwa National Park, Katerniaghat, and Kishanpur.
Characterized by typical Tarai-Bhabar habitat of the upper Gangetic plains.
Drained by rivers such as Mohana, Suheli, Joraha, and Nagro.

sections.title: Salkhan Fossil Park
sections.focus: Geo-heritage significance, stromatolites, and Great Oxidation Event connection of Salkhan Fossil Park.
sections.rationale: A unique geological site added to the UNESCO Tentative List, linking biology (cyanobacteria) with Earth's history (Great Oxidation Event).
sections.sectionExcerpt: Salkhan Fossil Park - Context: Salkhan Fossil Park added to UNESCO Tentative List For World Heritage Sites, officially known as Sonbhadra Fossils Park, Sonbhadra, Uttar Pradesh... Location: Kaimur Range (part of Vindhya range), adjacent to Kaimur Wildlife Sanctuary. One of oldest and most well-preserved sites in world (fossils dating ~1.4 billion years). Site's fossil assemblage features Stromatolites created by communities of cyanobacteria... record Great Oxidation Event.
evidenceBullets: Added to the UNESCO Tentative List for World Heritage Sites.
Located in the Kaimur Range, part of the Vindhya range in Uttar Pradesh.
Features approximately 1.4 billion-year-old Stromatolite fossils created by cyanobacteria.
The fossilized microbial structures record the Great Oxidation Event.

sections.title: Bhagirathi Eco Sensitive Zone
sections.focus: Ecological significance, regulatory framework, and location of the Bhagirathi ESZ.
sections.rationale: Covers the concept of Eco-Sensitive Zones (ESZ) and specific environmental governance (Zonal Master Plan) in a fragile Himalayan stretch.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Uttarakhand Bhagirathi Eco Sensitive Zone (ESZ) - Context: Recently, Uttarakhand Government granted approval for Netala bypass project in fragile Bhagirathi ESZ despite previous rejections... Created to protect River Ganga's most pristine stretch between Gaumukh and Uttarkashi town. Eco-sensitive Zones are ecologically fragile areas around Protected Areas... Notification: By the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC) in 2012... under Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
evidenceBullets: Created to protect the pristine stretch of the River Ganga between Gaumukh and Uttarkashi.
Notified by MoEF&CC under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
Eco-sensitive Zones act as 'Shock Absorbers' around Protected Areas and wildlife corridors.
Requires a Zonal Master Plan (ZMP) based on a watershed approach.

sections.title: Sundarbans Tiger Reserve
sections.focus: Expansion, geographical borders, and international designations of Sundarbans Tiger Reserve.
sections.rationale: India's second-largest tiger reserve with a unique mangrove habitat and multiple international tags (UNESCO, CA|TS, MAB).
sections.sectionExcerpt: West Bengal. Sundarbans Tiger Reserve (STR). Context: STR is now India's second largest after expansion of its area was approved by National Board of Wildlife (NBWL). - Now STR is just behind Andhra Pradesh's Nagarjunasagar-Srisailam Tiger Reserve in size... Only mangrove forest (with Bangladesh) housing a significant tiger population. Borders: Bangladesh (east), Bay of Bengal (south), River Matla (west), Rivers Bidya & Gomdi (north-west). Associated Designations: UNESCO World Heritage Site, CA|TS (Conservation Assured | Tiger Standards), MAB.
evidenceBullets: Now India's second-largest tiger reserve, behind Nagarjunasagar-Srisailam.
The only mangrove forest in the world housing a significant tiger population.
Bordered by the River Matla to the west and Rivers Bidya & Gomdi to the north-west.
Holds designations as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, CA|TS, and MAB Programme site.

sections.subtopic: Snow Leopard, Rhino, and Nilgiri Tahr
sections.title: Snow Leopard
sections.focus: Habitat, characteristics, conservation status, and international/national conservation efforts for the Snow Leopard.
sections.rationale: The Snow Leopard is a highly examinable species due to its specific high-altitude habitat, unique characteristics, and multiple international conservation agreements (Bishkek, Samarkand).
sections.sectionExcerpt: Study estimated 477 snow leopards in Ladakh. Habitat: Mountainous regions of South and Central Asia. India: Jammu & Kashmir, Ladakh, Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Sikkim and Arunachal Pradesh. Globally, Hemis National Park, Kargil, and Leh have highest extensive densities. Characteristics: Solitary animals; unlike other big cats they cannot roar. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix I; CMS: Appendix I; IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Conservation Efforts: Project Snow Leopard India; International Big Cat Alliance; Global Snow Leopard and Ecosystem Protection Programme; Bishkek Declaration (2017); Samarkand Resolution (2024); CaTRAT to identify Himalayan snow leopards.
evidenceBullets: IUCN Status is Vulnerable (VU); listed under WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, and CMS Appendix I.
Found in mountainous regions of J&K, Ladakh, HP, Uttarakhand, Sikkim, and Arunachal Pradesh.
Hemis National Park, Kargil, and Leh have the highest extensive densities globally.
Unlike other big cats, snow leopards cannot roar.
Conservation initiatives include the Bishkek Declaration (2017), Samarkand Resolution (2024), and the CaTRAT (Camera Trap data Repository and Analysis Tool).

sections.subtopic: Snow Leopard, Rhino, and Nilgiri Tahr
sections.title: Greater One-Horned Rhino (Indian Rhino)
sections.focus: DNA profiling, habitat distribution, species comparison, and conservation initiatives of the Indian Rhino.
sections.rationale: The Indian Rhino is a critical megafauna species. UPSC frequently tests species comparisons (the 5 rhino species and their IUCN statuses) and specific habitats (Kaziranga).
sections.sectionExcerpt: Assam Forest Department started DNA profiling of Rhino horns and add it to Rhino DNA Index System (RhoDIS) India DNA data library. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix I; IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Habitat: Assam has the world's largest population. More than 90% of them are in Kaziranga National Park. Characteristics: One among 5 species of rhino: 2 African: White Rhino (Near Threatened), Black Rhino (Critically Endangered). 3 Asian: Sumatran (Critically Endangered), Javan (Critically Endangered), and Indian Rhino. Indian Rhino is the largest of all Asian rhino species. Usually solitary. Conservation initiatives: National Rhino Conservation Strategy 2019; New Delhi Declaration on Asian Rhinos 2019; Indian Rhino Vision 2020.
evidenceBullets: IUCN Status of Indian Rhino is Vulnerable (VU); listed under WPA Schedule I and CITES Appendix I.
Assam holds the world's largest population, with over 90% located in Kaziranga National Park.
Indian Rhino is the largest of all Asian rhino species.
Other rhino species statuses: White (Near Threatened), Black (Critically Endangered), Sumatran (Critically Endangered), Javan (Critically Endangered).
RhoDIS India is the DNA data library used for profiling rhino horns.

sections.subtopic: Snow Leopard, Rhino, and Nilgiri Tahr
sections.title: Nilgiri Tahr
sections.focus: Habitat, endemism, cultural significance, and conservation status of the Nilgiri Tahr.
sections.rationale: The Nilgiri Tahr is important due to its endemism to the Western Ghats, its status as the only mountain ungulate in southern India, and its unique mention in ancient Sangam literature.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Traditional Nilgiri tahr habitats in Coimbatore Forest Division show signs of population revival. Habitat: Open montane grassland habitats. Endemic to Western Ghats. Eravikulam National Park, Kerala has the largest contiguous population. Characteristics: Only mountain ungulate in southern India. Locally known as Varaiaadu. Silappathikaram and Sivakasindamani mention its descriptions. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; IUCN Status: Endangered (EN). Conservation Efforts: Project Nilgiri Tahr of Tamil Nadu. State animal of Tamil Nadu. Nilgiri Tahr Day (October 07).
evidenceBullets: Endemic to the open montane grassland habitats of the Western Ghats.
Eravikulam National Park in Kerala hosts the largest contiguous population.
It is the only mountain ungulate in southern India.
Mentioned in ancient Tamil literature such as Silappathikaram and Sivakasindamani.
IUCN Status is Endangered (EN); it is the State animal of Tamil Nadu.

sections.title: Dhole (Asiatic Wild Dog)
sections.focus: Habitat, characteristics, and conservation status of the Dhole.
sections.rationale: Dhole is a distinct species with specific IUCN (Endangered) and WPA (Schedule I) statuses, making it a prime candidate for UPSC environment questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Dhole has made a comeback in Assam's Kaziranga-Karbi Anglong Landscape. It inhabits Eastern & Western Ghats, Assam, Meghalaya, West Bengal, Ladakh, etc. It is a carnivore resembling a small wolf, hunting mainly in the early morning or late evening. Conservation Status: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix II, IUCN Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Comeback in Assam's Kaziranga-Karbi Anglong Landscape (KKAL).
Habitat includes Eastern & Western Ghats, Assam, Meghalaya, West Bengal, Ladakh, and countries like Cambodia, Nepal, Bhutan, Thailand.
Carnivorous, hunts in early morning or late evening.
Conservation: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix II, IUCN Endangered.

sections.title: Alpine Musk Deer
sections.focus: Habitat, unique physical traits, threats, and conservation status of the Alpine Musk Deer.
sections.rationale: The mix-up with the Himalayan counterpart and its unique physical characteristics (fangs, no antlers) make it highly testable.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Central Zoo Authority reported a mix-up in identifying Alpine musk deer for conservation breeding. Native to India, Nepal, Bhutan, and China, it inhabits alpine meadows and fir forests. It is solitary, shy, and herbivorous, uniquely possessing fangs but lacking antlers and a gall bladder. Threatened by poaching for muskpods. Conservation Status: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Native to India, Nepal, Bhutan, and China; inhabits alpine meadows and fir forests.
Solitary, shy, herbivorous.
Unique physical traits: possesses fangs, lacks antlers and gall bladder.
Threatened by poaching for muskpods used in perfumes and traditional medicines.
Conservation: WPA Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.

sections.title: Indian Grey Wolf
sections.focus: Habitat, characteristics, and conservation status of the Indian Grey Wolf.
sections.rationale: Its specific niche in arid/semi-arid plains and recent threats from dog attacks in Maharashtra are relevant for ecology questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Indian grey wolf faces risks from dog attacks in Kadbanwadi grassland, Maharashtra. It inhabits Peninsular India, including Rajasthan and Gujarat. A subspecies of the grey wolf, it travels in smaller packs, is less vocal, and occupies a top predator niche in arid and semi-arid open plains and grasslands. Conservation Status: WPA Schedule I.
evidenceBullets: Faces risk from dog attacks in Kadbanwadi grassland, Maharashtra.
Habitat: Peninsular India including Rajasthan, Gujarat, etc.
Subspecies of grey wolf; travels in smaller packs and is less vocal.
Top predator in arid and semi-arid open plains and grasslands of India.
Conservation: WPA Schedule I.

sections.title: Bonobo
sections.focus: Habitat, behavioral characteristics, and conservation status of the Bonobo.
sections.rationale: The discovery of compositionality in their vocal communication and their genetic proximity to humans make them a unique topic for science and environment intersections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A study found that vocal communication between Bonobos shows compositionality, similar to human language. Found only in the forests of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). Like Chimpanzees, they share 98.7% of DNA with humans. They are peaceful, female-led, and smaller/darker than chimpanzees. Conservation Status: CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.
evidenceBullets: Vocal communication shows compositionality, similar to human language.
Endemic to the forests of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC).
Share 98.7% of DNA with humans, making them our closest living relatives alongside chimpanzees.
Peaceful species with female-led groups.
Conservation: CITES Appendix I, IUCN Endangered.

sections.title: Malabar Grey Hornbill
sections.focus: Habitat, characteristics, and conservation status of the Malabar Grey Hornbill.
sections.rationale: This is a distinct endemic species with specific physical traits and conservation statuses that are highly testable in UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Malabar Grey Hornbill Context: Researchers from Kerala were awarded Future Conservationist Award... Habitat: Endemic to Western Ghats of India. Characteristics: Lacks the casque typical of Hornbill; Loud, laugh-like call. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I. IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable).
evidenceBullets: Endemic to the Western Ghats of India.
Lacks the casque typical of other Hornbill species.
Protected under WPA, 1972: Schedule I.
IUCN Status is Vulnerable (VU).

sections.title: Greater Flamingo
sections.focus: Breeding grounds, habitat preferences, and conservation initiatives for the Greater Flamingo.
sections.rationale: The notification of a new sanctuary in Tamil Nadu and the unique breeding ground in Gujarat's Kachchh make this a high-yield geography and environment topic.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Greater Flamingo Context: Tamil Nadu notified Greater Flamingo Sanctuary at Dhanushkodi to preserve a critical stopover point along Central Asian Flyway. Habitat: Breeds in shallow wetlands that are either saline or alkaline... Kachchh Desert Wildlife Sanctuary in Great Rann of Kachchh (GRK) in Gujarat, is a unique Protected Area, supporting South Asia’s only breeding ground of Greater Flamingos... Characteristics: Great dispersal capacity outside breeding season, highly philopatric... Conservation Status: CITES: Appendix II. IUCN Status: LC (Least Concern).
evidenceBullets: Tamil Nadu notified the Greater Flamingo Sanctuary at Dhanushkodi.
Breeds in shallow wetlands that are either saline or alkaline.
Kachchh Desert Wildlife Sanctuary in Gujarat supports South Asia's only breeding ground of Greater Flamingos.
Highly philopatric species (return to or remain near a particular site).
IUCN Status is Least Concern (LC).

sections.title: Dugong
sections.focus: India's Dugong Conservation Reserve, habitat distribution, and unique biological characteristics.
sections.rationale: The establishment of India's first Dugong Conservation Reserve and its unique status as a marine herbivorous mammal are prime targets for UPSC questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Dugong Context: IUCN adopted a motion recognizing India's first Dugong Conservation Reserve in Palk Bay... established in 2022, by Tamil Nadu government... Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I. CMS: Appendix II. IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Covered under Species Recovery Programme. Habitat: 40 countries from East Africa to the Pacific... India: Palk Bay (highest), Gulf of Mannar, Gulf of Kutch, etc. Characteristics: Only marine herbivorous mammals that depend on seagrass. ("sea cows").
evidenceBullets: India's first Dugong Conservation Reserve was established in Palk Bay by the Tamil Nadu government.
IUCN Status is Vulnerable (VU) and protected under WPA, 1972: Schedule I.
In India, they are found in Palk Bay, Gulf of Mannar, and Gulf of Kutch.
They are the only marine herbivorous mammals that depend on seagrass (known as 'sea cows').

sections.title: Olive Ridley Turtle
sections.focus: Mass nesting behavior (arribadas), conservation status, and Operation Olivia.
sections.rationale: Olive Ridley Turtles are a recurring UPSC theme due to their unique mass nesting phenomenon and specific conservation operations in Odisha.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Olive Ridley Turtle (ORT) Context: Operation Olivia shielded over 8-lakh ORT on Odisha's Gahirmatha coast. Conservation Status: WPA, 1972: Schedule I. CITES: Appendix I. IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Habitat: Warm, tropical waters of the Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans. Characteristics: Undergo unique mass nesting called arribadas. Smallest and most abundant of all sea turtles.
evidenceBullets: Operation Olivia protects Olive Ridley Turtles on Odisha's Gahirmatha coast.
IUCN Status is Vulnerable (VU) and protected under WPA, 1972: Schedule I.
They undergo a unique mass nesting phenomenon known as 'arribadas'.
They are the smallest and most abundant of all sea turtles.

sections.subtopic: Softshell Turtles and New Fish Species
sections.title: Operation Olivia
sections.focus: Indian Coast Guard initiative for turtle conservation in Odisha.
sections.rationale: Standalone conservation operation conducted by the Indian Coast Guard, highly relevant for prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Initiated in 1980s by Indian Coast Guard, conducted every year from November to May. Focuses on Gahirmatha Beach, Rushikulya River Mouth, and Devi River Mouth.
evidenceBullets: Initiated in 1980s by Indian Coast Guard
Conducted annually from November to May
Focuses on Gahirmatha Beach, Rushikulya River Mouth, and Devi River Mouth

sections.subtopic: Softshell Turtles and New Fish Species
sections.title: Asian Giant Softshell Turtle
sections.focus: Habitat, conservation status, and characteristics of the Asian Giant Softshell Turtle.
sections.rationale: Critically endangered species in news due to an award-winning documentary; important for environment and biodiversity questions.
